topics.title: Metallurgy and Strengthening Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metallurgy and Strengthening Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Metallurgy — The Science of Metals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Metallurgy* is the branch of materials science and engineering that studies the **physical and chemical behavior of metallic elements, their intermetallic compounds, and their mixtures (alloys)**. In the context of this module, it specifically refers to the **crystallography of metals**, the **defect structures** within metallic crystals, and the **response of these crystals to applied mechanical/thermal loads**.

**Key Sub-disciplines Relevant to PEMET635:**
- **Physical Metallurgy** – Studies the relationship between structure and properties.
- **Mechanical Metallurgy** – Studies deformation and fracture behavior.
- **Phase Transformations** – Studies how microstructure evolves during processing.

## 1.2 Strengthening Mechanisms — The Engineer's Toolkit

A pure metal is almost always **too soft** for engineering applications. The art of strengthening lies in **deliberately introducing microstructural obstacles** that resist the motion of **dislocations** (the carriers of plastic deformation).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Central Dogma of Strengthening:**
> *Strengthening a metal is essentially the act of making dislocation motion harder.* Every mechanism discussed in this module is a different *strategy* of blocking, pinning, or multiplying dislocations.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Crowd in a Corridor:**

Imagine a long, narrow corridor filled with people trying to walk from one end to the other.
- **Pure metal = empty corridor:** People (dislocations) move freely → material deforms easily → **soft**.
- **Add pillars (precipitates) at intervals:** People must detour → **precipitation hardening**.
- **Add pot-holes (solute atoms of different size):** Every step is a stumble → **solid solution strengthening**.
- **Build walls every 10 meters (grain boundaries):** Each wall stops the crowd → **grain boundary strengthening**.
- **Create a stampede (more dislocations):** Dislocations collide and tangle with each other → **strain/work hardening**.

All these strategies share the same goal: **increase the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)** required for slip.

## 1.3 Why Does This Matter in Real Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:**
> - The **yield strength of structural steel** in bridges (~250 MPa) vs. **maraging steel** in aerospace landing gear (~1900 MPa) — a ~7.6× difference achieved purely by metallurgy, not chemistry.
> - **Aluminum alloys** for aircraft skins (2xxx, 7xxx series) are strengthened almost entirely by **precipitation hardening**.
> - **Grain size control** in automotive sheet metals (IF steels, AHSS) determines both strength and formability.

**Standard Performance Metric:**
> The **Yield Strength** (σ_y in **MPa**) is the single most important metric for a structural engineer. Every strengthening mechanism discussed here ultimately aims to raise σ_y.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystal Structures of Metals — The Building Blocks

Metallic bonding (delocalized electron "sea") favors **dense, symmetric packing**. Three structures dominate ~90% of engineering metals:

| Structure | Examples | Atoms/Unit Cell | Coordination Number | APF | Slip Systems |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|---------------------|-----|--------------|
| **FCC** (Face-Centered Cubic) | Cu, Al, Ni, γ-Fe, Au, Ag | 4 | 12 | 0.74 | 12 |
| **BCC** (Body-Centered Cubic) | α-Fe, Cr, Mo, W, V | 2 | 8 | 0.68 | 12 (or 48) |
| **HCP** (Hexagonal Close-Packed) | Ti, Mg, Zn, Be, Co | 6 | 12 | 0.74 | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** = (Volume of atoms in unit cell) / (Volume of unit cell). Higher APF generally implies more ductility.

## 2.2 Defects in Crystals — The Real Microstructure

Perfect crystals don't exist in engineering. Real metals contain **defects**, which are *responsible* for both plasticity (via dislocations) and strengthening (by blocking them).

### Classification of Defects:

**A. Point Defects (0-D)**
- **Vacancy** — Missing atom from lattice site.
- **Interstitial** — Extra atom squeezed between lattice sites.
- **Substitutional** — Foreign atom replacing a host atom.

**B. Line Defects (1-D) — Dislocations**
- **Edge dislocation** — Extra half-plane of atoms; Burger vector ⊥ to line.
- **Screw dislocation** — Helical ramp of atoms; Burger vector ∥ to line.
- **Mixed dislocation** — Curved line combining both characters.

**C. Surface/Planar Defects (2-D)**
- **Grain boundaries** — Interface between two crystals of different orientation.
- **Twin boundaries** — Mirror-symmetric interface.
- **Stacking faults** — Error in stacking sequence (e.g., ABCBCABC in FCC).

**D. Volume Defects (3-D)**
- **Voids, inclusions, precipitates** — Second-phase particles.

## 2.3 Dislocation Motion — Schmid's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A dislocation moves (slip) only when the **resolved shear stress (τ_R)** on the slip plane, in the slip direction, exceeds the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS, τ_CRSS)** of the crystal.

This is quantified by **Schmid's Law:**

$$\tau_R = \sigma \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot \cos(\lambda)$$

Where:
- σ = applied normal tensile stress (**MPa**)
- φ (phi) = angle between tensile axis and the normal to the slip plane
- λ (lambda) = angle between tensile axis and the slip direction

**Schmid Factor:** $m = \cos(\phi) \cdot \cos(\lambda)$ → maximum value is **0.5** (at 45°).

## 2.4 The Four Primary Strengthening Mechanisms

### Mechanism 1: Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall-Petch)

Smaller grains → more grain boundaries → more obstacles → higher yield strength.

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

- σ_y = yield strength (**MPa**)
- σ_0 = friction stress resisting dislocation motion
- k_y = Hall-Petch coefficient (**MPa · μm^½**)
- d = average grain diameter (**μm**)

> [!NOTE]
> The **√d** in the denominator means that to **halve** the grain size, you must **increase** σ_y by a finite amount — diminishing returns at very fine grains (typically d < 1 μm, where the Hall-Petch relation eventually breaks down).

### Mechanism 2: Solid Solution Strengthening

Solute atoms distort the lattice (size misfit, modulus misfit), creating a stress field that impedes dislocations.

$$\tau = G \cdot \varepsilon_s^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{c}$$

- G = shear modulus (**MPa**)
- ε_s = atomic size misfit parameter (dimensionless)
- c = concentration of solute (atomic fraction)
- τ = increase in CRSS (**MPa**)

### Mechanism 3: Strain (Work) Hardening

Plastic deformation multiplies dislocations; they entangle and become harder to move.

$$\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \quad \text{(Hollomon Equation)}$$

- σ = true stress (**MPa**)
- ε = true plastic strain
- K = strength coefficient (**MPa**)
- n = strain hardening exponent (0.1 to 0.5 for most metals)

> [!NOTE]
> **Taylor's dislocation-density relation:** $\tau \propto \sqrt{\rho}$, where ρ = dislocation density. This is the physical origin of the √c dependence seen in solid solution strengthening.

### Mechanism 4: Precipitation (Age) Hardening

Second-phase particles (e.g., Al_3Cu in Al-Cu alloys) block dislocations via:
- **Dislocation cutting** (small, coherent particles): $\tau \propto \sqrt{r \cdot f}$
- **Orowan looping** (large, incoherent particles): $\tau \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ where $L$ = inter-particle spacing.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Mechanism / Law | Equation | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schmid's Law | τ_R = σ·cos φ·cos λ | σ = applied stress | **MPa** |
| 2 | Hall-Petch | σ_y = σ_0 + k_y/√d | d = grain size | **μm** |
| 3 | Solid Solution | τ = G·ε_s^(3/2)·√c | c = atomic fraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | Hollomon (Strain Hardening) | σ = K·ε^n | n = hardening exponent | dimensionless |
| 5 | Taylor (Dislocation Density) | τ = τ_0 + α·G·b·√ρ | ρ = dislocation density | **m/m³** |
| 6 | Orowan Looping | τ = Gb/L | L = inter-particle spacing | **m** |
| 7 | Atomic Packing Factor (FCC) | APF = π/(3√2) | — | 0.74 |
| 8 | Atomic Packing Factor (BCC) | APF = π√3/8 | — | 0.68 |
| 9 | Burger Vector (FCC, BCC) | b = (a/2)·<110> | a = lattice parameter | **nm** |
| 10 | Critical Shear Stress | τ_CRSS (material property) | Fe ≈ 27.2 MPa | **MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Schmid's Law** is used in single-crystal turbine blade design (Ni-superalloys).
> - **Hall-Petch** governs thermomechanical processing (TMP) of HSLA steels.
> - **Hollomon** is used in cold-rolling mill design to predict roll forces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Hall-Petch Equation from Dislocation Pile-Up Theory

> The Hall-Petch equation is empirically observed but has a rigorous mechanistic basis from **dislocation pile-ups**. We derive it here step by step, as this is a **favourite KTU long-answer derivation**.

### Step 1 — The Pile-Up Concept

Consider a slip plane terminating at a grain boundary (which acts as a barrier). A **leading dislocation** is pushed against the boundary by an applied shear stress τ. Behind it, **n** dislocations queue up — this is the **pile-up**.

### Step 2 — Number of Dislocations in the Pile-Up

The number of dislocations in the pile-up under shear stress τ is:

$$n = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

- L = length of pile-up (≈ grain diameter d)
- G = shear modulus (**MPa**)
- b = Burgers vector magnitude (**m**)

**Reasoning:** Each dislocation in the pile-up contributes stress, and the equilibrium of forces balances applied τ against back-stress from n dislocations.

### Step 3 — Stress Concentration at the Pile-Up Tip

The shear stress concentrated at the **tip** of the pile-up (where it meets the grain boundary) is amplified by the number of dislocations:

$$\tau_{tip} = n \cdot \tau$$

This is because each dislocation in the pile-up adds linearly to the stress at the leading edge (similar to the stress concentration at a sharp crack tip).

### Step 4 — Activation of Slip in the Next Grain

Slip propagates into the next grain when the concentrated tip stress reaches a critical value τ_c (a material constant representing the stress needed to nucleate dislocations in the adjacent grain):

$$\tau_{tip} = \tau_c$$

Substituting from Steps 2 and 3:

$$\tau_c = \tau \cdot n = \tau \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

Solving for τ:

$$\tau^2 = \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi \cdot L}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Tensile Yield Strength

Using Schmid's law, τ ≈ σ_y / 2 (assuming maximum Schmid factor m = 0.5), and L ≈ d (grain diameter):

$$\left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi \cdot d}$$

Expanding and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{4} = \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi \cdot d}$$

$$\sigma_y^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot \tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{d}$$

Taking the square root (and noting τ_c is a constant for the material):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

This is the famous **Hall-Petch equation**, where:

$$\sigma_0 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \tau_c}{\sqrt{\pi}} \quad \text{and} \quad k_y = \sqrt{4 \cdot \tau_c \cdot G \cdot b / \pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **key physical insight**: yield strength increases as the **inverse square root** of grain size. This is why **thermomechanical controlled processing (TMCP)** in steelmaking focuses so intensely on refining austenite grains.

## 3.2 Derivation: Orowan Looping Stress (Precipitation Hardening)

When a dislocation encounters a hard, non-shearable particle of radius r, it **bows around** the particle, leaving an **Orowan loop**.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Bowing Dislocation

The dislocation bows into a circular arc of radius R between two particles separated by distance L (center-to-center). The line tension of the dislocation is:

$$T \approx \dfrac{1}{2} G b^2$$

(per unit length, where b = Burgers vector magnitude).

### Step 2 — Force Balance on the Bowed Segment

At the critical bowing condition, the segment forms a semicircle (radius R = L/2). Equating applied shear stress τ (acting over length L) to the line tension at the two ends:

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot L = 2T \cdot \sin(90°) = 2T$$

Substituting T = (1/2) G b²:

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot L = G b^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for τ

$$\tau = \dfrac{G b}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Orowan stress**. Note the inverse dependence on L: **closely spaced, finely dispersed particles give the highest strengthening**.

### Step 4 — Relating L to Particle Radius and Volume Fraction

For spherical particles of radius r and volume fraction f, geometric analysis gives:

$$L \approx \dfrac{2r}{\sqrt{f}} \quad \text{(for square array)}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{f}}{2r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Strengthening **increases** with volume fraction f (more particles) but **decreases** with particle radius r (coarser particles are easier to bypass). This is why **over-aged** alloys lose strength — particles coarsen, r increases, τ drops.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hall-Petch

**Problem:** A mild steel has σ_0 = 70 MPa and k_y = 0.74 MPa·m^(1/2). If the grain size is reduced from 50 μm to 5 μm, what is the increase in yield strength?

**Solution:**

For d_1 = 50 μm:

$$\sigma_{y1} = 70 + \dfrac{0.74}{\sqrt{50}} = 70 + \dfrac{0.74}{7.071} = 70 + 0.1047 = 70.10 \text{ MPa}$$

Wait — that gives an absurdly small contribution. Let me recheck units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention for k_y:**
> If d is in **mm** → k_y is in **MPa · mm^(1/2)** (typical values 20–25).
> If d is in **μm** → k_y is in **MPa · μm^(1/2)** (typical values 0.5–0.8).

**Corrected Solution** (d in μm, k_y = 0.74 MPa·μm^(1/2)):

For d_1 = 50 μm:

$$\sigma_{y1} = 70 + \dfrac{0.74}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6}}} \text{ (if d in m)}$$

Let us use **d in mm** for cleaner numbers (k_y = 23 MPa·mm^(1/2) is a more typical value for low-carbon steel):

$$\sigma_{y1} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{\sqrt{0.050}} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{0.2236} = 70 + 102.9 = 172.9 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{\sqrt{0.005}} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{0.0707} = 70 + 325.3 = 395.3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\Delta\sigma_y = 395.3 - 172.9 = 222.4 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A 10× reduction in grain size (from 50 μm to 5 μm) **more than doubles** the yield strength of mild steel. This is the empirical foundation of modern **HSLA (High-Strength Low-Alloy)** steels.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Strengthening Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pure Metal Soft and Weak] --> B{Choose Strengthening Strategy}
    B --> C1[Reduce Grain Size via TMCP]
    B --> C2[Alloy with Solute Atoms]
    B --> C3[Plastic Deform Cold Work]
    B --> C4[Heat Treat to form Precipitates]

    C1 --> D1[More Grain Boundaries]
    C2 --> D2[Lattice Strain Fields]
    C3 --> D3[Dislocation Entanglement]
    C4 --> D4[Second Phase Particles]

    D1 --> E[Dislocation Motion Impeded]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F[Increased CRSS]
    F --> G[Increased Yield Strength sigma_y]
    G --> H[Stronger Engineering Component]

    subgraph Mechanisms
    C1
    C2
    C3
    C4
    end

    subgraph Physical Obstacles
    D1
    D2
    D3
    D4
    end
```

## 4.2 Dislocation Pile-Up Schematic (Hall-Petch Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Shear Stress tau] --> M[Moving Dislocations on Slip Plane]
    M --> GB[Grain Boundary Barrier]
    GB --> PU[Pile-up of n Dislocations]
    PU --> TIP[Stress Concentration at Tip]
    TIP --> NG[Nucleates Slip in Adjacent Grain]
    NG --> Y[Yielding Occurs]

    PU -.amplification.-> TIP
    TIP -.reaches tau_c.-> NG
```

## 4.3 Orowan Looping Mechanism (Precipitation Hardening)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dislocation Approaches Particle] --> B[Particle is Hard Non Shearable]
    B --> C[Dislocation Bows Between Particles]
    C --> D[Forms Circular Arc Radius L over 2]
    D --> E{Stress exceeds critical bowing stress?}
    E -->|No| F[Dislocation Held in Place]
    E -->|Yes| G[Dislocation Wraps Around Particle]
    G --> H[Orowan Loop Remains Around Particle]
    H --> I[Dislocation Continues Forward]
    I --> J[Net Result: Increased CRSS, Strengthened Alloy]
```

## 4.4 Stress-Strain Curve with Strengthening Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Elastic Region Hooke's Law] --> B[Yield Point sigma_y]
    B --> C[Uniform Plastic Deformation]
    C --> D[Necking Begins UTS]
    D --> E[Fracture]

    A -.region1.-> R1[Stage I Elastic Reversible]
    B -.region2.-> R2[Stage II Onset of Dislocation Motion]
    C -.region3.-> R3[Stage III Strain Hardening Active]
    D -.region4.-> R4[Stage IV Damage Accumulation]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Slip System Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metallic Crystal] --> B{FCC}
    A --> C{BCC}
    A --> D{HCP}

    B --> E[12 Slip Systems less CRSS very ductile]
    C --> F[12 to 48 Slip Systems moderate ductility]
    D --> G[3 Slip Systems limited ductility brittle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** HCP metals like Mg and Ti (at room temperature) have only **3 slip systems** — making them harder to deform plastically and explaining why **hot working** is mandatory for these alloys.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between edge and screw dislocations with neat sketches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Edge Dislocation | Screw Dislocation |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Extra half-plane of atoms inserted into the crystal | Shear distortion producing a helical ramp |
| **Burger vector vs. line** | Perpendicular (⊥) | Parallel (∥) |
| **Cause** | Local plastic shear on a single plane | Local shear parallel to dislocation line |
| **Motion** | Glide/climb (climb needs diffusion) | Glide only |
| **Symbol** | ⊥ or ┬ | ⊙ (out of page) or ⊗ (into page) |

(1 mark for clear definitions, 1 mark for the burger vector distinction, 1 mark for the comparison table/sketch indication)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Atomic Packing Factor. Calculate the APF for an FCC crystal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Atomic Packing Factor (APF) is the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms within a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
- For FCC: 4 atoms per unit cell, each atom touches the next along the face diagonal.
- Face diagonal length = 4r (where r = atomic radius)
- Face diagonal = a√2
- Therefore: a√2 = 4r → a = 2r√2
- Volume of atoms = 4 × (4/3)πr³ = (16/3)πr³
- Volume of cell = a³ = (2r√2)³ = 16√2 · r³
- APF = (16π/3) / (16√2) = π/(3√2) ≈ **0.74 (or 74%)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of dislocation pile-up theory, derive the **Hall-Petch equation**:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-carbon steel has σ_0 = 70 MPa and k_y = 23 MPa·mm^(1/2). Calculate the yield strength for grain sizes of **(i)** d = 0.1 mm and **(ii)** d = 0.01 mm. Comment on the engineering significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Pile-up description (2 marks):** When a slip band of n dislocations is forced against a grain boundary by applied shear stress τ, the leading dislocation is held while subsequent dislocations pile up behind it. The pile-up acts as a stress concentrator at the boundary.

2. **Number of dislocations (2 marks):** From equilibrium of forces:

$$n = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

3. **Stress concentration (1 mark):** The concentrated stress at the pile-up tip is n·τ. This must reach a critical value τ_c to nucleate slip in the next grain.

4. **Final equation (2 marks):** Combining n, tip stress, and τ_c = n·τ, then solving for τ and converting via Schmid's law (τ ≈ σ_y/2):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- **σ_0** = intrinsic lattice friction stress (resists dislocation motion within a grain).
- **k_y** = Hall-Petch coefficient — measures the effectiveness of grain boundaries as barriers.
- **d** = average grain diameter.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Case (i): d = 0.1 mm**

$$\sqrt{d} = \sqrt{0.1} = 0.3162 \text{ mm}^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{0.3162} = 70 + 72.74 = 142.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Case (ii): d = 0.01 mm**

$$\sqrt{d} = \sqrt{0.01} = 0.1 \text{ mm}^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 70 + \dfrac{23}{0.1} = 70 + 230 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
A 10× reduction in grain size (from 0.1 mm to 0.01 mm) raises yield strength from ~143 MPa to ~300 MPa — a **2.1× increase**. This justifies **thermomechanical controlled processing (TMCP)** and **grain refinement** as primary strengthening strategies in modern HSLA steels used in pipelines, automotive, and structural applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Forgetting that k_y units depend on d units (MPa·mm^(1/2) if d in mm; MPa·μm^(1/2) if d in μm). Mixing them yields wildly wrong numerical answers. **Always check units first.**
> - **Second common mistake:** In the derivation, students write "stress = nτ" without justifying that it's a **linear superposition** of pile-up stresses. Mention the analogy with crack-tip stress concentration to score full marks.
> - **Third mistake:** In the final step, students forget to convert τ to σ_y via the Schmid factor.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four major strengthening mechanisms in metals with suitable stress-strain or microstructure diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 7075-T6 aluminum alloy (used in aircraft) is strengthened by precipitation of MgZn_2 particles. Using the Orowan looping model, estimate the increase in CRSS given: G = 27 GPa, b = 0.286 nm, average inter-particle spacing L = 50 nm. Comment on whether cutting or looping is dominant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Four Mechanisms (7 marks = 1.75 marks each):**

1. **Grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch):** σ_y = σ_0 + k_y/√d. Smaller grains → more boundaries → more barriers to dislocation motion. Used in TMCP steels.

2. **Solid solution strengthening:** Solute atoms of different size/modulus distort the lattice, creating strain fields. σ ∝ √c. Example: Zn in Cu (brass), C in Fe (steel).

3. **Strain (work) hardening:** σ = K·ε^n. Cold working multiplies dislocations; their mutual interactions raise flow stress. Example: cold-drawn steel wire.

4. **Precipitation (age) hardening:** Hard second-phase particles block dislocations. Below critical size r_c → cutting; above r_c → Orowan looping. Example: Al-Cu (θ' precipitates), Al-Zn-Mg (7075 alloy).

**Part (b) — Orowan Looping Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- G = 27 GPa = 27 × 10³ MPa = 27 × 10⁹ Pa
- b = 0.286 nm = 0.286 × 10⁻⁹ m
- L = 50 nm = 50 × 10⁻⁹ m

**Applying the Orowan equation:**

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L}$$

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{(27 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.286 \times 10^{-9})}{50 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{27 \times 0.286}{50} \times \dfrac{10^9 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-9}}$$

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{7.722}{50} \times 10^{9} = 0.15444 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{Orowan} \approx 154 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Cutting vs. Looping — Discussion (2 marks):**
Since L = 50 nm is significantly larger than typical coherent-particle diameters (~5–10 nm) for peak-aged 7075-T6, the dislocation cannot easily cut through particles. Therefore, **Orowan looping is the dominant mechanism** at this inter-particle spacing. This is consistent with the fact that 7075-T6 (over-aged/peak-aged condition) derives most of its strength from looping, with MgZn_2 precipitates acting as impenetrable barriers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Unit errors dominate this problem:** GPa vs Pa, nm vs m. Always convert to SI base units first (Pa and m). A student writing τ = 27 × 0.286 / 50 will score zero because the answer comes out to ~0.15 (dimensionally meaningless).
> - **Orowan vs. cutting confusion:** Cutting is favored when particles are small and coherent; looping when they are large and incoherent. The boundary is roughly at r ≈ 10–15 nm for typical Al alloys.
> - **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in line tension:** Some textbooks use T = Gb² (full), others T = ½ Gb² (line energy per unit length with the self-energy convention). The KTU convention is the **simpler form** τ = Gb/L (which assumes T = ½ Gb² as used in this derivation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Metallurgy & Strengthening Mechanisms**

- ✅ **FCC = 4 atoms/cell, APF = 0.74, 12 slip systems** (ductile — Al, Cu, Ni, γ-Fe).
- ✅ **BCC = 2 atoms/cell, APF = 0.68, 12 (48) slip systems** (less ductile — α-Fe, Cr, W).
- ✅ **HCP = 6 atoms/cell, APF = 0.74, 3 slip systems** (brittle at RT — Mg, Zn, Ti).
- ✅ **Dislocation** = line defect; Burger vector **b** characterizes it.
- ✅ **Edge dislocation:** b ⊥ line. **Screw dislocation:** b ∥ line.
- ✅ **Schmid's Law:** τ_R = σ·cos φ·cos λ. Slip begins when τ_R ≥ τ_CRSS.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch:** σ_y = σ_0 + k_y/√d — strength increases with **smaller** grains.
- ✅ **Solid solution strengthening:** τ ∝ √c — more solute → stronger.
- ✅ **Strain hardening (Hollomon):** σ = K·ε^n — cold work multiplies dislocations.
- ✅ **Orowan looping:** τ = Gb/L — hard particles force dislocations to bow around them.
- ✅ **Cutting vs. looping transition:** at critical particle radius r_c.
- ✅ **Stacking fault energy (SFE):** Low SFE → wider dissociation → cross-slip hard → high n.
- ✅ **Strengthening mechanisms are additive:** σ_total = σ_0 + σ_HP + σ_SS + σ_SH + σ_PPT.
- ✅ **Pure metals are weak; strengthening = impeding dislocations; ductility is the trade-off.**
- ✅ **Engineering alloys (7075, maraging steel, Inconel) use combinations of all four mechanisms.**
- ✅ **APT/APF = π/(3√2) ≈ 0.74 for FCC and HCP** — memorize for derivation questions.
- ✅ **k_y units depend on d units** — be careful in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Super Alloys and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Super Alloys and Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SUPERALLOY (KTU 2024 Official Definition)** > A **superalloy** is a metallic alloy engineered for service at extreme temperatures (typically above **540 °C / 1000 °F**) and severe mechanical stress, possessing exceptional **creep resistance**, **oxidation resistance**, **corrosion resistance**, and **microstructural stability** under thermomechanical loading. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMET635 – Advanced Materials)**, superalloys form a critical class of *high-temperature engineering materials* that retain their mechanical and surface-integrity properties at temperatures where conventional alloys (steels, brasses, aluminium alloys) would soften, oxidize catastrophically, or creep-fail. The three officially recognized families are: | Family | Base Metal (wt% > 50%) | Maximum Service Temperature | |---|---|---| | **Iron-based** | Fe | ~ 650 °C | | **Nickel-based** | Ni | ~ 1100 °C | | **Cobalt-based** | Co | ~ 950 °C | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Workhorses of a Jet Engine" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Check for First-Time Learners** > Imagine a fighter-jet engine as a marathon runner who must keep sprinting inside a furnace at half the speed of melting ice. - **Nickel-based superalloys** are the *marathon champion* of the trio. They have an **FCC (Face-Centered Cubic) crystal structure** that never changes even when heated to red-hot temperatures (unlike steels that transform to BCC). This makes them ideal for the **hottest parts** — single-crystal turbine blades in modern jet engines. - **Cobalt-based superalloys** are the *specialist swimmer*. They fight off **hot corrosion** (attack by salts and sulphur in jet fuel) better than anyone — used in marine gas turbines and combustor liners. - **Iron-based superalloys** are the *budget workhorse*. They give reasonable high-temperature performance at **one-third the cost** of nickel-based grades — used where temperatures are moderate, such as **steam turbine casings** and **boiler tubes**. ## 1.3 Why Superalloys Exist — The Engineering Need > [!IMPORTANT] > The driving force behind superalloy development is the **Carnot efficiency** requirement. The thermal efficiency $\eta$ of a gas-turbine cycle rises linearly with the **turbine-inlet temperature (TIT)**. Every **100 °C** rise in TIT yields approximately **2–4 %** improvement in specific fuel consumption. The governing thermodynamic limit: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$ Since $T_{\text{hot}}$ (turbine-inlet) is the only variable engineers can aggressively push, the **maximum allowable metal temperature** in the hot section becomes the *single most important material property*. This is exactly what superalloys are engineered to maximize. ## 1.4 Historical Snapshot (for KTU Exam Essays) - **1929** — Merica, Teeple & Freeman patent the first Ni-based superalloy (80 Ni – 20 Cr with Ti + Al additions) at the **National Bureau of Standards (USA)**. This was the birth of the **γ′ (gamma-prime) precipitation-hardened** class. - **1940s** — Nimonic 80A introduced by **Mond Nickel Company (UK)** — first commercial superalloy for Whittle jet engines. - **1960s–1970s** — Directionally solidified (DS) and single-crystal (SX) turbine blades become reality. - **1980s** — Oxide-dispersion-strengthened (ODS) alloys (e.g., MA 956) developed by mechanical alloying. - **2000s–Present** — 4th & 5th generation single-crystal Ni-superalloys (e.g., TMS-238, CMSX-4 Plus) with Re, Ru, and Ir additions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Master Strengthening Mechanisms All superalloys, irrespective of their base metal, rely on a **synergy of four strengthening pillars**. Understanding these is *mandatory* for KTU 14-mark questions. ### Pillar I — Solid-Solution Strengthening - Solute atoms (e.g., **W, Mo, Cr, Co**) of larger atomic radii are dissolved in the **γ (gamma) FCC matrix**. - They generate a **coherency strain field** that impedes dislocation motion. - Strengthening contribution scales with the **shear modulus mismatch** and **atomic-size misfit parameter $\delta$**. $$\delta = \frac{d_{\text{solute}} - d_{\text{solvent}}}{d_{\text{solvent}}}$$ where $d$ is the atomic diameter. ### Pillar II — Precipitation Strengthening (γ′ and γ″) This is the **signature mechanism** of Ni-based superalloys. - **γ′ (gamma-prime)** = Ni₃(Al, Ti) — an ordered **L1₂ (cubic)** intermetallic, coherent with the matrix. - **γ″ (gamma-double-prime)** = Ni₃Nb — an ordered **DO₂₂ (tetragonal)** phase, present in **Inconel 718**. - Strengthening depends on: - **Volume fraction $f_v$** of γ′ (modern SX alloys: $f_v \approx 60\text{–}70\%$) - **Lattice misfit $\delta$** between γ and γ′ - **Particle size $r$** and **anti-phase boundary (APB) energy $\gamma_{\text{APB}}$** ### Pillar III — Carbide Strengthening Three families of carbides form at grain boundaries: - **MC** (M = Ti, Nb, Hf, Ta) — primary, blocky - **M₂₃C₆** (M = Cr) — discrete grain-boundary precipitates, inhibit sliding - **M₆C** (M = Mo, W) — degenerate script form ### Pillar IV — Oxide Dispersion Strengthening (ODS) - Nano-scale inert oxides (Y₂O₃, typically 0.5–2 wt%, 10–50 nm) are mechanically alloyed into the matrix. - They are **thermodynamically stable up to the melting point** and are not consumed during service. - Used in **rocket nozzles, heat-exchanger tubes, and nuclear reactor claddings**. ## 2.2 Why FCC? — The Crystal-Structure Argument > [!NOTE] > Steels are BCC and undergo **allotropic transformation** (α-Fe ↔ γ-Fe ↔ δ-Fe) on heating. This destroys the precipitate distribution and causes *catastrophic grain growth*. Nickel and cobalt remain **FCC from room temperature to melting point** — giving them intrinsic microstructural stability at high T. Cobalt is an *exception*: it is **HCP at room temperature** and **FCC above 417 °C**. This is why Co-superalloys are typically used in the *fully stabilized FCC* form (often with **Ni additions** to lower the HCP→FCC transition). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This table contains the **entire mathematical toolkit** required to solve KTU numerical/semi-numerical questions on superalloys. | # | Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{ss}} = A_{\text{ss}}\,\sigma^{n_{\text{ss}}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{\text{ss}}}{RT}\right)$ | Norton-Bailey steady-state creep law; $A_{\text{ss}}$ = Dorn constant, $n_{\text{ss}}$ = stress exponent (3–8), $Q_{\text{ss}}$ = activation energy of creep (J mol⁻¹), $R$ = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | Compute secondary creep rate | | 2 | $\text{LMP} = \dfrac{T}{1000}\,\bigl(\log\,t_r + C\bigr)$ | **Larson–Miller Parameter**; $T$ in Kelvin, $t_r$ = rupture time in hours, $C \approx 20$ (constant) | Correlate stress, T, and rupture life | | 3 | $\sigma_{\text{ys}} = \sigma_0 + k\,d^{-1/2}$ | **Hall–Petch** relation; $d$ = mean grain diameter | Yield strength vs grain size | | 4 | $\tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}}}{2b}\left[\left(\dfrac{2f_v\,\pi\,\gamma_{\text{APB}}}{Gb^2}\right)^{1/2} - f_v\right]$ | Shear stress to cut a γ′ precipitate; $\gamma_{\text{APB}}$ = anti-phase boundary energy, $b$ = Burgers vector, $G$ = shear modulus | Precipitation hardening model (weak-pair coupling) | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$ | Carnot efficiency of ideal heat engine | Justify demand for higher-T alloys | | 6 | $\delta = \dfrac{a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma}}{a_{\gamma}}$ | **Lattice misfit** between γ′ and γ | Predict rafting behaviour | | 7 | $\sigma_{\text{OR}} \;\propto\; \dfrac{f_v \cdot \gamma_{\text{APB}}}{b \cdot L}$ | **Orowan looping** stress; $L$ = inter-particle spacing | When particles are bypassed, not cut | | 8 | $T_m \;\uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{creep rate} \;\downarrow$ | Homologous temperature $T/T_m$ controls creep | General qualitative comparison | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Pitfall:** Throughout the table, **use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for modulus**, never the bare pipe symbol. In markdown it breaks table layout. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations - **Equation 1 (Norton creep)** is used by **GE Aviation** to size the wall thickness of a turbine disc so it survives 30,000 flight cycles without creep-rupture. - **Equation 2 (LMP)** is the foundation of *stress-rupture extrapolation* — engineers can run a 1000-hour test and *predict* 100,000-hour life. - **Equation 6 (lattice misfit)** is what material scientists tune by adding Re, W, and Ru to next-generation SX alloys. A *negative* misfit of $-0.2\%$ produces the famous **rafting** microstructure that gives single-crystal blades extra life. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Type-by-Type Deep Dive > [!IMPORTANT] > This section replaces algebraic derivations (superalloy theory is microstructural, not calculus-driven) with a **full analytical matrix** that compares every sub-type. The "derivation" here is the *logical chain of reasoning* by which composition produces microstructure, which produces property, which produces application. ## 3.1 Detailed Type-I: Iron-Based Superalloys ### Composition Logic - Base: **Fe** (>50 wt%) - Major alloying: **Cr (10–25 wt%)** for oxidation resistance, **Ni (10–40 wt%)** to stabilize the FCC γ-matrix, **Mo, W, V** for solid-solution strengthening. - Minor: **Ti, Al, Nb, B, Zr** for precipitation and grain-boundary control. ### Microstructure - **γ (FCC Fe-Ni matrix)** + **γ′ [Ni₃(Al,Ti)]** + **carbides (MC, M₂₃C₆)** + occasional **Laves phase**. - Cannot tolerate large γ′ fractions (Fe destabilizes γ′). Maximum service temperature is therefore limited to ~ 650 °C. ### Commercial Examples - **A-286** — workhorse; 25 Ni – 15 Cr – 1.2 Mo – 2 Ti – 0.3 Al – 0.005 B - **Incoloy 800 / 800H / 800HT** — 33 Ni – 21 Cr – 0.4 Ti – 0.4 Al - **N-155** (Multimet) — complex Fe-Ni-Co grade for combustor liners ### Application Map - Steam-turbine **casings and bolts** (650 °C max) - **Petrochemical cracker tubes** - **Heat-exchanger tubing** in nuclear power plants - **Low-pressure turbine blades** in older aero engines (e.g., JT3D first stage) ## 3.2 Detailed Type-II: Nickel-Based Superalloys This is the **dominant family** — it accounts for > 75 % of all superalloy tonnage used in jet engines. ### Sub-Categorization by Strengthening Mode | Sub-Class | Strengthening Mode | Example | T-max (°C) | |---|---|---|---| | Solid-solution | γ + carbides only | **Hastelloy X**, Inconel 600 | 950 | | γ′-strengthened | γ + γ′ | **Nimonic 80A**, Waspaloy | 1000 | | γ′+γ″ strengthened | Both precipitates | **Inconel 718** (workhorse) | 700 | | Oxide-dispersion | γ + Y₂O₃ | **MA 754**, MA 956 | 1200 | | Directionally solidified (DS) | γ + γ′ aligned | **Mar-M 002 DS** | 1050 | | Single-crystal (SX) | γ + γ′ no grain boundaries | **CMSX-4**, PWA 1484, TMS-238 | 1100–1150 | ### Composition Logic (CMSX-4 as the Reference Standard) - **Ni = bal., Cr = 6.5, Co = 9, Mo = 0.6, W = 6, Al = 5.6, Ti = 1, Ta = 6.5, Re = 3, Hf = 0.1, C < 0.006** - **Re (Rhenium)** is the expensive "magic element" — adds **solid-solution strengthening** and **slows γ′ coarsening**. Modern 4th-generation alloys contain 4–6 wt% Re. - **Al + Ti + Ta + Nb** are the **γ′-formers**. Total γ′-former content controls the volume fraction of γ′. - **Cr** gives oxidation resistance but *excess Cr (>10%)* promotes **TCP (Topologically Close-Packed)** brittle phases — a major design constraint. - **Hf, Y, La** improve oxide-scale adhesion and **DS-scratch resistance**. ### Microstructure - **γ matrix** (FCC, Ni-rich, ~70 vol%) - **γ′ precipitates** (L1₂ ordered, cubic, 0.2–0.5 µm in solution-treated condition, ~30 vol% in CMSX-4) - **γ/γ′ eutectic islands** in as-cast structure - **MC carbides** at interdendritic regions - **TCP phases** (σ, µ, P) form as embrittling by-products if composition drifts out of the safe envelope ### Property → Application Mapping > [!NOTE] > The *application flow* in a modern high-bypass turbofan is rigorously zone-dependent. Reading the engine cold-to-hot: 1. **Fan blades & discs** — Ti-alloys (lower T) 2. **Compressor stages 1–3** — Ti-alloys transitioning to **Inconel 718** at stage 8+ 3. **Combustor liner** — **Haynes 188** (Co-based) or **Hastelloy X** (Ni-based) 4. **HP/HP turbine blades** — **CMSX-4 / PWA 1484** single-crystal superalloys 5. **HP turbine disc** — **Udimet 720Li** or **ME3** powder-metallurgy alloys 6. **After-burner / exhaust nozzle** — **Inconel 625** or **Waspaloy** 7. **Rocket nozzle throat** — **René N5** ODS variants ## 3.3 Detailed Type-III: Cobalt-Based Superalloys ### Composition Logic - **Co = bal., Cr = 20–30 wt%** (for oxidation and hot-corrosion resistance) - **Ni (10–25%)** stabilizes FCC, suppresses ε-HCP phase - **W, Mo (5–15%)** solid-solution strengthening - **C (0.25–1%)** produces M₂₃C₆ carbides (the *primary* strengthening agent in Co-superalloys) - **Ta, Ti, Zr, B** carbide formers and grain-boundary modifiers ### Microstructure - **γ FCC matrix** (Co-Ni-Cr-W) - **M₂₃C₆ carbides** (mainly Cr-rich) — often script-like or lamellar - **MC carbides** (Ti, Ta-rich) - No γ′ phase — Co cannot form the L1₂ γ′ to a useful extent (the compound Co₃(Al,Ti) is not stable enough). - Hence Co-superalloys are *carbide-strengthened only* — explains why they cannot compete with Ni-based γ′ alloys in raw creep life. ### Commercial Examples - **Haynes 188** — 22 Ni – 22 Cr – 14 W – 0.1 La — aircraft combustor & after-burner - **Haynes 25 (L-605)** — 10 Ni – 20 Cr – 15 W — medical implants, springs - **Stellite 6B** — 28 Cr – 4.5 W – 1.1 C — hard-facing alloy (not a "true" superalloy but classified under the family) - **MAR-M 302 / 509** — high-Cr cast grades for vanes and combustors - **X-40 / X-45** — dental and industrial furnace alloys ### Application Map - **Combustor liners** in marine and aero gas turbines — salt-laden air → hot corrosion - **Industrial furnace rolls**, radiant tubes - **Guide vanes** (HP/LP stators) in older gas turbines - **Biomedical implants** (Co-Cr-Mo) — uses fatigue strength and biocompatibility, not high T - **Hard-facing weld overlays** on boiler tubes, valve seats ## 3.4 The Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **highest-density page** of the entire KTU Module 1 syllabus. Memorize this matrix — it answers 80 % of the 14-mark essay questions. | Property | Iron-Based | Nickel-Based | Cobalt-Based | |---|---|---|---| | Base metal | Fe | Ni | Co | | Crystal structure at RT | FCC (γ) | FCC (γ) | HCP (ε) → FCC (γ) at 417 °C | | Dominant precipitate | γ′ weak / carbides | γ′ (L1₂) and γ″ (DO₂₂) | M₂₃C₆ carbides | | Max service T (°C) | 650 | 1100–1150 | 950 | | Creep resistance | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good | | Hot-corrosion resistance | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** | | Oxidation resistance | Good | Excellent | Excellent | | Cost | **Lowest** | High (Re, W expensive) | High (Co expensive) | | Weldability | Excellent | Fair (γ′-rich grades crack) | Excellent | | Density (g cm⁻³) | ~ 7.9 | ~ 8.0–8.5 | ~ 8.5–9.0 | | Typical component | Boiler tubes, casings | **Turbine blades & discs** | Combustor liners, vanes | | Key example | A-286, Incoloy 800H | Inconel 718, CMSX-4 | Haynes 188, Stellite 6B | ## 3.5 Logical Derivation of the Application Selection Rule > [!NOTE] > This is a *reasoning chain*, not algebraic. KTU examiners love questions framed as "Justify why a nickel-based SX superalloy is used in HP turbine blades and not an iron-based alloy." **Step 1 — Identify the operating regime.** HP turbine inlet temperature = **1600 °C** (gas-side). Metal-side temperature at the blade leading edge = ~ 1150 °C. Centrifugal stress on the blade = ~ 150 MPa. **Step 2 — Compute homologous temperature.** For Ni-base superalloy with $T_m \approx 1400\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $$\dfrac{T}{T_m} = \dfrac{1150 + 273}{1400 + 273} = 0.85$$ Above 0.5, creep dominates. The selected alloy must therefore have **exceptional creep-rupture life** at $T/T_m = 0.85$. **Step 3 — Screen by precipitate type.** - Fe-based: max $T/T_m \approx 0.55$ → eliminated. - Co-based: max $T/T_m \approx 0.75$ → marginal; also no γ′. - Ni-based with γ′: can survive $T/T_m \approx 0.85$ → **selected**. **Step 4 — Screen by form (cast / wrought / SX).** Creep is grain-boundary-sliding-dominated. A wrought (polycrystalline) blade has grain boundaries *perpendicular* to the centrifugal stress axis — the *worst* orientation. A **single-crystal** blade has *no grain boundaries at all* — the maximum-creep-resistant form. **Step 5 — Conclusion.** A **Ni-based single-crystal superalloy (CMSX-4 / PWA 1484)** is the only family that simultaneously satisfies *creep life* + *transverse-rupture strength* + *oxidation resistance* + *thermal-mechanical fatigue* at HP-blade service conditions. ## 3.6 Advanced Variants (for Topical Marks in KTU 14-Mark Essays) ### 3.6.1 ODS (Oxide-Dispersion-Strengthened) Superalloys - **MA 754, MA 956, PM 2000** — produced by **mechanical alloying** in attritor mills. - Y₂O₃ dispersoids (10–50 nm) survive even after the matrix melts. - Used in **combustion-chamber liners, nuclear fuel cladding, rocket thrust chambers**. ### 3.6.2 Powder-Metallurgy (PM) Superalloys - **René 95, Udimet 720, ME3, RR1000**. - Rapidly solidified (RS) pre-alloyed powder is hot-isostatically pressed (HIP) + extruded. - Gives **isotropic fine grain** — high fatigue strength with reasonable creep. - Used for **turbine discs** in modern high-bypass engines. ### 3.6.3 Intermetallic Superalloys (Frontier Research) - **NiAl, Ni₃Al, TiAl**. - B2-ordered NiAl has **density 5.9 g cm⁻³** (vs 8.5 for Inconel 718) — a **30 % mass saving**. - Low ductility at RT remains the *show-stopper* — under active research. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Superalloys > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **entire taxonomy** of superalloys as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each leaf node lists a *representative alloy* and its *primary engineering application*. ```mermaid graph TD ROOT["SUPERALLOYS
High-T Alloys above 540 C"]:::root ROOT --> FB["Iron Based"] ROOT --> NB["Nickel Based"] ROOT --> CB["Cobalt Based"] FB --> FB1["Solid Solution Grades"] FB --> FB2["Precipitation Grades"] FB1 --> FB1A["Incoloy 800H
Steam Pipes"] FB1 --> FB1B["N 155 Multimet
Combustor Cans"] FB2 --> FB2A["A 286
Bolts Casings"] FB2 --> FB2B["Incoloy 901
LP Turbine Discs"] NB --> NB1["Wrought Ni Base"] NB --> NB2["Cast Polycrystalline"] NB --> NB3["Directionally Solidified"] NB --> NB4["Single Crystal SX"] NB --> NB5["ODS Ni Base"] NB1 --> NB1A["Inconel 718
Discs Casings"] NB1 --> NB1B["Waspaloy
Compressor Blades"] NB1 --> NB1C["Hastelloy X
Combustor Ducts"] NB2 --> NB2A["Mar M 247
Industrial Gas Turbine"] NB2 --> NB2B["IN 100
Aerospace Vanes"] NB3 --> NB3A["Mar M 002 DS
DS Turbine Blades"] NB3 --> NB3B["GTD 111
Power Gen Blades"] NB4 --> NB4A["CMSX 4
Aero HP Blades"] NB4 --> NB4B["PWA 1484
Jet Engine Stage 1"] NB4 --> NB4C["TMS 238
5th Gen Re Ru Alloy"] NB5 --> NB5A["MA 754
Combustor Liners"] NB5 --> NB5B["MA 6000
Turbine Blades ODS"] CB --> CB1["Wrought Co Base"] CB --> CB2["Cast Co Base"] CB1 --> CB1A["Haynes 25 L605
Springs Medical"] CB1 --> CB1B["Haynes 188
Afterburner Parts"] CB2 --> CB2A["MAR M 302

Hard Facing Welds"] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef fe fill:#6b8e9b,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef ni fill:#b58d49,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef co fill:#8a4a6a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff class FB,FB1,FB2,FB1A,FB1B,FB2A,FB2B fe class NB,NB1,NB2,NB3,NB4,NB5,NB1A,NB1B,NB1C,NB2A,NB2B,NB3A,NB3B,NB4A,NB4B,NB4C,NB5A,NB5B ni class CB,CB1,CB2,CB1A,CB1B,CB2A,CB2B,CB2C co ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Strength-to-Application Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["ENGINEER DEFINES
Operating T and Stress"]:::input B{"T less than 650 C"}:::decision C{"T between 650 and 950 C"}:::decision D{"T above 950 C"}:::decision E{"Stress state cyclic or static"}:::decision F["Iron Based
A 286 Incoloy 800H"]:::iron G["Co Base Haynes 188
