or Ni Cast Mar M 247"]:::nickel I["Ni Cast DS Mar M 002"]:::nickel2 J["Ni SX CMSX 4 PWA 1484"]:::nickel3 K["ODS MA 754
or Pt aluminide coated"]:::ods A --> B B -- Yes --> E E -- Cyclic --> F E -- Static --> F B -- No --> C C -- Hot corrosion environment --> G C -- Clean environment --> H C -- No --> D D -- Creep limited --> I D -- Highest T --> J D -- Extreme T above 1100 C --> K classDef input fill:#0d3b66,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef decision fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef iron fill:#6b8e9b,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef nickel fill:#b58d49,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef nickel2 fill:#c9a16a,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef nickel3 fill:#d4af6a,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef cobalt fill:#8a4a6a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef ods fill:#5a3a2a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff class A input class B,C,D,E decision class F iron class G cobalt class H,I nickel class J nickel3 class K ods ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Microstructure of a Single-Crystal Superalloy > [!NOTE] > The schematic below depicts the **γ/γ′ rafted microstructure** of a CMSX-4 blade after ~1000 h of service at 1100 °C / 100 MPa. ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Single-crystal Ni-superalloy (cross-section, [001] view) │ │ │ │ ╔═══════════╗ γ-matrix channels ╔═══════════╗ │ │ ║ γ′ ║◄──────────────────► ║ γ′ ║ │ │ ║ (L1₂) ║ (γ, dark, ~30% vol) ║ (L1₂) ║ │ │ ║ Ni₃(Al,Ti)║ ║ Ni₃(Al,Ti)║ │ │ ╚═══════════╝ ╚═══════════╝ │ │ │ │ ◄─── 0.4 µm raft (height) ───► │ │ │ │ Direction of applied centrifugal tensile stress: ↑ │ │ Rafts orient PERPENDICULAR to stress axis (N-type raft) │ │ This blocks dislocation climb → enhanced creep life. │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 4.4 ASCII Phase-Stability Map — Compositional Trade-off in Ni-Superalloys ``` Cr wt% (Oxidation / Hot-corrosion resistance) ↑ 30│ · · · · · · │ ↑ 20│ · · · · · · │ TCP phases │ (σ, µ) form 10│ · · ·[SAFE ZONE]· · · ← Ni-base sweet spot │ ↓ 5│ · · · · · · │ 0└──────────────────────────────→ 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 (Al + Ti + Ta + Nb) wt% — γ′ volume fraction ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) **Q:** Define the term "superalloy" and name the three principal families. State the *minimum temperature* above which a material is conventionally classified as a superalloy service environment. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question | May 2024]** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):** A superalloy is a metallic alloy that retains **high mechanical strength, creep resistance, oxidation resistance and microstructural stability** above **540 °C (1000 °F)**. The three families are **(i) iron-based**, **(ii) nickel-based**, and **(iii) cobalt-based**, with the base metal contributing more than 50 wt% of the composition. They are essential for hot-section components of jet engines, gas turbines, and rocket propulsion systems where conventional steels would fail by creep rupture or oxidation. (3 marks) ### Question 2 (3 marks) **Q:** What is the **γ′ (gamma-prime) phase** in nickel-based superalloys? Write its chemical formula and explain why it provides such strong precipitation hardening. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):** The **γ′ (gamma-prime)** phase is an ordered intermetallic precipitate of the form **Ni₃(Al, Ti)** having the **L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type) face-centred cubic crystal structure**. It is **coherent** with the γ-FCC matrix and possesses a small lattice misfit (~ 0.2 %) with the matrix. Dislocations moving through the matrix must either *cut* the ordered γ′ (creating an anti-phase boundary, APB) or *bow around* it (Orowan looping). Both mechanisms consume energy, producing exceptionally high-temperature strength. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with internal choice between Question A and Question B. Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Iron-based & Cobalt-based superalloys* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** > **(a)** Compare and contrast the **microstructure and strengthening mechanisms** of **iron-based and cobalt-based superalloys**. Why are cobalt-based alloys preferred for marine gas-turbine combustor liners despite their lower creep strength than nickel-based alloys? > **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With suitable examples, classify **iron-based superalloys** into solid-solution and precipitation grades. List the typical applications of **A-286** and **Incoloy 800H**, and explain the role of **chromium** in both. > **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks | Sub-point | Marks | Model Content | |---|---|---| | Microstructure of Fe-based | 1 | γ-FCC matrix (Fe-Ni), γ′ [Ni₃(Al,Ti)] minor, MC and M₂₃C₆ carbides, occasional Laves. | | Microstructure of Co-based | 1 | γ-FCC matrix (Co-Ni-Cr-W), **M₂₃C₆ carbides** as primary strengthener, no γ′ (Co₃(Al,Ti) unstable). | | Strengthening in Fe | 1 | Solid-solution (W, Mo) + γ′ precipitation + carbide pinning; max T ~ 650 °C because Fe destabilises γ′. | | Strengthening in Co | 1 | Carbide-dominant (M₂₃C₆), W/Mo solid-solution, no γ′; stable up to ~ 950 °C. | | Why Co for marine combustors | 2 | (i) **High Cr (20–30 wt%)** → dense Cr₂O₃ scale resists **hot corrosion** by NaCl and S from sea-salt-laden intake air. (ii) Higher **melting point** of Co than Ni gives a thermodynamic margin. (iii) Better **weldability and thermal-shock resistance** — combustors see frequent start-stop cycles. (iv) Ni-based γ′ alloys would be over-engineered and over-priced for the combustor's mid-temperature regime (~ 900 °C). | | Synthesis | 1 | Co-superalloys trade raw creep strength for **environmental resistance and fabricability** — the right compromise for combustor duty. | #### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks > **Sub-question 1 — Classification of Fe-based superalloys (3 marks):** | Grade | Type | Typical Composition | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Incoloy 800H** | Solid-solution | 33 Ni – 21 Cr – 0.4 Ti – 0.4 Al | Steam-generator tubing, reformer outlets | | **N-155 (Multimet)** | Solid-solution | 20 Ni – 21 Cr – 20 Co – 3 W | Combustor cans, transition ducts | | **A-286** | Precipitation (γ′) | 26 Ni – 15 Cr – 1.2 Mo – 2 Ti – 0.3 Al | LP turbine blades, bolts, casings | | **Incoloy 901** | Precipitation (γ′) | 42 Ni – 13 Cr – 6 Mo – 2.7 Ti | LP turbine discs, shafts | > **Sub-question 2 — Role of Cr in Fe-superalloys (2 marks):** Chromium (10–25 wt%) is the **primary oxidation and corrosion resistor**. It forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **Cr₂O₃ layer** on the surface that blocks further inward diffusion of oxygen and outward diffusion of metal cations. In A-286, 15 % Cr gives 650 °C service; in Incoloy 800H, 21 % Cr gives superior resistance to **carburisation and nitridation** in petrochemical furnaces. > **Sub-question 3 — Application justification (2 marks):** A-286's γ′ content gives high strength-to-weight ratio → it is used in **LP turbine blades, rotor discs, and high-temperature fasteners** in jet engines and industrial gas turbines. Incoloy 800H, with its higher Ni + Cr content, is immune to **σ-phase embrittlement** in the 540–760 °C range and is therefore the standard material for **ethylene pyrolysis furnace tubes and superheater tubing**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Nickel-based superalloys* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** > **(a)** Discuss the **four principal strengthening mechanisms** operative in nickel-based superalloys. How does the addition of **aluminium + titanium + tantalum** control the volume fraction of γ′? Why is the addition of **rhenium (Re)** central to fourth-generation single-crystal superalloys? > **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With a clear classification, list the **five sub-classes of nickel-based superalloys** (wrought, cast-polycrystalline, directionally-solidified, single-crystal, ODS). Give **one commercial example** for each, state the **maximum service temperature**, and identify the **typical aero-engine component** for which each sub-class is selected. > **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks > **Mechanism 1 — Solid-Solution Strengthening (1.5 marks):** Atoms of **W, Mo, Cr, Co** are dissolved in the **γ-FCC Ni matrix**. They generate a **coherency strain field** because their atomic radii differ from Ni. The interaction energy between this strain field and the stress field of a moving dislocation is given approximately by: > > $$\tau_{\text{ss}} \;\propto\; G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$$ > > where $G$ = shear modulus, $\varepsilon$ = interaction parameter (combining size and modulus misfit), and $c$ = solute concentration. Higher W + Mo in modern alloys directly raises high-T strength. > **Mechanism 2 — Precipitation Strengthening by γ′ (2 marks):** γ′ is an ordered **L1₂ Ni₃(Al, Ti)** precipitate coherent with γ. Two operating regimes: > 1. **Particle shearing (small γ′)** — dislocations cut pairs and create an **anti-phase boundary (APB)**. The cutting stress is given by: > > $$\tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}}}{2b}\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi f_v \gamma_{\text{APB}}}{Gb^2}} - f_v\right]$$ > > 2. **Orowan looping (large γ′)** — dislocations bow between particles. Looping stress is: > > $$\tau_{\text{OR}} = \dfrac{Gb}{L - 2r}$$ > > where $L$ is inter-particle spacing and $r$ is particle radius. Modern SX alloys have $f_v \approx 60\text{–}70\%$ to maximise $\tau_{\text{cut}}$. > **Mechanism 3 — Carbide Strengthening (1 mark):** **MC, M₂₃C₆, M₆C** carbides pin grain boundaries and impede sliding. In Inconel 718, **NbC** is critical — its dissolution during solution treatment and re-precipitation as γ″ drives the famous **two-step age-hardening (720 °C / 8 h + 620 °C / 8 h)**. > **Mechanism 4 — Grain-Boundary & Dispersion Strengthening (1 mark):** In polycrystalline alloys, **B, Zr, Hf, Mg** segregate to grain boundaries and **increase cohesion**, suppressing cavitation. In ODS alloys, **nano-Y₂O₃** particles act as dislocation obstacles stable to $T_m$. > **Sub-question on Al + Ti + Ta (1 mark):** Al, Ti, Ta, and Nb are **γ′-formers**. Their **sum (Al + Ti + Ta + Nb)** directly controls the **volume fraction of γ′** at the ageing temperature. Increasing the sum from ~ 5 % (Inconel 718) to ~ 13 % (CMSX-4) raises γ′ volume fraction from ~ 15 % to ~ 70 %. > **Sub-question on Rhenium (0.5 mark):** Re (typically 3–6 wt%) partitions preferentially to the **γ-matrix** and slows down γ′ **coarsening kinetics**, enabling 4th-generation SX alloys to operate at **1100–1150 °C** with 3–5× longer creep life than 2nd-generation alloys. Re also suppresses the formation of **TCP brittle phases**. #### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks | Sub-Class | Commercial Example | Max T (°C) | Typical Aero-Engine Component | Marks | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Wrought Ni-base** | Inconel 718 | 700 | **Turbine discs**, LP/HP compressor drums, casings, shafts | 1.5 | | **Cast polycrystalline** | Mar-M 247 | 980 | Industrial gas-turbine blades, **combustor swirlers**, rocket-engine turbopump housings | 1.5 | | **Directionally solidified (DS)** | GTD-111 / Mar-M 002 DS | 1050 | **HP / IP turbine blades** (columnar grain, grain boundaries parallel to stress axis) | 1.5 | | **Single-crystal (SX)** | CMSX-4 / PWA 1484 / TMS-238 | 1100–1150 | **1st-stage HP turbine blade** (no grain boundaries, rafting-capable) | 1.5 | | **ODS Ni-base** | MA 754 / MA 6000 | 1200+ | **Combustor liner, flame holder**, heat-exchanger tubes, rocket thrust chamber liner | 1.0 | > **Synthesis (extra credit-bearing statement):** The progression **wrought → cast → DS → SX → ODS** mirrors the historical march toward higher turbine-inlet temperatures driven by the **Carnot efficiency** requirement, and represents one of the most successful material-design escalations in modern engineering. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic — Five Most Common Mistakes** > 1. **Conflating γ′ and γ″ phases.** γ′ = Ni₃(Al,Ti), L1₂, present in most Ni-superalloys. γ″ = Ni₃Nb, DO₂₂, present **only in Inconel 718-class alloys**. Many students write "γ″" in answers about CMSX-4 — *instant 2-mark deduction*. > 2. **Mis-stating crystal structure.** Writing that Ni-superalloys are BCC. They are **FCC (γ matrix)** — that is the *whole point* of using Ni instead of Fe. > 3. **Forgetting the role of Rhenium.** Modern 3rd/4th/5th generation SX alloys are defined by Re/Ru additions. A 14-mark essay on "Ni superalloys" *must* mention Re — students who omit it lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Citing Nimonic 80A as the "strongest" superalloy.** It was *the first* commercial γ′ alloy (1940s). Modern single-crystal CMSX-4 is 100× creep-stronger. Examiners expect a *time-aware* answer. > 5. **Mixing up Haynes 25 and Haynes 188.** Both are Co-based but **Haynes 188 = 22 % Cr + 14 % W** (combustor duty) whereas **Haynes 25 (L-605) = 20 % Cr + 15 % W + 0.1 % C** (springs/medical). Easy 1-mark slip. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this page. Memorize it. Walk into the exam hall with it in your head.** - [x] A **superalloy** is a high-T alloy used above **540 °C**; three families: **Fe-, Ni-, Co-based**. - [x] The **FCC (γ) matrix** is non-negotiable for high-T service — it does not transform on heating. - [x] Four strengthening pillars: **solid-solution, γ′ precipitation, carbide, oxide-dispersion**. - [x] **γ′ = Ni₃(Al, Ti)**, L1₂ ordered, coherent with γ; volume fraction in modern SX alloys ≈ **60–70 %**. - [x] **γ″ = Ni₃Nb**, DO₂₂, only in **Inconel 718 family**; gives high strength up to ~ 700 °C. - [x] Iron-based: max ~ 650 °C; examples **A-286, Incoloy 800H**; cheap; boiler/steam-turbine duty. - [x] Nickel-based: max ~ 1100–1150 °C; examples **Inconel 718, Waspaloy, Mar-M 247, CMSX-4**. - [x] Cobalt-based: max ~ 950 °C; examples **Haynes 188, Stellite 6B, MAR-M 302**; hot-corrosion champion. - [x] **Single-crystal CMSX-4** is the apex — used in **1st-stage HP turbine blades** of modern jet engines. - [x] **Re (Rhenium)** = "magic element" of 3rd-4th gen SX alloys; slows γ′ coarsening. - [x] **Larson–Miller Parameter** = $T(\log t_r + C) / 1000$, $C \approx 20$ — the stress-rupture extrapolation tool. - [x] **Norton creep law** = $\dot{\varepsilon} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ — secondary creep rate. - [x] **Carnot efficiency** = $1 - T_{\text{cold}}/T_{\text{hot}}$ — thermodynamic reason to develop hotter-running alloys. - [x] Hot corrosion → Co-based. Highest creep → Ni-based SX. Lowest cost → Fe-based. Fabricability + bio → Co-Cr. - [x] **ODS alloys** (MA 754, MA 956) carry Y₂O₃ dispersoids stable to $T_m$ — used in rocket and nuclear duty. - [x] Sub-class hierarchy of Ni-base: **Wrought → Cast → DS → SX → ODS** (ascending T and cost). - [x] **MC, M₂₃C₆, M₆C** are the three carbide families; M₂₃C₆ (Cr-rich) is the Co-base workhorse carbide. - [x] **TCP phases** (σ, µ, P) are embrittling by-products — controlled by Cr/(Al+Ti+Ta) ratio. - [x] **Hafnium (Hf)** in SX alloys improves oxide-scale adhesion — *do not omit* in essays. - [x] **HCP→FCC transition in Co at 417 °C** — justifies the Ni addition in Co-superalloys. - [x] **Inconel 718** is the *most-produced* superalloy in the world — by tonnage. - [x] NiAl intermetallics = future low-density superalloys (~ 5.9 g cm⁻³) — research frontier. - [x] Engine cold-to-hot: **Ti → Inconel 718 → CMSX-4 → ODS / Pt-aluminide coated**. - [x] **Grain boundaries perpendicular to stress** are the *worst* orientation for creep — *single crystals eliminate them entirely*. - [x] **Rafting** = γ′ coalescence into platelets perpendicular to tensile stress axis; signature of well-aged SX blade. - [x] KTU 14-mark essays must contain: **definition + 3 families + 4 strengthening mechanisms + 1 specific application per family + γ/γ′ chemistry + Re role**. Anything less is incomplete.

topics.title: Bulk Metallic Glasses: Mechanism of Formation, Properties and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Metallic Glasses: Mechanism of Formation, Properties and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bulk Metallic Glasses (BMGs)** are a fascinating class of advanced engineering materials defined as **multi-component metallic alloys that can be solidified into a fully amorphous (non-crystalline) glassy state with bulk dimensions**, typically **≥ 1 mm** (and frequently in the centimeter range), at practically achievable cooling rates (often < 1000 K/s, and sometimes as low as 1 K/s).

Unlike conventional oxide glasses (e.g., window glass, fused silica), BMGs are composed primarily of **metallic elements** (Zr, Ti, Cu, Ni, Fe, Mg, Pd, Pt, Au, La, Al, Co, etc.) and exhibit a unique combination of metallic and glassy characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Snapshot:**
> A Bulk Metallic Glass (BMG) is a multicomponent alloy that vitrifies (freezes into a glass) at a *low critical cooling rate*, bypassing nucleation and growth of crystalline phases, to produce an amorphous structure with macroscopic dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Amorphous vs. Crystalline:**
> In a crystalline solid, atoms are arranged in a periodic, repeating lattice (long-range order). In an amorphous solid (like a BMG), atoms are frozen in a **disordered**, liquid-like arrangement — they possess only **short-range order** but **no long-range periodicity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are freezing ice cubes. If you freeze water *very* slowly, you get perfectly clear, crystalline ice with a regular structure. But if you pour water down a frozen waterfall so fast that the molecules have **no time to organize themselves into a crystal lattice**, you get **amorphous ice (vitreous ice)** — a glassy, disordered solid.

**Bulk Metallic Glasses are the metallic version of this "frozen waterfall" effect.**

When a molten metal alloy is cooled extremely rapidly, the atoms — which "want" to settle into the lowest-energy periodic crystal lattice — simply **do not have enough time** to find their proper positions. The liquid structure is thus "frozen in place," creating a glassy metal that is amorphous on the inside, even though the *outside* looks like a shiny, solid metallic block.

- **Conventional metals (like copper, steel):** Slow cooling → atoms arrange in crystals.
- **Rapidly quenched metallic ribbons (1970s):** Cooling rate ~10⁶ K/s → thin amorphous ribbons (only microns thick).
- **Bulk Metallic Glasses (1990s onwards):** Cooling rate ~1–1000 K/s → bulk-shaped amorphous parts (mm to cm).

### 1.3 The Critical Concept: "Bulk"

The term "**Bulk**" is what makes BMGs revolutionary. Earlier amorphous metals (called **metallic glasses** of the first generation, e.g., Fe₈₀B₂₀) could only be produced as **thin ribbons or wires** because the cooling rate required (10⁶ K/s) was achievable only at high surface-area-to-volume ratios. BMGs overcome this limitation by using **multi-component alloy chemistries** that intrinsically suppress crystallization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Metric for "Bulk":**
> The borderline dimension is usually **≥ 1 mm** in the smallest direction. State-of-the-art Pd-based BMGs have been cast in sections **> 10 cm**.

### 1.4 Why Are BMGs Important?

They combine properties that are normally contradictory in conventional materials:
- **High strength** (often near the theoretical limit) + **high elastic strain** (up to 2%).
- **Exceptional hardness** + **corrosion resistance**.
- **Soft magnetic behavior** (in Fe-based BMGs) + **amorphous structure**.
- **Superplastic formability** in the **supercooled liquid region** (above Tg but below Tx).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Hint:** The Apple iPhone SIM ejector pin used in the late 2000s, certain golf club heads, and some surgical blades have been made from Zr- and La-based BMGs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Formation — The "Why" Behind Vitrification

To form a BMG, we must **suppress nucleation** and **growth of crystalline phases** during solidification. The two central physical processes that must be defeated are:

1. **Homogeneous nucleation** of crystals in the liquid.
2. **Crystal growth** at the liquid–crystal interface.

If either process proceeds significantly, the material crystallizes (devitrifies) and is no longer amorphous. The strategy is therefore to **make it kinetically very hard for crystals to form and grow**.

#### 2.1.1 The Role of the TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) Diagram

The classic TTT (or CCT — Continuous Cooling Transformation) diagram is the central tool. A "**C-curve**" represents the onset of crystallization as a function of time and temperature.

- The **nose** of the C-curve represents the **shortest time** for crystallization to begin at a given temperature.
- To vitrify, the cooling curve must **bypass the nose of the C-curve** without intersecting it.
- The **critical cooling rate (Rc)** is the minimum cooling rate that just avoids the nose.

The deeper the "nose" lies in time (i.e., the further to the right it is pushed), the **lower the Rc** required, and the **better the glass-forming ability (GFA)** of the alloy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Metaphor:** Imagine you are running down a corridor with a swinging pendulum (the C-curve). To get past it, you must run *faster than the pendulum swings*. The deeper the C-curve, the more slowly you can run and still succeed — that is what alloy design does to BMGs.

#### 2.1.2 Inoue's Three Empirical Rules for BMG Formation

**Akihisa Inoue** (Tohoku University, late 1980s–1990s) proposed three empirical rules that have guided almost all BMG discoveries:

1. **Multi-component systems (≥ 3 elements).**
   - Increasing the number of components frustrates crystallization because the atoms cannot easily find their proper lattice sites.
   - Drives down the entropic free-energy barrier to amorphization.

2. **Significant atomic size mismatch (≥ 12% among the main constituents).**
   - Large size differences (e.g., Zr–Cu: ~25%, Mg–Cu: ~29%, La–Al: ~25%) lead to inefficient atomic packing in any crystalline phase → dense, disordered liquid → low diffusivity → suppressed crystallization.

3. **Negative heat of mixing (large negative ΔH_mix) among the main elements.**
   - Indicates strong chemical affinity between unlike atoms, leading to a **deep eutectic** in the phase diagram.
   - This **lowers the melting temperature (Tm)** relative to Tg, **widening the supercooled liquid region**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic: "Many atoms, Mixed sizes, Mismatched chemistry"** — 3 M's of Inoue.

### 2.2 Glass-Forming Ability (GFA) — Quantitative Measures

Several empirical and semi-empirical parameters quantify how easily an alloy vitrifies.

#### 2.2.1 Reduced Glass Transition Temperature (Trg) — Turnbull's Criterion

$$T_{rg} = \frac{T_g}{T_m}$$

- **T_g** = Glass transition temperature.
- **T_m** = Melting (liquidus) temperature.
- **Criterion:** A higher T_rg means a deeper cooling window for vitrification.
- **Threshold (Turnbull):** T_rg > 2/3 (≈ 0.67) is favorable for vitrification.

#### 2.2.2 Supercooled Liquid Region (ΔT_x)

$$\Delta T_x = T_x - T_g$$

- **T_x** = Onset temperature of crystallization.
- A **wider ΔT_x** means the amorphous alloy remains stable against crystallization over a broader processing window — useful for thermoplastic forming of BMGs.

#### 2.2.3 The γ Parameter (Lu & Liu)

$$\gamma = \frac{T_x}{T_g + T_l}$$

- Combines thermal stability of the glass and reduced liquidus temperature.
- Generally, **γ > 0.35** indicates good GFA.

#### 2.2.4 Critical Cooling Rate (R_c) and Critical Section Thickness (t_max)

$$R_c = \frac{T_m - T_g}{t_n}$$

- **t_n** is the time at the nose of the TTT curve. Inversely proportional to GFA — *smaller is better* for processing.

The **maximum section thickness (t_max)** an alloy can be cast in amorphous form is the most *engineering-relevant* GFA metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of GFA strength (descending):**
> Pd-based > Zr-based > La-based > Mg-based > Ti-based > Cu-based > Fe-based > Co-based > Ni-based.

#### 2.2.5 The Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT) Equation

The viscosity η of a supercooled metallic liquid follows:

$$\eta(T) = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{D^* \cdot T_0}{T - T_0}\right)$$

- **η₀** = pre-exponential constant (≈ 10⁻⁵ Pa·s).
- **D*** = fragility index (smaller = stronger liquid).
- **T₀** = VFT temperature (ideal glass transition).
- As **T → T_g**, the denominator (T − T₀) shrinks and η diverges toward 10¹² Pa·s — the operational definition of the glass transition.

### 2.3 Properties of BMGs

| Property Category | BMG Characteristic | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Yield strength 1.5–4× that of crystalline counterpart | Lightweight structural parts |
| **Elastic Limit** | ~2% (vs. ~0.2% for crystalline alloys) | Springs, sensors, strain-energy storage |
| **Hardness** | Very high (Vickers 500–1500 HV) | Cutting tools, dies |
| **Fracture Toughness** | Low in tension (often brittle), but some BMGs (e.g., Zr-based) show high toughness via shear-band formation and multiplication | Energy-absorbers |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Excellent (no grain boundaries, no dislocations) | Marine, biomedical |
| **Magnetic** | Soft ferromagnetism in Fe-based BMGs (low coercivity) | Transformer cores, sensors |
| **Formability** | Superplastic in ΔT_x region | Net-shape forming like plastics |
| **Thermal Expansion** | Often lower than crystalline counterpart | Precision instruments |
| **Dimensional Stability** | Very high (no phase changes) | Mirrors, optical parts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mechanical Pitfall — Shear Band Instability:**
> BMGs typically deform via highly localized **shear bands** (10–20 nm thick). Under unconstrained loading, a single shear band can catastrophically propagate, causing **sudden, brittle-like fracture**. This is a KTU-favorite question.

### 2.4 Applications of BMGs

1. **Consumer Electronics:** Casings (e.g., Zr-based BMG used in certain high-end smartphone cases), SIM ejector pins, hinges.
2. **Sporting Goods:** Golf club heads (Zr-based), tennis racquets, baseball bats, ski bindings.
3. **Aerospace & Defense:** Penetrator cores, kinetic-energy projectiles.
4. **Medical:** Surgical blades, implants (biocompatible Zr- and Ti-based), micro-surgical instruments.
5. **Energy & Electromagnetic:** Soft-magnetic Fe-based BMGs for distribution transformer cores (low core loss).
6. **Automotive:** Gears, springs, transmission components.
7. **Space:** NASA’s Bulk Metallic Glass Gears (gearbox in 2018 Mars InSight lander — *Vitreloy 1*).
8. **Watches & Jewelry:** Decorative BMG parts (high luster, scratch resistance).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reduced Glass Transition Temp. | $T_{rg} = T_g / T_m$ | Turnbull's GFA criterion |
| 2 | Supercooled Liquid Region | $\Delta T_x = T_x - T_g$ | Thermal stability of glass |
| 3 | Gamma Parameter (Lu & Liu) | $\gamma = T_x / (T_g + T_l)$ | Combined GFA indicator |
| 4 | Critical Cooling Rate | $R_c = (T_m - T_g) / t_n$ | Minimum rate to vitrify |
| 5 | VFT Equation | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp[D^* T_0 / (T - T_0)]$ | Viscosity of supercooled liquid |
| 6 | Atomic Size Mismatch | $\lambda = \sum c_i \left\vert 1 - r_i / \bar{r} \right\vert$ | Quantitative Inoue rule-2 indicator |
| 7 | Reduced Density (Packing) | $\rho^* = \rho_{\text{glass}} / \rho_{\text{crystal}}$ | High ρ* = better GFA |
| 8 | Elastic Strain Limit | $\varepsilon_{el} = \sigma_y / E$ | Up to ~2% in BMGs vs. ~0.2% in steels |
| 9 | Hardness (Tabor) | $H \approx 3 \sigma_y$ | Approximate relation |
| 10 | Fragility Index (Avramov–Milchev) | $m = d \log \tau / d(T_g/T)\vert_{T=T_g}$ | Slope of viscosity curve at Tg |

> [!TIP]
> **Threshold Values to Memorize (KTU favorites):**
> - T_rg ≥ 0.69 → good GFA
> - ΔT_x ≥ 50 K → good thermoplastic formability
> - γ ≥ 0.35 → good GFA
> - Atomic size mismatch ≥ 12% among major elements
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Critical Cooling Rate (R_c) from a Simplified TTT Model

We model the **TTT "nose"** as a curve where the time to crystallization, t(T), is governed by the **combined effect of nucleation and growth** rates. We assume a simplified, classical expression:

$$t(T) = \frac{a}{\exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{k_B T}\right)}$$

where:
- $\Delta G^*$ = thermodynamic barrier for nucleation (free energy of critical nucleus formation).
- $Q$ = activation energy for atomic diffusion across the liquid–crystal interface.
- $a$ = a constant with units of time.

**Step 1:** Combine the two exponentials.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t(T) &= a \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\Delta G^* + Q}{k_B T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Use the classical expression for $\Delta G^*$. For spherical nuclei of radius $r$ and interfacial energy $\gamma_{sl}$ (solid–liquid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^* &= \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{sl}^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta G_v$ is the volumetric free-energy difference between liquid and crystal.

**Step 3:** Use the **Turnbull approximation** for $\Delta G_v$ near $T_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_v &\approx \frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_m} = \frac{\Delta H_f (T_m - T)}{T_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The "nose" of the TTT curve corresponds to the **minimum value** of $t(T)$. We find the temperature $T_n$ where $dt/dT = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(\ln t)}{dT} &= -\frac{1}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\Delta G^* + Q}{k_B} = 0 \;\;\text{extremum}
\end{aligned}
$$

The extremum is found by balancing the **rising thermodynamic driving force** for nucleation against the **falling atomic mobility** as T decreases. Detailed analysis (omitted in the simplified KTU derivation) yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &\approx \frac{T_m + T_g}{2} \;\;\text{(approx. for metallic glass formers)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The time at the nose, $t_n$, is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_n &\approx a \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\Delta G^*(T_n) + Q}{k_B T_n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** The critical cooling rate is defined as the rate that just **skims** the nose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c &= \frac{T_m - T_n}{t_n} \;\;\approx\;\; \frac{T_m - T_g}{2 \, t_n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final compact expression:**

$$
\boxed{R_c \approx \frac{T_m - T_g}{2 \, a \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta G^*(T_n) + Q}{k_B T_n}\right)}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A *small* $T_m - T_g$ (i.e., *high* $T_{rg}$) and a *large* activation barrier $\Delta G^* + Q$ both push $R_c$ down. That is exactly what Inoue's three rules accomplish — they increase $\Delta G^*$ (multi-component disorder) and increase $Q$ (slow diffusion due to atomic size mismatch), thus reducing $R_c$ by many orders of magnitude.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Quantitative Atomic Size Mismatch Parameter (λ)

Inoue's second rule, qualitative in nature, can be quantified.

**Step 1:** Define the average atomic radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i \, r_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c_i$ is the atomic fraction of element $i$ and $r_i$ is its atomic radius.

**Step 2:** Define the mismatch parameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i \left\vert 1 - \frac{r_i}{\bar{r}} \right\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Example computation for a typical Zr-based BMG (e.g., Zr₆₅Cu₁₅Ni₁₀Al₁₀).

- Atomic radii (nm): Zr = 0.160, Cu = 0.128, Ni = 0.124, Al = 0.143.
- Atomic fractions: c_Zr = 0.65, c_Cu = 0.15, c_Ni = 0.10, c_Al = 0.10.

Compute $\bar{r}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} &= 0.65(0.160) + 0.15(0.128) + 0.10(0.124) + 0.10(0.143) \\
       &= 0.1040 + 0.0192 + 0.0124 + 0.0143 \\
       &= 0.1499 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\lambda$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 0.65 \left\vert 1 - \frac{0.160}{0.1499} \right\vert + 0.15 \left\vert 1 - \frac{0.128}{0.1499} \right\vert \\
       &\quad + 0.10 \left\vert 1 - \frac{0.124}{0.1499} \right\vert + 0.10 \left\vert 1 - \frac{0.143}{0.1499} \right\vert \\
       &= 0.65(0.0674) + 0.15(0.1461) + 0.10(0.1728) + 0.10(0.0460) \\
       &= 0.0438 + 0.0219 + 0.0173 + 0.0046 \\
       &= 0.0876 \;\; \text{(i.e., 8.76\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although 8.76% is below the strict 12% threshold for the *overall* mismatch, the Zr–Cu pair alone shows ~17% mismatch, which is more than sufficient. Inoue's rule refers to the **dominant pair-wise mismatches**, not just the global λ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (KTU level):** When asked to compute λ, students should:
> 1. Compute $\bar{r}$.
> 2. Compute each $|1 - r_i/\bar{r}|$ separately.
> 3. Multiply by atomic fraction and sum.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key GFA Indicators for Representative BMGs

| Alloy System | T_g (K) | T_m (K) | T_x (K) | T_rg = T_g/T_m | ΔT_x (K) | γ = T_x/(T_g + T_m) | R_c (K/s) | t_max (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pd₄₀Cu₃₀Ni₁₀P₂₀ | 576 | 800 | 678 | 0.720 | 102 | 0.492 | 0.10 | > 40 |
| Zr₄₁.₂Ti₁₃.₈Cu₁₂.₅Ni₁₀Be₂₂.₅ (Vitreloy 1) | 623 | 937 | 705 | 0.665 | 82 | 0.452 | 1.4 | ~ 25 |
| Zr₅₇Cu₁₅.₄Ni₁₂.₆Al₁₀Nb₅ | 657 | 836 | 776 | 0.786 | 119 | 0.520 | 10 | ~ 16 |
| La₅₅Al₂₅Cu₁₀Ni₅Co₅ | 466 | 700 | 555 | 0.666 | 89 | 0.476 | 90 | ~ 9 |
| Mg₆₅Cu₂₅Y₁₀ | 420 | 745 | 490 | 0.564 | 70 | 0.421 | 50 | ~ 7 |
| Cu₄₇Ti₃₃Zr₁₁Ni₈Si₁ | 696 | 1149 | 763 | 0.606 | 67 | 0.413 | 250 | ~ 5 |
| Fe₇₃Al₅Ga₂P₁₁C₅B₄ (metallic glass, not bulk) | 730 | 1230 | 780 | 0.593 | 50 | 0.398 | 10⁶ (ribbon) | < 0.1 |
| Ti₄₀Zr₂₅Be₃₀Cr₅ | 600 | 950 | 700 | 0.632 | 100 | 0.451 | 1000 | ~ 8 |

**Observations for KTU answers:**
- Higher T_rg and γ correlate with better GFA (lower R_c, higher t_max).
- Pd-based alloys are the best glass formers known.
- Fe-based alloys (very useful for magnetic applications) generally have poor bulk GFA.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Relationship between Elastic Limit and Theoretical Strength

For a perfect crystal free of defects, the **theoretical shear strength** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{th} \approx \frac{G}{2\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $G$ is the shear modulus.

For an **isotropic** amorphous solid, the theoretical tensile strength is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{th} \approx \frac{E}{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus.

**Step 1 — Express elastic limit as a strain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{el} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For a typical BMG, $\sigma_y \approx E/50$ to $E/40$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{el} &\approx \frac{1}{50} \text{ to } \frac{1}{40} \\
                &= 0.020 \text{ to } 0.025 \;\; (2\% \text{ to } 2.5\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with crystalline alloys (typical $\sigma_y \approx E/500$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{el}^{crystal} &\approx \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \;\; (0.2\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** BMGs exhibit a *10× higher elastic strain limit* than crystalline metals. This is why they are excellent for **spring** and **strain-energy storage** applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "metallic glass" with "BMG".** Early metallic glasses (1970s ribbons) are *not* bulk. Examiners specifically look for the word "**bulk**" in answers.
> 2. **Mis-stating Inoue's rules** as "any three elements" — the *number* is necessary but the *size mismatch* and *negative heat of mixing* are equally critical.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in GFA parameters (T_rg is dimensionless, ΔT_x is in K).
> 4. **Confusing T_g with T_m** in the reduced glass transition temperature.
> 5. **Skipping the role of TTT diagrams** when explaining mechanism of formation — this is the **most common 2-mark loss** in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Not mentioning shear-band limitation** when describing mechanical properties — a frequent 1-mark loss in 3-mark questions.

---

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Bulk Metallic Glass (BMG). What is the minimum critical dimension commonly accepted to qualify a metallic glass as "bulk"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Bulk Metallic Glass (BMG)** is a multi-component metallic alloy that can be solidified into a fully amorphous (non-crystalline) state with a thickness or diameter of at least **1 mm** at practically achievable cooling rates (typically < 1000 K/s, often as low as 1 K/s). It is distinguished from conventional metallic glasses by its ability to vitrify in **macroscopic dimensions** due to its high glass-forming ability (GFA), typically achieved by following **Inoue's three empirical rules** (multi-component, atomic size mismatch ≥ 12%, and negative heat of mixing). The 1 mm threshold is the conventional engineering benchmark separating "ribbon" metallic glasses (typically < 50 µm) from "bulk" metallic glasses.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]**
**State and briefly explain the reduced glass transition temperature (T_rg) and its significance in evaluating glass-forming ability (GFA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **reduced glass transition temperature** is defined as the ratio of the glass transition temperature (T_g) to the melting temperature (T_m) of the alloy:

$$T_{rg} = \frac{T_g}{T_m}$$

It was proposed by **David Turnbull** as a key indicator of glass-forming ability. Significance:
- A **higher T_rg** implies a smaller temperature window (T_m − T_g) over which crystallization can occur, hence **easier vitrification**.
- Empirically, alloys with **T_rg ≥ 2/3 (≈ 0.67)** generally show good GFA. For example, Pd₄₀Cu₃₀Ni₁₀P₂₀ has T_rg ≈ 0.72, while conventional Fe-based metallic glasses have T_rg ≈ 0.59, explaining why the former is a bulk glass and the latter is not.
- T_rg is inversely related to the **critical cooling rate (R_c)**: higher T_rg ⇒ lower R_c.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, and the student must answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" as a whole (not mix sub-parts between the two).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of formation** of Bulk Metallic Glasses, with the help of a **Time–Temperature–Transformation (TTT) diagram**. Clearly state the role of the **critical cooling rate (R_c)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain **Inoue's three empirical rules** for the design of bulk metallic glass-forming alloys, with one example alloy system for each rule. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Mechanism of Formation (7 marks):**

The formation of a BMG is fundamentally a **kinetic competition** between **vitrification** (freezing of the liquid into an amorphous structure) and **crystallization** (nucleation and growth of crystalline phases).

**Step 1 — TTT Diagram Concept (2 marks):**
The TTT diagram plots the **time to crystallization** versus **temperature** for a supercooled liquid. It has a characteristic **C-shape**. The shortest time for crystallization to begin is at the **nose** of the C-curve, denoted by a time t_n and a temperature T_n (typically between T_g and T_m).

**Step 2 — Role of Critical Cooling Rate (2 marks):**
The **critical cooling rate (R_c)** is the **minimum cooling rate** required to bypass the nose of the TTT curve without intersecting it. Mathematically:

$$R_c = \frac{T_m - T_n}{t_n} \approx \frac{T_m - T_g}{2 t_n}$$

- If the actual cooling rate **R > R_c**, vitrification occurs → amorphous BMG.
- If **R < R_c**, the cooling curve intersects the C-curve → crystallization occurs.

**Step 3 — Strategies to Lower R_c (2 marks):**
Inoue-type multi-component alloys push the C-curve "nose" to longer times (larger t_n) by:
- Increasing the **thermodynamic barrier** to nucleation (multi-component system, ΔG* increases).
- Increasing the **kinetic barrier** to growth (large atomic size mismatch lowers atomic diffusivity).
- This results in **R_c dropping from ~10⁶ K/s** (early metallic glasses) to **~1 K/s** (best BMGs).

**Step 4 — Vitrification (1 mark):**
When the cooling curve bypasses the nose, atoms do not have time to rearrange into a periodic lattice. The **liquid structure is "frozen in"** at T_g, producing an amorphous solid — a BMG.

**(b) Inoue's Three Empirical Rules (7 marks):**

**Rule 1 — Multi-component systems (≥ 3 elements) (2 marks):**
The alloy must contain **three or more** elements. This increases the **configurational entropy of mixing** in the liquid state and decreases it in any potential crystalline compound, raising the **free energy of competing crystalline phases** and thus the **driving force barrier** ΔG*.
- *Example:* **Zr–Cu–Ni–Al** quaternary system (Vitreloy family).

**Rule 2 — Significant atomic size mismatch (≥ 12%) (2 marks):**
The atomic radii of the main constituent elements must differ by **at least 12%**. This frustrates efficient crystalline packing, increases the **interfacial energy** γ_sl, and reduces atomic mobility, hence suppressing both nucleation and growth.
- *Example:* In **Mg–Cu–Y**, atomic radii are Mg = 0.160 nm, Cu = 0.128 nm, Y = 0.180 nm — Y/Mg mismatch ≈ 12.5%, Mg/Cu mismatch ≈ 25%.

**Rule 3 — Large negative heat of mixing among main elements (2 marks):**
The major components should have **large negative ΔH_mix**, indicating strong chemical affinity. This produces **deep eutectics** in the phase diagram, lowering T_m relative to T_g, widening ΔT_x, and stabilizing the supercooled liquid.
- *Example:* In **Zr–Be**, ΔH_mix ≈ −43 kJ/mol, leading to a deep eutectic and excellent GFA in Zr–Ti–Cu–Ni–Be (Vitreloy 1).

**Synergistic Outcome (1 mark):**
All three rules together dramatically reduce R_c, enabling the production of **bulk amorphous samples** in mm-to-cm sizes at low cooling rates.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** List the **key engineering properties** of bulk metallic glasses, and explain why BMGs exhibit a much higher elastic strain limit than conventional crystalline alloys. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss at least **four major applications** of bulk metallic glasses, with a brief engineering justification for each selection. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Key Properties of BMGs (7 marks):**

**1. Mechanical Properties (3 marks):**
- **High yield strength** (often 1.5–4× that of crystalline counterparts). For example, Vitreloy 1 (Zr-based) has σ_y ≈ 1.9 GPa vs. ~0.8 GPa for crystalline Zr alloys.
- **Very high elastic strain limit** — typically **~2%** (vs. ~0.2% for crystalline metals), because BMGs have **no dislocations** to initiate plastic flow at low stresses.
- **High Vickers hardness** (500–1500 HV) due to absence of easy slip systems.
- **Fracture behavior:** Often brittle in tension due to adiabatic shear-band localization; under confinement, multiple shear bands form, giving high toughness.

**2. Chemical Properties (1 mark):**
- Excellent **corrosion resistance** because there are **no grain boundaries, no dislocations, and no second-phase precipitates** to act as localized corrosion sites. Passive oxide films are more uniform.

**3. Physical Properties (1 mark):**
- Many BMGs (especially Fe-based) exhibit **soft magnetic behavior** with very low coercivity and high permeability — used in transformer cores.
- **Lower thermal expansion** coefficient than crystalline counterparts.

**4. Processing Property (1 mark):**
- In the **supercooled liquid region (ΔT_x = T_x − T_g)**, the alloy behaves as a **viscous superplastic material** that can be formed like a polymer — this enables net-shape manufacturing of micro-parts.

**5. Why Higher Elastic Limit (1 mark):**
In a crystal, plastic deformation begins at a tiny fraction of the theoretical strength because dislocations nucleate and glide easily. In a BMG, **there are no dislocations, no grain boundaries, and no slip planes** — yielding must occur by **collective atomic rearrangement** across an entire shear band, which requires stresses much closer to the **theoretical strength** (~E/50). Hence σ_y/E ≈ 0.02 (2%) vs. ~0.002 (0.2%) in crystals.

**(b) Major Applications of BMGs (7 marks):**

**1. Sporting Goods — Golf Club Heads (1.5 marks):**
BMG inserts in golf club heads redistribute ~99% of impact energy to the ball (vs. ~70% for crystalline Ti alloys), giving greater distance. The high elastic limit allows the face to "spring back" efficiently. Used in Callaway's "Full Metal Jacket" and other premium clubs.

**2. Consumer Electronics — Watch Cases and Phone Parts (1.5 marks):**
Zr- and Cu-based BMGs have a **mirror-like luster**, high scratch resistance (HV > 500), and can be cast into complex shapes by thermoplastic forming. Used in luxury watch cases and premium smartphone components.

**3. Aerospace — Penetrators and Gears (1.5 marks):**
W-reinforced BMG penetrators self-sharpen on impact (shear-band instabilities), outperforming depleted uranium. NASA's Mars Insight lander (2018) used **Vitreloy 1 BMG gears** in its seismometer, eliminating the need for lubrication and providing low wear.

**4. Medical — Surgical Blades and Implants (1.5 marks):**
Zr- and Ti-based BMGs are **biocompatible**, with sharp, durable edges (used in ophthalmic and micro-surgical scalpels), and excellent corrosion resistance in body fluids (orthopedic implants).

**5. Energy — Fe-based Soft-Magnetic BMG Transformer Cores (1 mark):**
Fe-Si-B-P-C amorphous ribbons (e.g., Metglas 2605SA1) have **core losses ~1/5** of crystalline silicon steel, dramatically reducing transformer heating and saving energy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** BMG = multi-component, fully amorphous metallic alloy with critical dimension ≥ 1 mm.
- **Mechanism:** Vitrification by **bypassing the TTT "nose"** at cooling rate R ≥ R_c.
- **Critical cooling rate (R_c):** Inversely related to glass-forming ability; for Pd-based BMGs, R_c ≈ 0.1 K/s; for Fe-based glasses, R_c ≈ 10⁶ K/s.
- **Turnbull's criterion:** T_rg = T_g / T_m ≥ 2/3 ⇒ good GFA.
- **Supercooled liquid region:** ΔT_x = T_x − T_g; ΔT_x > 50 K is desirable for thermoplastic forming.
- **Gamma parameter:** γ = T_x / (T_g + T_l); γ > 0.35 ⇒ good GFA.
- **Inoue's Three Rules (3M's):**
  1. **Many** components (≥ 3).
  2. **Mismatched** atomic sizes (≥ 12%).
  3. **Mutual** chemical affinity (negative ΔH_mix).
- **VFT equation:** η(T) = η₀ exp[D* T₀ / (T − T₀)] — diverges as T → T_g.
- **Properties to memorize:**
  - σ_y ≈ E/50 (vs. E/500 for crystals).
  - Elastic strain limit ≈ 2% (vs. 0.2% for crystals).
  - HV typically 500–1500.
  - Brittle in unconstrained tension due to shear-band localization.
- **Applications to memorize:** Sporting goods, electronics casings, magnetic cores, surgical tools, aerospace gears/penetrators, biomedical implants.
- **Landmark systems to know:** Pd-Cu-Ni-P, Zr-Ti-Cu-Ni-Be (Vitreloy 1), La-Al-Cu-Ni-Co, Mg-Cu-Y, Cu-Zr-Al, Fe-Si-B-P-C.
- **Best GFA system overall:** Pd-based (Vit1 series with P addition) — also the first to reach cm-scale amorphous casting.
- **Hallmark advantage over crystals:** Combination of metallic conductivity (in some BMGs) with glassy formability, no grain boundaries, and high elastic resilience.
- **Shear-band pitfall:** Always mention the **shear-band mechanism** when discussing BMG mechanical behavior — it is the single most KTU-favored explanation point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Entropy Alloys (HEA): Thermodynamics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High Entropy Alloys (HEAs)** are a revolutionary class of advanced metallic materials first conceptualized and defined by **Jien-Wei Yeh (Yeh et al., 2004, Advanced Engineering Materials)**. According to the formal KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET635 - Advanced Materials) definition, HEAs are defined based on **two complementary criteria**:

### Criterion 1: Composition-Based Definition
> A multi-component alloy containing **five or more principal elements**, each with an atomic (molar) concentration between **5% and 35%**.

### Criterion 2: Entropy-Based Definition
> A multi-component alloy whose **configurational entropy (ΔS_config) in the liquid or random solid solution state is greater than or equal to 1.5 R**, where R is the **Universal Gas Constant (8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Definition (Must Memorize):** HEAs are alloys with ≥ 5 principal elements in 5-35 at.% each, possessing a configurational entropy ΔS_config ≥ 1.5 R ≈ 12.57 J/(mol·K).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **cocktail party** instead of a traditional family dinner:
- **Traditional Alloy (e.g., steel, brass):** One "host" element (Iron or Copper) dominates, with 2-3 minor guests (C, Cr, Zn). The host sets all the rules.
- **High Entropy Alloy:** **5-5-5-5-5 guests** of equal status arrive. No one dominates, so they all blend into a unique, surprisingly stable, and uniform "party solution."

**Why the name "High Entropy"?** In thermodynamics, **entropy = disorder/randomness**. By mixing 5+ elements in near-equal amounts, we **maximize the disorder** at the atomic lattice positions. Counter-intuitively, this high disorder **stabilizes** simple solid solution phases (FCC, BCC, HCP) and **suppresses** the formation of brittle intermetallic compounds.

### 1.3 The Genesis of HEA Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **2004:** Two independent papers (Yeh from Taiwan and Cantor from UK) introduced the concept.
> - **2014:** Cantor renamed the field with the "Cantor Alloy" - Fe₂₀Cr₂₀Mn₂₀Co₂₀Ni₂₀ (the most studied HEA).
> - **2010s-Present:** HEAs shifted from laboratory curiosity to structural, cryogenic, and nuclear applications.

### 1.4 The Four Signature "Core Effects" of HEAs

| # | Core Effect | Physical Meaning |
|---|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **High Entropy Effect** | Maximizes ΔS_config, stabilizing simple solid solutions over intermetallics |
| 2 | **Severe Lattice Distortion** | Atomic size mismatch distorts the crystal lattice, strengthening the alloy |
| 3 | **Sluggish Diffusion Kinetics** | Atomic diffusion becomes slow, enhancing thermal stability and creep resistance |
| 4 | **Cocktail Effect** | Unexpected synergistic properties arising from multi-element interactions |

### 1.5 Classification of HEAs

HEAs are classified into three generations based on elemental design philosophy:

1. **First-Generation (Cantor-type):** Equiatomic 3d-transition metal HEAs (e.g., CoCrFeMnNi).
2. **Second-Generation (Refractory HEAs):** High-melting point elements like W, Mo, Ta, Nb, V (e.g., WMoTaNbV).
3. **Third-Generation (Lightweight/Functionally-Graded HEAs):** Incorporating Al, Mg, Ti, Si for low-density aerospace applications.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations of HEA Stability

The stability of a phase in any alloy is governed by the **Gibbs Free Energy Equation**:

$$G_{mix} = H_{mix} - T \cdot S_{mix}$$

For an HEA to form a stable solid solution, the **free energy of the solid solution must be lower** than that of competing intermetallic phases. This requires a **highly negative ΔG_mix**, achieved through the **large negative T·ΔS_mix term** from the high configurational entropy.

### 2.2 Configurational Entropy of Mixing (ΔS_config)

For a general **n-component** alloy with molar fractions $x_i$:

$$\Delta S_{config} = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \ln x_i$$

For the special case of an **equiatomic** n-component alloy ($x_i = 1/n$):

$$\Delta S_{config}^{equiatomic} = R \ln(n)$$

### 2.3 Enthalpy of Mixing (ΔH_mix)

The enthalpy of mixing in a multi-component system is calculated using the **pair-wise regular solution model**:

$$\Delta H_{mix} = \sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} 4 \Delta H_{ij}^{mix} \cdot x_i \cdot x_j$$

where $\Delta H_{ij}^{mix}$ is the **binary mixing enthalpy** between elements i and j (obtained from Miedema's model).

### 2.4 Atomic Size Difference Parameter (δ)

This parameter quantifies the **lattice distortion** due to atomic size mismatch:

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \left(1 - \frac{r_i}{\bar{r}}\right)^2}$$

where $\bar{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i r_i$ is the average atomic radius, and $c_i$ is the atomic percentage of element $i$.

### 2.5 The Ω Parameter (Thermodynamic Geometric Factor)

The Ω parameter (proposed by **Yang & Zhang, 2012**) predicts solid solution formation by comparing entropic contribution at melting temperature vs. enthalpic penalty:

$$\Omega = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta S_{mix}}{\vert \Delta H_{mix} \vert}$$

where $T_m = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i (T_m)_i$ is the **rule-of-mixtures melting temperature**.

### 2.6 Valence Electron Concentration (VEC)

VEC determines the **type of solid solution phase** (BCC vs. FCC vs. mixed):

$$VEC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \cdot (VEC)_i$$

### 2.7 Electronegativity Difference (Δχ)

$$\Delta \chi = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \left(\chi_i - \bar{\chi}\right)^2}$$

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Empirical Range for Solid Solution** | **Physical Interpretation** |
|---------------|-------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| $\Delta S_{config}$ | $-R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | $\geq 1.5 R$ (HEA criterion) | Atomic disorder at lattice sites |
| $\Delta H_{mix}$ | $\sum 4 \Delta H_{ij}^{mix} x_i x_j$ | $-20$ to $+5$ kJ/mol | Enthalpic penalty for mixing |
| $\delta$ | $100 \sqrt{\sum c_i (1 - r_i/\bar{r})^2}$ | $\leq 6.6\%$ | Lattice distortion level |
| $\Omega$ | $T_m \Delta S_{mix} / \vert \Delta H_{mix} \vert$ | $\geq 1.1$ | Entropy vs. Enthalpy dominance |
| VEC | $\sum c_i (VEC)_i$ | $< 6.87 \rightarrow$ BCC ; $\geq 8 \rightarrow$ FCC | Phase selector (BCC vs. FCC) |
| $\Delta \chi$ | $\sqrt{\sum c_i (\chi_i - \bar{\chi})^2}$ | Small values preferred | Chemical compatibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **two simultaneous conditions** for solid solution formation: $\Omega \geq 1.1$ **AND** $\delta \leq 6.6\%$. If only one is satisfied, the alloy tends to form intermetallics or amorphous phases.

### 2.9 Phase Formation Rules (Zhang's Empirical Criteria)

| **Phase Formed** | **ΔH_mix (kJ/mol)** | **δ (%)** | **Ω** | **VEC** |
|------------------|----------------------|------------|--------|----------|
| **Solid Solution (SS)** | $-20$ to $+5$ | $0 - 6.6$ | $\geq 1.1$ | $< 6.87$ (BCC) or $\geq 8$ (FCC) |
| **Intermetallic + SS** | $< -20$ or $> +5$ | $> 6.6$ | $< 1.1$ | $6.87$ to $8.0$ |
| **Bulk Metallic Glass** | $< -20$ | $> 6.6$ | $< 1.1$ | - |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These criteria are used by materials engineers to **screening-design new HEA compositions** (e.g., for nuclear reactor cladding, jet engine turbine blades, cryogenic tanks) before any expensive experimental trial.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Configurational Entropy from Boltzmann's Equation

### Starting Point: Boltzmann's Fundamental Equation

The **configurational entropy** originates from statistical mechanics via Ludwig Boltzmann's entropy equation:

$$S = k_B \ln(W)$$

where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** ($1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) and $W$ is the **number of microstates** (atomic arrangements).

### Step 1: Calculate the Number of Microstates (W)

For a crystal lattice containing **N total atomic sites** occupied by **n distinct elements** with $N_i$ atoms of element $i$ each, the number of distinguishable arrangements is:

$$W = \frac{N!}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i!}$$

**Explanation:** We divide the factorial of total atoms by the product of factorials of each element's occupancy because **atoms of the same element are indistinguishable**.

### Step 2: Convert to Molar Quantities

Let $N = N_A$ (Avogadro's number, $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol) for 1 mole of alloy. The molar fraction of element $i$ is $x_i = N_i / N_A$.

Substituting into Boltzmann's equation:

$$S_{config} = k_B \ln \left( \frac{N_A!}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i!} \right)$$

### Step 3: Apply Stirling's Approximation

For large N, we use **Stirling's approximation**: $\ln(N!) \approx N \ln(N) - N$. Applying this to both numerator and denominator:

$$S_{config} = k_B \left[ N_A \ln(N_A) - N_A - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( N_i \ln(N_i) - N_i \right) \right]$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

The $-N_A$ and $+\sum N_i = N_A$ terms **cancel** because $\sum N_i = N_A$:

$$S_{config} = k_B \left[ N_A \ln(N_A) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \ln(N_i) \right]$$

### Step 5: Factor Out and Convert to Mole Fraction

$$S_{config} = k_B N_A \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( -x_i \ln \left( \frac{N_i}{N_A} \right) \right) = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \ln(x_i)$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{config} = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \ln x_i}$$

where $R = k_B N_A = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

### Step 6: Special Case - Equiatomic Alloy

For $x_i = 1/n$ for all $i$:

$$\Delta S_{config} = -R \cdot n \cdot \frac{1}{n} \ln\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) = R \ln(n)$$

### Worked Numerical Example: Entropy of Cantor Alloy

For **CoCrFeMnNi** (5 elements, equimolar, $x_i = 0.2$):

$$\Delta S_{config} = -R \cdot 5 \cdot (0.2) \cdot \ln(0.2) = -8.314 \cdot 1.0 \cdot (-1.6094)$$

$$\Delta S_{config} = 13.38 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \approx 1.61 R$$

This is **above the 1.5R HEA threshold**, confirming Cantor alloy as a true HEA.

## 3.2 Comparative Entropy Analysis: HEA vs. Conventional Alloys

| **Alloy Type** | **Composition Example** | **n** | **ΔS_config** | **Ratio to R** |
|----------------|--------------------------|-------|---------------|----------------|
| Pure Element | Pure Fe | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| Traditional Alloy | Steel (Fe + 0.8% C) | 2 | $\approx 0.05 R$ | 0.05 R |
| Medium-Entropy Alloy (MEA) | CoCrNi (3 elements) | 3 | $R \ln 3 = 1.099 R$ | 1.10 R |
| High-Entropy Alloy (HEA) | CoCrFeMnNi (5 elements) | 5 | $R \ln 5 = 1.609 R$ | 1.61 R |
| Maximally-Disordered HEA | 10 elements equiatomic | 10 | $R \ln 10 = 2.303 R$ | 2.30 R |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The jump from MEA to HEA gives an entropy boost of $\Delta S = R \ln(5/3) \approx 0.51 R$ ≈ **47% increase in disorder** for just 2 additional elements. Beyond 5-7 elements, the marginal entropy gain diminishes (logarithmic saturation).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing δ and Ω for Cantor Alloy

**Given Data (typical values):**

| Element | Atomic Radius $r_i$ (pm) | $T_m$ (K) | VEC |
|---------|---------------------------|------------|-----|
| Co      | 125                       | 1768       | 9   |
| Cr      | 128                       | 2180       | 6   |
| Fe      | 126                       | 1811       | 8   |
| Mn      | 127                       | 1519       | 7   |
| Ni      | 124                       | 1728       | 10  |

### Step A: Average Atomic Radius

$$\bar{r} = \frac{125 + 128 + 126 + 127 + 124}{5} = \frac{630}{5} = 126 \text{ pm}$$

### Step B: Compute δ Parameter

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \left[ (1-125/126)^2 + (1-128/126)^2 + (1-126/126)^2 + (1-127/126)^2 + (1-124/126)^2 \right]}$$

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{0.2 \left[ 0.000063 + 0.000254 + 0.0 + 0.000063 + 0.000254 \right]}$$

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{0.2 \times 0.000634} = 100 \times \sqrt{0.0001268} = 100 \times 0.01126$$

$$\delta \approx 1.13\%$$

This is **well below 6.6%**, satisfying the solid solution criterion.

### Step C: Compute T_m (Rule of Mixtures)

$$T_m = \frac{1768 + 2180 + 1811 + 1519 + 1728}{5} = \frac{9006}{5} = 1801.2 \text{ K}$$

### Step D: Compute ΔH_mix

Using binary mixing enthalpies (kJ/mol) from Miedema's model:
- Co-Cr: $-4$ ; Co-Fe: $-1$ ; Co-Mn: $-5$ ; Co-Ni: $0$
- Cr-Fe: $-1$ ; Cr-Mn: $2$ ; Cr-Ni: $-7$
- Fe-Mn: $0$ ; Fe-Ni: $-2$
- Mn-Ni: $-8$

$$\Delta H_{mix} = 4 \sum_{i<j} \Delta H_{ij}^{mix} \cdot x_i \cdot x_j = 4 \times 0.04 \times \sum_{i<j} \Delta H_{ij}^{mix}$$

(Here $x_i \cdot x_j = 0.2 \times 0.2 = 0.04$, and there are 10 pairs for 5 elements)

$$\sum \Delta H_{ij}^{mix} = (-4) + (-1) + (-5) + 0 + (-1) + 2 + (-7) + 0 + (-2) + (-8) = -26 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_{mix} = 4 \times 0.04 \times (-26) = -4.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Step E: Compute Ω Parameter

$$\Omega = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta S_{mix}}{\vert \Delta H_{mix} \vert} = \frac{1801.2 \times 13.38}{4160} = \frac{24100}{4160} \approx 5.79$$

This is **far above 1.1**, confirming Cantor alloy forms a stable solid solution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phase Prediction Using VEC

| **VEC Range** | **Predicted Phase(s)** | **Representative HEA Example** |
|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| $< 6.87$ | Single-phase BCC | AlCoCrFeNi (VEC ≈ 6.6) |
| $6.87 \leq VEC < 8$ | Mixed BCC + FCC | CoCrFeMnNi (VEC ≈ 7.8) |
| $\geq 8$ | Single-phase FCC | CoFeNi (VEC ≈ 8.67) |

**For Cantor Alloy:**

$$VEC = \frac{9 + 6 + 8 + 7 + 10}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0$$

This places Cantor alloy **exactly at the BCC+FCC transition boundary**, which is why experimentally it shows an FCC structure (favored at room temperature) but BCC nucleation at high temperatures.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define High Entropy Alloys (HEAs) using both composition-based and entropy-based criteria. State the numerical value of the configurational entropy threshold that distinguishes HEAs from conventional alloys.**

**Model Answer:**

High Entropy Alloys (HEAs) are defined by two complementary criteria:

**(a) Composition-Based Criterion (Yeh, 2004):**
An alloy containing **five or more principal elements**, each present in a concentration ranging from **5 at.% to 35 at.%**.

**(b) Entropy-Based Criterion:**
An alloy whose **configurational entropy in a disordered state satisfies:**

$$\Delta S_{config} \geq 1.5 R$$

where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Numerically, $1.5R \approx 12.57$ J/(mol·K).

This threshold arises because for an equiatomic **4-component alloy**, $\Delta S_{config} = R \ln 4 \approx 1.386 R$ (just below 1.5R), whereas for **5 elements**, $R \ln 5 \approx 1.609 R$ (above 1.5R).

**Key Examples:** Cantor alloy CoCrFeMnNi (ΔS_config = 1.609 R).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: List the "Four Core Effects" that distinguish HEAs from conventional alloys. Briefly explain the "High Entropy Effect."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Four Core Effects** of HEAs (Jien-Wei Yeh) are:

1. **High Entropy Effect:** Maximizes $\Delta S_{config}$, stabilizing simple solid solutions.
2. **Severe Lattice Distortion:** Multi-element atomic size mismatch creates a distorted lattice.
3. **Sluggish Diffusion:** Complex local environments slow down atomic diffusion kinetics.
4. **Cocktail Effect:** Emergent properties from multi-element synergy beyond rule-of-mixtures predictions.

**Explanation of High Entropy Effect:**
The **Gibbs free energy** of mixing is $\Delta G_{mix} = \Delta H_{mix} - T \Delta S_{mix}$. For HEAs, the very large $T \Delta S_{config}$ term dominates at high temperatures, making $\Delta G_{mix}$ **highly negative for disordered solid solutions**. This thermodynamically **favors the formation of simple solid solution phases** (FCC, BCC, HCP) and **suppresses the formation of ordered intermetallic compounds** that would otherwise form in a complex multi-component system.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the configurational entropy of mixing ($\Delta S_{config}$) for an n-component alloy starting from Boltzmann's entropy equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the $\Delta S_{config}$ for the Cantor alloy CoCrFeMnNi and verify whether it qualifies as an HEA. Compare its entropy with a 3-component equiatomic medium-entropy alloy (MEA) like CoCrNi. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) - Derivation:**

The derivation starts from Boltzmann's statistical mechanics equation:

$$S = k_B \ln(W)$$

where $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant and $W$ = number of microstates.

**Step 1:** For N total atomic sites distributed among n elements, the number of microstates is:

$$W = \frac{N!}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i!}$$

**Step 2:** Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln(W) = \ln(N!) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln(N_i!)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stirling's approximation $\ln(N!) \approx N \ln N - N$:

$$\ln(W) = N \ln N - N - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i \ln N_i - N_i)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using $\sum N_i = N$:

$$\ln(W) = N \ln N - \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \ln N_i$$

**Step 5:** Convert to molar quantities ($N = N_A$ for 1 mole):

$$S_{config} = k_B N_A \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( -\frac{N_i}{N_A} \ln \frac{N_i}{N_A} \right) = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \ln x_i$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{config} = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \ln x_i}$$

**Part (b) - Numerical Computation:**

**For Cantor Alloy (CoCrFeMnNi):** 5 elements, equiatomic ($x_i = 0.2$):

$$\Delta S_{config}^{Cantor} = -R \cdot 5 \cdot (0.2) \cdot \ln(0.2) = -8.314 \cdot 1.0 \cdot (-1.6094)$$

$$\Delta S_{config}^{Cantor} = 13.38 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 1.609 R$$

**Verification:** Since $1.609 R \geq 1.5 R$, Cantor alloy **qualifies as an HEA**. ✓

**For MEA CoCrNi:** 3 elements, equiatomic ($x_i = 1/3$):

$$\Delta S_{config}^{MEA} = -R \cdot 3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -8.314 \cdot 1.0 \cdot (-1.0986)$$

$$\Delta S_{config}^{MEA} = 9.134 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 1.099 R$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Alloy | n | $\Delta S_{config}$ (J/mol·K) | Ratio to R | HEA Status |
|-------|---|-------------------------------|------------|------------|
| CoCrNi (MEA) | 3 | 9.13 | 1.099 R | Below 1.5R threshold |
| CoCrFeMnNi (HEA) | 5 | 13.38 | 1.609 R | Above 1.5R threshold ✓ |

**Conclusion:** Going from 3 to 5 equiatomic elements increases $\Delta S_{config}$ by $\approx 46.5\%$, pushing the alloy across the HEA definition boundary.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Ω parameter** and the **atomic size difference parameter (δ)** used for predicting solid solution formation in HEAs. State the empirical thresholds for stable solid solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the data below, compute δ and Ω for the equiatomic alloy AlCoCrFeNi and predict the resulting phase. **Given:** $r_{Al} = 143$ pm, $r_{Co} = 125$ pm, $r_{Cr} = 128$ pm, $r_{Fe} = 126$ pm, $r_{Ni} = 124$ pm. $T_m$ values: Al = 933 K, Co = 1768 K, Cr = 2180 K, Fe = 1811 K, Ni = 1728 K. $\Delta H_{mix} = -12.5$ kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) - Theoretical Explanation:**

**Atomic Size Difference Parameter (δ):** This parameter quantifies the lattice distortion arising from atomic size mismatch in a multi-component alloy. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \left(1 - \frac{r_i}{\bar{r}}\right)^2}$$

A small δ means atoms have similar sizes, leading to low lattice strain and easier solid solution formation. **Empirical threshold for solid solution: δ ≤ 6.6%.**

**Ω Parameter:** This parameter compares the **entropic driving force** (which favors solid solution) with the **enthalpic penalty** (which favors phase separation or intermetallics). It is defined as:

$$\Omega = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta S_{mix}}{\vert \Delta H_{mix} \vert}$$

A high Ω means the entropic term $T_m \Delta S_{mix}$ dominates, stabilizing the solid solution. **Empirical threshold for solid solution: Ω ≥ 1.1.**

**Joint Criterion (Zhang, 2008):** For a stable disordered solid solution, **both** conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

$$\delta \leq 6.6\% \quad \text{AND} \quad \Omega \geq 1.1$$

**Part (b) - Numerical Computation for AlCoCrFeNi:**

**Step 1: Compute Average Atomic Radius**

$$\bar{r} = \frac{143 + 125 + 128 + 126 + 124}{5} = \frac{646}{5} = 129.2 \text{ pm}$$

**Step 2: Compute δ**

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\left[\left(1-\frac{143}{129.2}\right)^2 + \left(1-\frac{125}{129.2}\right)^2 + \left(1-\frac{128}{129.2}\right)^2 + \left(1-\frac{126}{129.2}\right)^2 + \left(1-\frac{124}{129.2}\right)^2\right]}$$

Computing individual squared terms:
- Al: $(1 - 1.1068)^2 = (-0.1068)^2 = 0.01141$
- Co: $(1 - 0.9675)^2 = (0.0325)^2 = 0.001056$
- Cr: $(1 - 0.9907)^2 = (0.0093)^2 = 0.0000865$
- Fe: $(1 - 0.9752)^2 = (0.0248)^2 = 0.000615$
- Ni: $(1 - 0.9598)^2 = (0.0402)^2 = 0.001616$

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{0.2 \times (0.01141 + 0.001056 + 0.0000865 + 0.000615 + 0.001616)}$$

$$\delta = 100 \times \sqrt{0.2 \times 0.014784} = 100 \times \sqrt{0.002957}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 100 \times 0.05438 \approx 5.44\%}$$

**Step 3: Compute T_m (Rule of Mixtures)**

$$T_m = \frac{933 + 1768 + 2180 + 1811 + 1728}{5} = \frac{8420}{5} = 1684 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\Delta S_{config}$**

For 5 equiatomic elements:

$$\Delta S_{config} = R \ln 5 = 8.314 \times 1.6094 = 13.38 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 0.01338 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5: Compute Ω**

$$\Omega = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta S_{config}}{\vert \Delta H_{mix} \vert} = \frac{1684 \times 0.01338}{12.5} = \frac{22.53}{12.5}$$

$$\boxed{\Omega \approx 1.80}$$

**Step 6: Phase Prediction**

| Parameter | Computed Value | Solid Solution Criterion | Verdict |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|---------|
| δ | 5.44% | ≤ 6.6% | ✓ Satisfied |
| Ω | 1.80 | ≥ 1.1 | ✓ Satisfied |

**Conclusion:** Both criteria are satisfied. **AlCoCrFeNi will form a stable disordered solid solution.** Additionally, computing VEC:

$$VEC = \frac{3 + 9 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = \frac{36}{5} = 7.2$$

Since $6.87 \leq VEC = 7.2 < 8$, the predicted phase is a **mixed BCC + FCC structure**, which matches experimental observations for this alloy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning - Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Many students forget to multiply δ by 100, leading to wrong decimal-point results.
> 2. **Forgetting the AND Condition:** Students often state only one criterion (Ω OR δ) instead of **both** required simultaneously. This loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Molar vs. Atomic Fractions:** In the derivation, students confuse $x_i$ (mole fraction) with $c_i$ (atomic %). For equiatomic alloys, both are 1/n, but the distinction matters generally.
> 4. **Sign Convention for ΔH_mix:** Always quote units (kJ/mol) along with the sign. Negative ΔH_mix means attractive interactions.
> 5. **VEC Boundary Conditions:** Memorize exactly: VEC < 6.87 → BCC, 6.87 ≤ VEC < 8 → mixed, VEC ≥ 8 → FCC. Boundary values like 6.87, 8.0 are tested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision - Memorize These:**

- [x] **HEA Definition:** ≥ 5 elements, 5-35 at.%, ΔS_config ≥ 1.5R.
- [x] **Equiatomic ΔS_config:** R ln(n); Cantor alloy = R ln 5 = 1.609 R.
- [x] **General ΔS_config:** -R Σ x_i ln x_i (memorize the derivation steps).
- [x] **Four Core Effects:** High Entropy, Severe Lattice Distortion, Sluggish Diffusion, Cocktail Effect.
- [x] **δ Formula:** 100 × √[Σ c_i (1 - r_i/r̄)²], threshold ≤ 6.6%.
- [x] **Ω Formula:** T_m ΔS_mix / |ΔH_mix|, threshold ≥ 1.1.
- [x] **Joint Criterion:** Both δ ≤ 6.6% AND Ω ≥ 1.1 must be satisfied.
- [x] **VEC Boundaries:** < 6.87 → BCC; 6.87-8 → mixed; ≥ 8 → FCC.
- [x] **ΔH_mix Range for SS:** -20 to +5 kJ/mol.
- [x] **Cantor Alloy:** CoCrFeMnNi (VEC = 8.0, FCC dominant at room T).
- [x] **Refractory HEAs:** W, Mo, Ta, Nb, V - for high-temperature applications.
- [x] **Synthesis Methods:** Arc melting (most common), induction melting, mechanical alloying, sputtering, additive manufacturing (SLM/LPBF).
- [x] **Key Applications:** Cryogenic storage (LNG tanks), aerospace turbine blades, nuclear reactor cladding, soft magnetic cores, hydrogen storage.
- [x] **Yeh (2004) and Cantor (2004):** Co-discoverers of HEA concept.
- [x] **Gibbs Free Energy Criterion:** Large T·ΔS_config makes ΔG_mix highly negative, stabilizing SS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core Effects of HEA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core Effects of HEA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are High Entropy Alloys (HEAs)?

High Entropy Alloys (HEAs), also known as **Multi-Principal Element Alloys (MPEAs)** or **Complex Concentrated Alloys (CCAs)**, are a revolutionary class of metallic materials that contain **five or more principal elements** in equiatomic or near-equiatomic concentrations, each typically ranging between **5 at.% and 35 at.%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An HEA is defined as an alloy containing at least **5 principal elements** with each elemental concentration lying between **5% and 35%**, possessing a **configurational entropy (ΔS_mix) ≥ 1.5 R** in the ideal random solid solution state, where **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the universal gas constant.

The concept was independently proposed in **2004** by:
- **B. Cantor** (University of Cambridge) — experimentally demonstrated multi-component alloys
- **J.W. Yeh** (National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan) — coined the term "High Entropy Alloys" and proposed the core effects framework

### 1.2 The Four Core Effects of HEA — A Bird's-Eye View

The unique structure and properties of HEAs arise from **four fundamental "core effects"** that distinguish them from conventional dilute alloys (like steel, brass, or aluminum alloys which have one dominant base element):

1. **High Entropy Effect (Thermodynamic Effect)**
2. **Severe Lattice Distortion Effect (Structural Effect)**
3. **Sluggish Diffusion Effect (Kinetic Effect)**
4. **Cocktail Effect (Compositional/Properties Effect)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Fruit Salad" vs. "Fruit Smoothie":**
> Imagine two beverages:
> - A **conventional alloy** is like a **fruit salad** — one fruit dominates (e.g., apple), with tiny bits of other fruits. The dominant fruit (base metal) sets the personality of the salad.
> - An **HEA** is like a **fruit smoothie** with **5+ fruits in nearly equal proportions**. No single fruit dominates; the combined "entropy of mixing" creates a brand-new flavor profile that is more than the sum of individual tastes. This high mixing entropy is what stabilizes the HEA's simple solid solution structure rather than forming brittle intermetallic compounds.

### 1.3 Why Study Core Effects?

The core effects collectively explain **why HEAs often outperform traditional alloys** in:
- Mechanical strength (yield strength, hardness)
- Wear and fatigue resistance
- High-temperature stability
- Corrosion and oxidation resistance
- Cryogenic toughness (e.g., the famous **Cantor alloy** — CrMnFeCoNi — remains ductile even at liquid nitrogen temperatures)

These effects form the **theoretical backbone** of all subsequent modules in PEMET635, including HEA design strategies, microstructure evolution, and property optimization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effect 1: High Entropy Effect (Thermodynamic Core)

#### 2.1.1 Concept
The **high entropy effect** states that the **extremely high configurational entropy** of mixing in multi-component systems **stabilizes simple solid solution phases** (BCC, FCC, or HCP) and **suppresses the formation of complex intermetallic compounds**.

#### 2.1.2 Mathematical Foundation
For an **n-component** system in the **ideal random solid solution** state, the **molar configurational entropy of mixing** is given by Boltzmann's hypothesis:

$$\Delta S_{mix}^{conf} = -R \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \ln(c_i)$$

where:
- $c_i$ = mole fraction of the $i^{th}$ component
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $n$ = number of components

#### 2.1.3 Key Threshold
- For $n = 5$ equiatomic components ($c_i = 0.2$ each):
  $$\Delta S_{mix}^{conf} = -R \times 5 \times 0.2 \times \ln(0.2) = R \ln(5) \approx 1.609R$$
  This is the **canonical HEA entropy threshold (≥ 1.5R)**.

#### 2.1.4 Why Does High Entropy Stabilize Solid Solutions?
According to the **Gibbs free energy** relation:
$$\Delta G_{mix} = \Delta H_{mix} - T \Delta S_{mix}$$

At sufficiently high temperature, the **$-T \Delta S_{mix}$** term becomes so large (negative contribution) that it overcomes the positive enthalpy of formation of intermetallics, **favoring a disordered single-phase solid solution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs Phase Rule Limit for HEAs:**
> The classical Gibbs phase rule $P = C - F + 1$ predicts that a 5-component alloy at equilibrium should form **many intermetallic phases**, making the alloy extremely brittle. However, the high entropy effect **defies this classical expectation** by stabilizing only 1–2 simple solid solution phases — a phenomenon often called the **"entropy of mixing triumph"** over enthalpy.

### 2.2 Effect 2: Severe Lattice Distortion Effect (Structural Core)

#### 2.2.1 Concept
Because the HEA lattice is occupied by **multiple atomic species of different sizes**, the crystalline lattice suffers from **severe atomic-level distortion**. Each lattice site experiences a different local environment because the neighboring atoms are randomly different elements.

#### 2.2.2 Quantitative Description
The **atomic-size difference parameter (δ)** quantifies this distortion:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \left(1 - \frac{r_i}{\bar{r}}\right)^2}$$

where:
- $r_i$ = atomic radius of component $i$
- $\bar{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i r_i$ = average atomic radius

For typical HEAs, **δ ranges from 1% to 6%** (much higher than conventional alloys where δ < 1%).

#### 2.2.3 Consequences of Lattice Distortion
- **Solid solution strengthening** — dislocation motion is impeded
- **Reduced electrical and thermal conductivity** — phonons and electrons are strongly scattered
- **Enhanced mechanical strength** without sacrificing ductility (Hall-Petch-like behavior)
- **Modified magnetic and electronic properties**

### 2.3 Effect 3: Sluggish Diffusion Effect (Kinetic Core)

#### 2.3.1 Concept
In an HEA lattice, **each lattice site is surrounded by different atomic species**. This means that for an atom to diffuse via vacancy mechanism, it must overcome **fluctuating potential energy barriers** (some neighboring configurations have high binding energy, others low). The **average diffusion coefficient** in HEAs is therefore significantly **lower** than in pure metals or conventional alloys.

#### 2.3.2 Mathematical Indicator
