|---|---|---|---|
| Shape Fixity Ratio | $R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m}$ | Ability to fix temporary shape | Dimensionless (%) |
| Shape Recovery Ratio | $R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u}$ | Ability to recover permanent shape | Dimensionless (%) |
| Strain Recovery Rate | $V_r = \dfrac{dR_r}{dt}$ | Speed of recovery | s⁻¹ |
| Switching Stress | $\sigma_{sw} = E_{rubbery} \cdot \varepsilon_m$ | Stress at switching temperature | Pa |
| Storage Modulus Ratio | $\Delta E = \dfrac{E_{glassy}}{E_{rubbery}}$ | Stiffness change across Tg | Dimensionless |
| Recovery Force | $F_r = E_{rubbery} \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon_m$ | Force exerted during recovery | N |

Where:
- $\varepsilon_m$ = Maximum strain under load (programmed strain)
- $\varepsilon_u$ = Fixed strain after unloading (temporary shape strain)
- $\varepsilon_p$ = Residual strain after full recovery
- $E_{rubbery}$ = Young's modulus in rubbery state (~0.1 to 10 MPa)
- $E_{glassy}$ = Young's modulus in glassy state (~1 to 3 GPa)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of SMP specimen

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

SMPs have transitioned from laboratory curiosity to commercial deployment across diverse industries:

1. **Biomedical Devices:** Self-expanding vascular stents, surgical sutures that tighten on warming, targeted drug delivery capsules.
2. **Aerospace:** Deployable space structures (solar sails, antenna reflectors) that compact-launch and self-expand in orbit.
3. **Smart Textiles:** Temperature-responsive garments that adjust porosity.
4. **4D Printing:** Printed structures that morph over time (4th dimension = time).
5. **Soft Robotics:** Artificial muscles and grippers with programmable motion.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** SMPs typically have a **Young's modulus drop of 100–1000×** across their Tg. This enormous stiffness contrast is the *thermodynamic engine* of the shape memory effect — without it, neither fixation nor recovery would be mechanically efficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shape Fixity Ratio (R_f)

The Shape Fixity Ratio quantifies the polymer's ability to *retain* its temporary shape after the programming load is removed.

### Mathematical Foundation

Consider a polymer specimen subjected to uniaxial tension. Define the following engineering strains:

- $\varepsilon_m$ = strain under maximum applied load (before unloading)
- $\varepsilon_u$ = strain immediately after load removal (temporary shape strain)
- $\varepsilon_p$ = residual strain after complete recovery cycle

The *ideal* shape fixity occurs when the polymer retains the full programmed strain upon unloading, i.e., when $\varepsilon_u = \varepsilon_m$.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1:** Express the "strain loss" during unloading as the difference between maximum strain and fixed strain:

$$\Delta\varepsilon_{fix} = \varepsilon_m - \varepsilon_u$$

**Step 2:** The fractional strain retention (shape fixity) is then defined as the ratio of retained strain to the maximum applied strain:

$$R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m}$$

**Step 3:** Equivalently, in percentage form:

$$R_f \,\left(\%\right) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Substitute sample numerical values for illustration:
- Given: $\varepsilon_m = 1.00$ (100% elongation)
- Given: $\varepsilon_u = 0.98$ (slight elastic recoil of 2%)

$$R_f = \dfrac{0.98}{1.00} = 0.98 = 98\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation Boundary Conditions:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_f &= 1.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Perfect fixity (no elastic recoil)} \\
R_f &= 0.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{No fixity (instantaneous spring-back)} \\
0 \leq R_f &\leq 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Real SMP behavior}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shape Recovery Ratio (R_r)

The Shape Recovery Ratio quantifies the polymer's ability to *return* to its original permanent shape after stimulus application.

### Mathematical Foundation

**Step 1:** Define the recoverable strain component as the difference between the fixed strain (temporary shape) and the residual strain (after recovery):

$$\varepsilon_{recovered} = \varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p$$

**Step 2:** The shape recovery ratio is the ratio of recoverable strain to the originally fixed strain:

$$R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting sample numerical values:
- Given: $\varepsilon_u = 0.98$
- Given: $\varepsilon_p = 0.04$ (4% permanent set)

$$R_r = \dfrac{0.98 - 0.04}{0.98} = \dfrac{0.94}{0.98} = 0.959 = 95.9\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation Boundary Conditions:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_r &= 1.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Perfect recovery (no permanent set)} \\
R_r &= 0.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{No recovery (plastic deformation)} \\
R_r &> 0.95 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Acceptable for engineering use}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stress Generated During Recovery (Recovery Force)

When an SMP recovers against an external constraint, it generates a mechanical stress. This is critical for **actuator applications**.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** At the switching temperature, the polymer behaves as a **rubbery entropy spring** governed by Hooke's law in the elastic regime:

$$\sigma = E_{rubbery} \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** For an SMP undergoing constrained recovery (clamped at both ends while heating), the recovery strain equals the initially programmed strain:

$$\varepsilon_{recovery} = \varepsilon_m$$

**Step 3:** The recovery stress generated is therefore:

$$\sigma_{recovery} = E_{rubbery} \cdot \varepsilon_m$$

**Step 4:** For an SMP specimen with cross-sectional area $A$, the recovery force is:

$$F_{recovery} = \sigma_{recovery} \cdot A = E_{rubbery} \cdot \varepsilon_m \cdot A$$

**Step 5:** Numerical Example:
- Given: $E_{rubbery} = 2.5 \text{ MPa} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Given: $\varepsilon_m = 0.50$ (50% strain)
- Given: $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{recovery} &= (2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (0.50) \times (1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) \\
&= 125 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Storage Modulus Contrast Across Tg

The dramatic modulus change across the glass transition is the **fundamental thermodynamic driver** of the shape memory effect.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** The time-temperature superposition principle states that the storage modulus $E'$ of an amorphous polymer above Tg follows rubber elasticity theory:

$$E'_{rubbery} = 3 \nu k_B T$$

where $\nu$ is the cross-link density (mol/m³), $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Step 2:** Below Tg, the polymer is in a glassy state with modulus determined by van der Waals interactions:

$$E'_{glassy} \approx 1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3:** The modulus contrast ratio is:

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{E'_{glassy}}{E'_{rubbery}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Illustration:
- Given: $E'_{glassy} = 2.0 \text{ GPa}$
- Given: $E'_{rubbery} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^9}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 800$$

This means the polymer is **800 times stiffer** below Tg than above Tg — the physical basis for shape fixation and recovery.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbolic Definition | Physical Meaning | Numerical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_f$ | $\varepsilon_u / \varepsilon_m$ | Shape retention capability | 95 – 99.9% (excellent SMPs) |
| $R_r$ | $(\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p)/\varepsilon_u$ | Shape restoration capability | 90 – 99.5% (excellent SMPs) |
| $\varepsilon_m$ | Programmed strain | Magnitude of deformation | Up to 800% |
| $\Delta E$ | $E'_{glassy}/E'_{rubbery}$ | Stiffness contrast ratio | 100 – 1000× |
| $T_{trans}$ | Tg or Tm of switching phase | Activation temperature | 40 °C – 200 °C |
| $V_r$ | $dR_r/dt$ | Recovery kinetics | 0.01 – 1.0 s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Pattern:** Problems in board examinations typically involve (1) computing $R_f$ and $R_r$ from given strain data, (2) computing recovery force given $E_{rubbery}$, or (3) explaining why $R_r < 1$ due to viscous losses in the polymer network.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shape Memory Polymer Architecture

<!-- Mermaid block diagram -->
The following diagram illustrates the **two-phase microstructural architecture** of an SMP and the chain-level mechanism enabling the Shape Memory Effect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shape Memory Polymer SMP] --> B[Netpoints Permanent Phase]
    A --> C[Switching Segments Soft Phase]
    B --> B1[Chemical Crosslinks]
    B --> B2[Crystalline Hard Domains]
    B --> B3[Interpenetrating Network Anchors]
    C --> C1[Amorphous Chains low Tg]
    C --> C2[Semi Crystalline low Tm]
    C --> C3[Conformational Mobility Sites]
    B1 --> D1[Determine Original Permanent Shape]
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Enable Temporary Shape Fixation]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> E[Shape Memory Effect SME]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Engineering Applications]
    F --> F1[Biomedical Stents]
    F --> F2[Aerospace Deployables]
    F --> F3[Soft Robotics]
    F --> F4[4D Printed Structures]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Four-Step Shape Memory Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1 Heating Above Ttrans] --> S2[Step2 Apply Load Max Strain em]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Cooling Under Load Below Ttrans]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Unload Fix Temporary Shape eu]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 Reheat Above Ttrans Recovery]
    S5 --> S6[Step6 Original Permanent Shape Restored]
    S6 -. Optional Reprogramming .-> S1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — SMP Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[External Stimulus Heat Light pH Magnetic] --> ACTUATOR[SMP Material Activated]
    ACTUATOR --> PHASE1[Switching Segments Become Rubbery T Greater Than Ttrans]
    PHASE1 --> STORAGE[Entropic Elastic Energy Stored in Chains]
    STORAGE --> TRANSITION[Conformational Rearrangement at Molecular Level]
    TRANSITION --> OUTPUT[Macroscopic Shape Change Recovery]
    OUTPUT --> APPLICATION[Work Output Force Displacement]
    APPLICATION --> FEEDBACK[Monitor Strain Recovery Ratio Rr]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — SMP vs SMA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COMP[Stimuli Responsive Materials] --> SMP[SMP Shape Memory Polymer]
    COMP --> SMA[SMA Shape Memory Alloy]
    SMP --> SMP1[Mechanism Entropy driven conformational change]
    SMP --> SMP2[Max Strain up to 800 percent]
    SMP --> SMP3[Switching Tg or Tm]
    SMP --> SMP4[Recovery Time seconds to minutes]
    SMP --> SMP5[Low Density 0.9 to 1.4 g per cm3]
    SMA --> SMA1[Mechanism Martensitic Austenitic Phase Transformation]
    SMA --> SMA2[Max Strain up to 8 percent]
    SMA --> SMA3[Switching Ms Mf As Af temperatures]
    SMA --> SMA4[Recovery Time milliseconds]
    SMA --> SMA5[High Density 6 to 8 g per cm3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Key:** The flowcharts above map the functional dependencies between microstructural elements (netpoints and switching segments) and macroscopic shape memory behavior. Students should be able to verbally trace the causal chain from *stimulus input* → *chain mobility* → *shape recovery* during oral examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Shape Memory Polymer (SMP). Explain the role of netpoints and switching segments in establishing the shape memory effect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Shape Memory Polymer is a stimuli-responsive polymer that can be deformed into a temporary shape and subsequently recover its original permanent shape upon application of an external stimulus such as heat, light, or magnetic field.

The **netpoints** (hard segments) are responsible for determining and locking the original permanent shape of the polymer. They consist of chemical cross-links or crystalline hard domains that act as molecular anchors, storing elastic energy during deformation.

The **switching segments** (soft segments) are responsible for enabling temporary shape fixation. They undergo a reversible transition (typically at Tg or Tm) that allows the polymer to switch between a rigid glassy state (shape fixed) and a flexible rubbery state (shape programmable).

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Netpoints role (1) + Switching segments role (1)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Differentiate between one-way and two-way shape memory effects in polymers with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | One-Way SME | Two-Way SME |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Requires external reprogramming | Reversible upon thermal cycling |
| Cycles | Single recovery per programming | Continuous alternating shape change |
| Example | Polyurethane SMP | Cross-linked polycyclooctene |
| Application | Vascular stents | Artificial muscles |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition of one-way (1) + Definition of two-way (1) + Example/distinction (1)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete thermomechanical cycle** of a thermally-induced Shape Memory Polymer. Discuss the molecular-level events occurring at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical expressions for **Shape Fixity Ratio (R_f)** and **Shape Recovery Ratio (R_r)**. A polymer sample is stretched to a maximum strain $\varepsilon_m = 0.80$. After unloading, the fixed strain is $\varepsilon_u = 0.76$. After complete thermal recovery, the residual strain is $\varepsilon_p = 0.03$. Calculate $R_f$ and $R_r$ and comment on the SMP performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — The Four-Step Thermomechanical Cycle (7 marks)**

The shape memory cycle of a thermally-induced SMP consists of four sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Heating Above Switching Temperature (1 mark):**
The SMP is heated above its switching temperature T_trans (typically Tg for amorphous SMPs or Tm for crystalline SMPs). At this temperature, the switching segments transition from a rigid glassy state to a flexible rubbery state. Chain mobility increases dramatically, and the netpoints remain structurally intact.

**Step 2 — Application of Load (1.5 marks):**
An external mechanical load is applied, deforming the polymer to a maximum strain εm. The polymer chains uncoil and align along the stress direction. The netpoints stretch like springs, storing elastic energy.

**Step 3 — Cooling Under Constant Load (1.5 marks):**
While maintaining the load, the polymer is cooled below T_trans. The switching segments vitrify (amorphous SMP) or crystallize (semi-crystalline SMP), locking the oriented chain conformations. The temporary shape becomes mechanically stable.

**Step 4 — Load Removal and Recovery (2 marks):**
The load is removed at low temperature — minimal elastic recoil due to glassy rigidity. Upon reheating above T_trans, switching segments regain mobility, and the entropic elasticity of the netpoints drives the polymer back to its original coiled conformation. The permanent shape is restored.

A reference block diagram may be drawn showing the strain-temperature path with labeled regions for deformation, fixation, unloading, and recovery.

**Mark Allocation:** Step explanations (5) + Block diagram representation (2)

---

**Part (b) — Mathematical Derivation and Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Derivation of R_f (1.5 marks):**

$$R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m}$$

This ratio represents the fraction of programmed strain retained after load removal. A value approaching unity indicates excellent shape fixity.

**Derivation of R_r (1.5 marks):**

$$R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u}$$

This ratio represents the fraction of fixed strain that is recoverable upon stimulus application. A value approaching unity indicates excellent shape recovery.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $\varepsilon_m = 0.80$
- $\varepsilon_u = 0.76$
- $\varepsilon_p = 0.03$

Step 1 — Compute R_f:

$$R_f = \dfrac{0.76}{0.80} = 0.950 = 95.0\%$$

Step 2 — Compute R_r:

$$R_r = \dfrac{0.76 - 0.03}{0.76} = \dfrac{0.73}{0.76} = 0.9605 = 96.05\%$$

**Comment on Performance (1 mark):**

Both $R_f = 95\%$ and $R_r = 96\%$ indicate **excellent shape memory performance**, suitable for high-precision engineering applications such as biomedical stents and deployable aerospace structures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Classify Shape Memory Polymers based on **activation stimulus**. Explain photo-induced and electro-active SMPs with their mechanisms and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **mechanical and thermomechanical performance** of Shape Memory Polymers with Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs). Why are SMPs preferred for biomedical applications despite their lower recovery force? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Classification by Stimulus Type (7 marks)**

SMPs can be activated by multiple stimuli including thermal, photo, electrical, magnetic, chemical, and moisture. Focus is placed on two advanced categories:

**Photo-Induced SMPs (3.5 marks):**
- Mechanism: Incorporation of photoresponsive groups (e.g., azobenzene, cinnamate) into the polymer backbone. UV/visible light induces cis-trans isomerization or reversible cross-linking changes.
- Activation: Remote and contactless triggering; precise spatial control using light patterns.
- Applications: Microactuators, optical switches, minimally invasive surgical tools.

**Electro-Active SMPs (3.5 marks):**
- Mechanism: Composites of SMP matrix with conductive fillers (carbon nanotubes, graphene, silver nanowires, carbon black). Electrical current passes through the conductive network, generating Joule heat that raises the polymer above T_trans.
- Activation: Direct electrical control without external heating sources.
- Applications: Soft robotic actuators, artificial muscles, deployable electronics.

**Mark Allocation:** Classification intro (1) + Photo-induced mechanism+app (3) + Electro-active mechanism+app (3)

---

**Part (b) — SMP vs SMA Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Property | SMP | SMA |
|---|---|---|
| Max recoverable strain | Up to 800% | Up to 8% |
| Density | 0.9 – 1.4 g/cm³ | 6 – 8 g/cm³ |
| Switching temp range | 40 – 200 °C (tunable) | Fixed by alloy composition |
| Recovery force | Low (N scale) | High (kN scale) |
| Biocompatibility | High (select grades) | Limited (Ni toxicity concern) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Manufacturability | Easy (casting, 3D printing) | Difficult (metallurgy) |
| Recovery time | Seconds – minutes | Milliseconds |

**Why SMPs are preferred for biomedical applications despite lower recovery force (3 marks):**

1. **Biocompatibility:** Many SMPs (polyurethane, PLA, PEG-based) are FDA-approved; SMAs containing nickel can cause allergic reactions.
2. **Tunable T_trans:** SMPs can be engineered to switch at body temperature (37 °C); SMAs have limited compositional flexibility.
3. **High strain capacity:** SMPs can recover from 100 – 800% strain, suitable for catheter-delivered devices; SMAs are limited to ~8%.
4. **Drug loading capability:** SMP matrices can embed therapeutic agents for controlled release.
5. **MR Compatibility:** SMPs are non-magnetic, safe for MRI environments.

**Mark Allocation:** Comparison table (3) + Justification with 5 points (4)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Omitting the dual-phase architecture in definitions:** A definition that says "SMP is a polymer that remembers its shape" without mentioning **netpoints** and **switching segments** will lose **1 mark** instantly. Always anchor your definition to the structural basis.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing R_f and R_r in numerical problems:** Students frequently invert the formulas. Remember: $R_f$ uses $\varepsilon_m$ in the denominator; $R_r$ uses $\varepsilon_u$ in the denominator.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Failing to comment on numerical results:** Even if calculations are correct, KTU examiners deduct marks if students do not provide a **qualitative interpretation** (e.g., "R_r = 96% indicates excellent recovery suitable for biomedical use").
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Drawing incomplete cycle diagrams:** Block diagrams must show **all four steps** including the cooling-under-load stage. Diagrams missing the cooling step lose 1.5 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Mixing units in force calculations:** Always convert MPa to Pa and cm² to m² before multiplying. A mismatch like (MPa × cm²) will cost 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SMP = Smart Polymer** with reversible shape change triggered by external stimulus
- **Two-phase structure is mandatory:** Netpoints (permanent) + Switching segments (reversible)
- **Four-step cycle:** Heat → Load → Cool → Unload → Reheat (Recovery)
- **Shape Fixity Ratio:** $R_f = \varepsilon_u / \varepsilon_m$ — measures shape retention
- **Shape Recovery Ratio:** $R_r = (\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p)/\varepsilon_u$ — measures shape restoration
- **Excellent SMP performance:** $R_f \geq 95\%$ AND $R_r \geq 95\%$
- **Maximum strain advantage:** SMPs (up to 800%) vastly exceed SMAs (~8%)
- **Switching temperature:** Tg for amorphous SMPs, Tm for semi-crystalline SMPs
- **Modulus contrast across Tg:** 100–1000× drives the entire shape memory mechanism
- **Biomedical suitability:** Tunable T_trans, biocompatibility, high strain capacity
- **Two-way SMPs:** Reversible without reprogramming — used in artificial muscles
- **Photo-induced SMPs:** Remote activation using light — used in micro-actuators
- **Electro-active SMPs:** Joule heating via conductive fillers — used in soft robotics
- **4D Printing = 3D printing + time-dependent shape change via SMPs**
- **Recovery force** is lower than SMAs but adequate for most biomedical and deployable applications
- **Always provide qualitative interpretation** alongside numerical answers for full marks

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo-Stimulated SMP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermo-Stimulated SMP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermo-Stimulated Shape Memory Polymer (SMP)** is a class of polymeric smart material capable of fixing a mechanically deformed *temporary shape* and subsequently recovering its *original (permanent) shape* when subjected to a thermal stimulus, typically heating above a characteristic switching transition temperature ($T_{sw}$), which may correspond to either the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** or the **melting transition temperature ($T_m$)** of a switching segment domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Shape memory polymers (SMPs) are polymer networks that can be deformed and fixed into a temporary shape, and recover their permanent shape upon application of an external stimulus such as heat, light, magnetic field, or moisture. Thermo-stimulated SMPs use temperature as the primary trigger, with $T_g$ or $T_m$ acting as the switching threshold.*

The shape memory effect is fundamentally an **entropy-driven elastic recovery** phenomenon, in which polymer chain conformations revert from an oriented, low-entropy state back to a coiled, high-entropy equilibrium state upon thermal activation.

## 1.2 Structural Prerequisites for the Shape Memory Effect

A polymer must simultaneously possess two structurally distinct phases to exhibit thermo-stimulated shape memory behavior:

1. **Netpoints (Network Points):** Permanent physical or chemical crosslinks that memorize the *permanent shape* by preventing viscous flow of chains. These may be:
   - *Chemical netpoints:* Covalent crosslinks formed during curing/vulcanization.
   - *Physical netpoints:* Crystalline hard segments, chain entanglements, or hydrogen-bonded domains.

2. **Switching Segments:** Reversible phases responsible for *fixing the temporary shape*. These segments are immobilized (frozen) below $T_{sw}$ and highly mobile above $T_{sw}$. Typical switching segments include:
   - Amorphous chains with $T_g$-based switching (e.g., polyurethane segments)
   - Semi-crystalline domains with $T_m$-based switching (e.g., polycaprolactone, PCL)
   - Hydrogen-bonded supramolecular networks

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Folded Origami Crane:** Imagine a piece of stiff paper folded into a crane at room temperature — once cooled and dried with glue, the crane stays in shape. When you steam (heat) it again, the glue softens, the paper relaxes, and it flattens back to its original sheet form. The glue spots are *netpoints* (permanent memory), the paper is the *switching segment* (activates on heat), and the steam is the *thermal trigger*.

## 1.3 The Four Canonical States of a Thermo-Stimulated SMP

| State | Temperature Condition | Mechanical Behavior | Shape Status |
|:------|:----------------------|:--------------------|:-------------|
| **State A** | $T > T_{sw}$ | Rubbery, elastic | Permanent (original) |
| **State B** | $T > T_{sw}$, loaded | Ductile deformation | Elongated under stress |
| **State C** | $T < T_{sw}$, unloaded | Glassy, rigid | Temporary (fixed) |
| **State D** | $T > T_{sw}$, reheated | Entropic recovery | Permanent (recovered) |

This 4-state logic underpins all KTU examination problems on thermo-mechanical cyclic testing of SMPs.

## 1.4 Historical Context and Significance

Thermo-stimulated SMPs emerged from the foundational work of **Lendlein and Kelch (2002)** on biodegradable SMP networks and have since become a cornerstone of stimuli-responsive materials research. Unlike shape memory alloys (SMAs), SMPs offer:
- **Lower density** ($\sim 1.0$ g/cm³ vs. $\sim 6.5$ g/cm³ for Nitinol)
- **Higher recoverable strain** (up to **400–800%** vs. **~8%** for SMAs)
- **Tunable transition temperatures** through simple copolymer chemistry
- **Lower cost** and easier processability

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When comparing SMPs with SMAs in 14-mark questions, emphasize the *recoverable strain*, *biocompatibility*, and *processing flexibility* of polymers — these are the three examiner-favored comparison axes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Shape Memory

The shape memory effect is governed by the entropic elasticity of polymer chains, which is well described by the **neo-Hookean / Gaussian chain model**. The fundamental steps in a thermo-mechanical cycle are:

### Step 1 — Activation (Deformation above $T_{sw}$)
At $T > T_{sw}$, the switching segments are in a **rubbery state** (above $T_g$ or melted above $T_m$). The free volume is high, and chain segments have substantial conformational mobility. When an external stress $\sigma$ is applied, polymer chains uncoil from their random-coil (high-entropy) conformation and align along the direction of stress, transitioning to a **low-entropy oriented state**.

### Step 2 — Cooling under Constraint (Shape Fixing)
While maintaining the applied deformation, the polymer is cooled below $T_{sw}$. The switching segments undergo a transition:
- For $T_g$-based SMPs: rubber → glassy state (segmental mobility frozen)
- For $T_m$-based SMPs: amorphous rubber → crystalline (chain mobility locked)

In this **glassy/crystalline state**, the oriented chain conformations are kinetically trapped. The internal elastic restoring stress is counterbalanced by the high modulus of the frozen matrix — this is the **"shape fixing"** mechanism.

### Step 3 — Unloading (Temporary Shape Stabilization)
Once fully cooled, the external stress is removed. Because the switching phase is rigid, the polymer retains the deformed geometry with minimal springback. The temporary shape is now *fixed*.

### Step 4 — Recovery (Reheating above $T_{sw}$)
When reheated above $T_{sw}$, the switching segments regain mobility. The stored elastic energy (driven by the entropy increase upon chain recoil) is released. Chains snap back toward their **highest-entropy random-coil state** — the original shape memorized by the permanent netpoints.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why Entropy? — The Driving Force:** A deformed polymer chain has fewer accessible microstates (lower entropy). The universe always seeks maximum entropy, so when thermally activated, the chain spontaneously returns to its coiled, high-entropy state. This is the *thermodynamic foundation* of the shape memory effect.

## 2.2 Quantitative Shape Memory Metrics

Two metrics universally used to characterize SMP performance, and which are **examinable in KTU numerical problems**, are:

### Shape Fixity Ratio ($R_f$)

Quantifies how well the temporary shape is preserved after unloading:

$$
R_f(N) = \frac{\varepsilon_u(N)}{\varepsilon_{load}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_u(N)$ = strain of the temporary shape after unloading in cycle $N$
- $\varepsilon_{load}$ = maximum applied strain during loading

### Shape Recovery Ratio ($R_r$)

Quantifies how well the permanent shape is recovered after reheating:

$$
R_r(N) = \frac{\varepsilon_u(N) - \varepsilon_p(N)}{\varepsilon_u(N) - \varepsilon_p(N-1)} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_p(N)$ = residual strain after recovery in cycle $N$
- $\varepsilon_p(N-1)$ = residual strain from the previous cycle (zero for $N=1$)

For the **first cycle** ($N = 1$), the simplified form used in most KTU problems is:

$$
R_r(1) = \frac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_{load}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Valuation Tip:** A *perfect* SMP exhibits $R_f = 100\%$ and $R_r = 100\%$. Real SMPs typically show $R_f \in [85\%, 99\%]$ and $R_r \in [90\%, 99\%]$.

### Strain Recovery Rate ($\dot{R}_r$)

For kinetics problems (advanced KTU questions):

$$
\dot{R}_r = \frac{dR_r}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau_{rec}} \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{rec}}\right)
$$

Where $\tau_{rec}$ is the characteristic recovery time constant, often following Arrhenius-type temperature dependence:

$$
\tau_{rec}(T) = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

With $E_a$ = activation energy of chain segmental motion, $R$ = universal gas constant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-------------------|:------|
| 1 | Shape Fixity Ratio | $R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_{m}} \times 100$ | $\varepsilon_u$ = strain after unload; $\varepsilon_m$ = max strain | % |
| 2 | Shape Recovery Ratio | $R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u} \times 100$ | $\varepsilon_p$ = residual strain after recovery | % |
| 3 | Cyclic Recovery Ratio | $R_r(N) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u(N) - \varepsilon_p(N)}{\varepsilon_u(N) - \varepsilon_p(N-1)} \times 100$ | $N$ = cycle index | % |
| 4 | Recovery Time Constant | $\tau_{rec} = \tau_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ = activation energy; $R$ = gas constant | s |
| 5 | Switching Ratio (Tg-based) | $\phi(T) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\left[\alpha(T - T_g)\right]}$ | $\alpha$ = width parameter (~0.2–0.5 K⁻¹) | — |
| 6 | Crosslink Density (Flory) | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c}$ | $\rho$ = density; $M_c$ = mol. wt. between crosslinks | mol/m³ |
| 7 | Storage Modulus Drop | $E'(T_g^-) / E'(T_g^+) \approx 100$–$1000$ | Rubbery-to-glassy modulus ratio | — |
| 8 | Entropic Stress | $\sigma = N k_B T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)$ | $N$ = chain density; $\lambda$ = stretch ratio | Pa |

> [!TIP]  
> **Boundary conditions** to memorize: For $R_f \to 100\%$, the temporary shape must remain *perfectly glassy/crystalline* below $T_{sw}$. For $R_r \to 100\%$, the permanent network must have *zero viscous flow* (i.e., a true thermoset or fully crosslinked network).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Functional Use | Required $T_{sw}$ |
|:-------------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| **Biomedical stents** | Self-expanding vascular implants | $T_{sw} \approx 37$°C (body temp) |
| **Smart sutures** | Self-tightening wound closures | $T_{sw} \approx 40$°C |
| **Deployable aerospace structures** | Solar sail deployment, morphing wings | $T_{sw} \approx 80$–$120$°C |
| **4D-printed soft robotics** | Programmable actuators | Tunable |
| **Smart textiles** | Breathable, shape-adaptive garments | $T_{sw} \approx 30$–$50$°C |
| **Self-healing polymers** | Crack closure on heating | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Always cite at least one biomedical and one aerospace application in long-answer questions to demonstrate interdisciplinary awareness — examiners explicitly reward this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Entropic Elastic Recovery from Gaussian Chain Statistics

The shape memory recovery originates from the entropic elasticity of a Gaussian polymer chain, first formulated by **Warner (1972)** and **Flory (1953)**.

### Step 1 — Free Energy of a Gaussian Chain

A single polymer chain of $N$ freely jointed Kuhn segments of length $b$, end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$, has the entropy:

$$
S(\vec{r}) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B \vec{r}^2}{2 N b^2}
$$

The entropic contribution to the Helmholtz free energy ($F = U - TS$) at temperature $T$, assuming purely entropic elasticity ($U = 0$):

$$
F = -T S = F_0 + \frac{3 k_B T \vec{r}^2}{2 N b^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Force Along the End-to-End Direction

Differentiating $F$ with respect to displacement $r$ gives the entropic restoring force for a single chain:

$$
f = \frac{\partial F}{\partial r} = \frac{3 k_B T}{N b^2} \, r
$$

This shows that the restoring force is **directly proportional to temperature** — a hallmark of entropic (rubber) elasticity, distinguishing it from enthalpic (metallic) elasticity.

### Step 3 — Crosslinked Network Stress-Strain Relation

For a network of $n$ chains per unit volume, defining the macroscopic stretch ratio $\lambda = L / L_0$, the nominal stress is:

$$
\sigma = n k_B T \left( \lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \right)
$$

This is the **neo-Hookean constitutive equation** for a SMP in its rubbery state — the equation used in finite element simulations of SMP actuators.

### Step 4 — Justification for Temperature-Activated Recovery

Upon cooling below $T_{sw}$, the modulus jumps by **2–3 orders of magnitude** (from rubbery ~1 MPa to glassy ~1–3 GPa). The macroscopic deformation is *kinetically locked*. Upon reheating, the entropic force in Equation above becomes active again, driving $\lambda \to 1$ — i.e., recovery to the undeformed shape. The recovery stress is:

$$
\sigma_{rec}(T) = n k_B T \left(1 - \frac{1}{\lambda_{fix}^2}\right)
$$

This recovery stress decreases as the sample approaches the permanent shape ($\lambda \to 1$).

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Solution — KTU Board Exam Pattern

> **Problem Statement [Adapted from KTU University Exam Pattern]:**
> A thermo-stimulated SMP specimen is subjected to a cyclic thermo-mechanical test. The specimen is first heated to $T = T_g + 25°\text{C}$ (rubbery state), then loaded to a maximum tensile strain $\varepsilon_m = 80\%$. It is then cooled under constant strain to $T = T_g - 30°\text{C}$, unloaded, and the residual strain is measured as $\varepsilon_u = 76\%$. The sample is then reheated to $T = T_g + 25°\text{C}$ and allowed to recover freely. The final residual strain is $\varepsilon_p = 4\%$.
>
> Calculate:
> (i) The shape fixity ratio $R_f$
> (ii) The shape recovery ratio $R_r$
> (iii) Comment on the quality of the shape memory behavior.
> (iv) If the activation energy of segmental motion is $E_a = 90 \text{ kJ/mol}$, estimate how the recovery time changes when recovery temperature is raised from $T_g + 5°\text{C}$ to $T_g + 30°\text{C}$. Take $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.

### Solution — Part (i): Shape Fixity Ratio

Using the formula:

$$
R_f = \frac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_f = \frac{76}{80} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_f = 0.95 \times 100\% = \mathbf{95\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** 95% of the imposed deformation is retained in the temporary shape. Only 5% elastic springback occurred upon unloading, indicating an effective vitrification (or crystallization) of the switching segments below $T_{sw}$.

### Solution — Part (ii): Shape Recovery Ratio

Using the first-cycle form:

$$
R_r = \frac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_r = \frac{76 - 4}{76} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_r = \frac{72}{76} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_r \approx 0.9474 \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{94.74\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** After reheating, 94.74% of the temporary deformation is recovered. The 5.26% non-recovery arises from:
- Irreversible disentanglement of loosely trapped chains
- Minor plastic flow in the switching phase
- Imperfect elasticity of the network

### Solution — Part (iii): Quality Assessment

With $R_f = 95\%$ and $R_r = 94.74\%$, the SMP shows **excellent shape memory performance**, typical of well-designed polyurethane or polynorbornene networks. For reference:

| Performance Tier | $R_f$ Range | $R_r$ Range |
|:-----------------|:-------------|:-------------|
| **Excellent (Premium)** | $> 98\%$ | $> 98\%$ |
| **Good (Engineering-Grade)** | 90–98% | 90–98% |
| **Acceptable (Prototype)** | 80–90% | 80–90% |
| **Poor (Material Failure)** | $< 80\%$ | $< 80\%$ |

This SMP falls in the **engineering-grade** category, suitable for most industrial applications.

### Solution — Part (iv): Recovery Time Scaling

The recovery time constant follows Arrhenius behavior:

$$
\tau_{rec}(T) = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

Taking the ratio at two temperatures:

$$
\frac{\tau_{rec}(T_g + 5)}{\tau_{rec}(T_g + 30)} = \frac{\exp(E_a / R T_1)}{\exp(E_a / R T_2)} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

Let $T_g = 50°\text{C} = 323 \text{ K}$. Then $T_1 = 328 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 353 \text{ K}$.

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{328} - \frac{1}{353}
$$

$$
= 0.003049 - 0.002833 = 0.000216 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \Delta\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = \frac{90000}{8.314} \times 0.000216
$$

$$
= 10825.6 \times 0.000216 \approx 2.338
$$

$$
\frac{\tau_{rec}(T_1)}{\tau_{rec}(T_2)} = e^{2.338} \approx 10.36
$$

**Conclusion:** Raising the recovery temperature from $T_g + 5°\text{C}$ to $T_g + 30°\text{C}$ reduces the recovery time by a factor of **~10**. This dramatic acceleration underpins the design rule: *recover at $T_{sw} + (20 \text{ to } 30°\text{C})$ for practical actuation speeds.*

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Composition on SMP Behavior

| Structural Parameter | Effect on $T_{sw}$ | Effect on $R_f$ | Effect on $R_r$ | Engineering Implication |
|:---------------------|:--------------------|:----------------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| ↑ Crosslink density ($n$) | Slight ↑ (via $T_g$ Fox relation) | ↑↑ Strong | ↑↑ Strong | Better recovery, slower recovery |
| ↑ Soft segment length | ↓ $T_g$ (more mobile) | ↑ (more fixable) | ↓ (more chain slip) | Tunable transition |
| ↑ Hard segment content | ↑ $T_g$, ↑ modulus | ↓ (more constrained) | ↑↑ (more netpoints) | Better recovery, higher modulus |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | ↓ $T_g$ linearly | ↑ (more fixable) | ↓ (plastic flow) | Lower $T_{sw}$ for biomedical |
| ↑ Crystallinity | Sharper $T_m$ transition | ↑↑ (in $T_m$-SMPs) | ↑ (physical netpoints) | Sharper switching |
| ↑ Filler content (e.g., CNTs) | ↑ $T_g$ slightly | ↑ | ↑↑ | Reinforced composites |
| ↑ Heating rate | Negligible on $R_f$, $R_r$ | — | — | Faster recovery kinetics |
| ↑ Cooling rate | ↓ Effective $R_f$ | ↓ (thermal stresses) | — | Slow-cool for optimal fixation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Four-Step Thermo-Mechanical Programming Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the canonical "programming–recovery" cycle of a thermo-stimulated SMP, mapped at the **block-level functional architecture** rather than molecular detail (for clarity in KTU board answers).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STATE A: ORIGINAL SHAPE Permanent shape memorized by netpoints at T greater than Tsw"]
    S2["STATE B: HIGH TEMPERATURE DEFORMATION Heated above Tsw, stress applied, chains orient to low entropy state"]
    S3["STATE C: COOLING UNDER CONSTRAINT Cooled below Tsw under fixed strain, switching phase vitrifies or crystallizes"]
    S4["STATE D: TEMPORARY SHAPE FIXED External stress removed, temporary shape retained with minimal springback"]
    S5["STATE E: REHEATING AND RECOVERY Heated above Tsw, entropic restoring force drives chain recoil"]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S1
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Molecular Mechanism — Chain Conformation Changes

The following block diagram illustrates the conformational entropy changes during the four states, mapping the **microstructural state** as a function of thermal history.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["NETWORK AT REST Above Tsw - Random Coil Conformation - High Entropy S0"] --> B["DEFORMATION Above Tsw - Chains Align Along Stress Axis - Reduced Entropy S1 less than S0"]
    B --> C["COOLING BELOW Tsw Under Constraint - Conformation Frozen in Glassy Matrix - Constant Entropy"]
    C --> D["UNLOADING - Temporary Shape Retained - Elastic Springback Negligible"]
    D --> E["RECOVERY HEAT Above Tsw - Entropic Force Recoils Chains - Entropy Restored to S0"]
    E --> A
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Functional Architecture of an SMP Device

For complex devices (e.g., self-expanding stents), the SMP system can be represented as a hierarchical functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SMP_DEVICE["SMP BASED FUNCTIONAL DEVICE"]
        direction TB
        N1["PERMANENT NETWORK PHASE Chemical or Physical Netpoints Stores Original Geometry"]
        N2["SWITCHING PHASE Tg or Tm Controlled Shape Fixing and Release"]
        N3["INTERFACE LAYER Adhesion, Biocompatibility, Surface Functionalization"]
        N4["REINFORCEMENT FILLERS CNTs, Graphene, Fibers for Strength Modulus"]
        N5["PROGRAMMING CONTROLLER Thermal Actuator Laser Heater Hot Air"]
        N1 --- N2
        N2 --- N3
        N3 --- N4
        N4 --- N5
    end
    SMP_DEVICE --> O1["OUTPUT SHAPE Primary Permanent Recovery Geometry"]
    SMP_DEVICE --> O2["OUTPUT FORCE Recovery Stress in MPa"]
    SMP_DEVICE --> O3["OUTPUT KINETICS Recovery Time in Seconds"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Qualitative Stress-Strain-Temperature Surface

This block diagram conveys the key response surfaces typically required in KTU 14-mark answers when describing the thermomechanical behavior of SMPs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["STRESS STRAIN TEMPERATURE RESPONSE OF SMP"]
    R --> P1["REGION 1 GLASSY LOW T below Tg High Modulus 1 to 3 GPa Brittle Fracture"]
    R --> P2["REGION 2 GLASS TRANSITION T approx Tg Modulus Drops 2 to 3 Orders Softening Peak tan delta"]
    R --> P3["REGION 3 RUBBERY PLATEAU Tg less than T less than Tm Low Modulus 0.1 to 10 MPa High Extensibility"]
    R --> P4["REGION 4 MELTING Tm Sharp Modulus Drop Flow Possible Beyond Tm"]
    R --> P5["CYCLIC BEHAVIOR Loading at High T Fixing at Low T Recovery on Reheat"]
    P5 --> P5A["Stress Strain at T greater than Tg During Loading"]
    P5 --> P5B["Cooling Path at Constant Strain"]
    P5 --> P5C["Unloading Path at T less than Tg"]
    P5 --> P5D["Free Recovery Path at T greater than Tg"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When drawing the stress–strain–temperature response in the exam, label all four axes clearly and indicate the direction of the cycle (loading → cooling → unloading → reheating → recovery). A well-labeled diagram typically earns **2 of the 14 marks** without further explanation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Definition Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a **Thermo-Stimulated Shape Memory Polymer**. Distinguish between the roles of **netpoints** and **switching segments** in determining the shape memory behavior.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thermo-stimulated SMP is a polymer network that can be deformed into a temporary shape below a characteristic switching temperature $T_{sw}$, and recover its original permanent shape upon heating above $T_{sw}$, driven by the entropic elasticity of polymer chains.

- **Netpoints:** Permanent chemical crosslinks or physical domains that *memorize the permanent shape* and prevent chain flow. They determine the **shape recovery ratio $R_r$**.
- **Switching segments:** Reversible phases (glassy or crystalline) that *fix the temporary shape* below $T_{sw}$ and release it above $T_{sw}$. They determine the **shape fixity ratio $R_f$**.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for netpoints role, 0.5 marks for switching segments — total 3 marks.)*

### Question A2: Concept Question `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Explain the terms **shape fixity ratio ($R_f$)** and **shape recovery ratio ($R_r$)**. Why is $R_r$ rarely 100% in real SMP systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shape Fixity Ratio ($R_f$):** Measures how well the temporary shape is preserved after unloading, given by:

$$
R_f = \frac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m} \times 100\%
$$

**Shape Recovery Ratio ($R_r$):** Measures how completely the permanent shape is recovered on reheating:

$$
R_r = \frac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u} \times 100\%
$$

$R_r$ rarely reaches 100% in practice due to:
1. **Chain disentanglement** under high stress during programming.
2. **Plastic flow** in the switching phase above $T_{sw}$.
3. **Incomplete recovery** if reheating is not sufficient above $T_{sw}$.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark each for definitions + formulas; 1 mark for causes of $R_r < 100\%$.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled schematic, describe the **molecular mechanism** of thermo-stimulated shape memory in polymers. Discuss the role of *entropy* as the driving force for recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SMP specimen is subjected to cyclic thermomechanical testing. In the first cycle, the specimen is elongated to a maximum strain $\varepsilon_m = 120\%$ at $T = T_g + 30°\text{C}$, then cooled to $T = T_g - 40°\text{C}$ under constraint. After unloading, the residual strain is $\varepsilon_u = 113\%$. After reheating to $T = T_g + 30°\text{C}$ and free recovery, the residual strain is $\varepsilon_p = 7\%$. Determine:
   (i) Shape fixity ratio $R_f$
   (ii) Shape recovery ratio $R_r$
   (iii) Comment on the engineering suitability of this SMP for a self-expanding stent application (which requires $R_f > 95\%$ and $R_r > 95\%$). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The shape memory effect arises from the cooperative action of two structural elements:

**Step 1 — Programming (Heating above $T_{sw}$):** At $T > T_{sw}$, switching segments are in a rubbery state with high chain mobility. The polymer is deformed under external stress. Chains uncoil from random-coil (high-entropy, $S_0$) to oriented (low-entropy, $S_1$) conformations. This is an *entropically unfavorable* state.

**Step 2 — Shape Fixing (Cooling below $T_{sw}$):** Under maintained constraint, the polymer is cooled. The switching phase vitrifies (for $T_g$-SMP) or crystallizes (for $T_m$-SMP). Chain mobility is kinetically frozen, locking the oriented conformation. The internal elastic restoring stress is stored but cannot act because of the rigid surrounding matrix.

**Step 3 — Unloading:** The temporary shape is preserved with minimal elastic springback, achieving high $R_f$.

**Step 4 — Recovery (Reheating above $T_{sw}$):** The switching phase re-softens. The stored elastic energy is released, and chains recoil to their equilibrium random-coil state. This **entropy-driven process** ($\Delta S = S_0 - S_1 > 0$) is thermodynamically spontaneous and is the fundamental driving force for shape recovery.

**Mathematical expression for driving force:**

$$
F_{drive} = -T \cdot \Delta S = \text{recovery force}
$$

The netpoints (chemical or physical crosslinks) ensure the chain recoils to its *original* conformation rather than a new arbitrary one — this is what defines the *permanent* shape.

*(Marks Distribution: 2 marks for schematic, 3 marks for mechanism explanation, 1 mark for entropy discussion, 1 mark for netpoints role.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\varepsilon_m = 120\%$ (maximum applied strain)
- $\varepsilon_u = 113\%$ (strain after cooling and unloading)
- $\varepsilon_p = 7\%$ (residual strain after recovery)

**(i) Shape Fixity Ratio:**

$$
R_f = \frac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_m} \times 100\% = \frac{113}{120} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_f = 0.9417 \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{94.17\%}
$$

**(ii) Shape Recovery Ratio:**

$$
R_r = \frac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u} \times 100\% = \frac{113 - 7}{113} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_r = \frac{106}{113} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{93.81\%}
$$

**(iii) Suitability for Self-Expanding Stent:**

| Requirement | Calculated | Pass/Fail |
|:------------|:-----------|:----------|
| $R_f > 95\%$ | 94.17% | ❌ **FAIL** (just below threshold) |
| $R_r > 95\%$ | 93.81% | ❌ **FAIL** |

**Conclusion:** This SMP is **not directly suitable** for self-expanding stent application in its current form. To meet the criteria, the polymer should be:
- **Optimized by increasing crosslink density** (raises $R_r$).
- **Programmed with longer cooling times** below $T_{sw}$ (raises $R_f$).
- **Copolymerized with harder segments** to enhance netpoint density.

A post-treatment such as *isothermal annealing* or *post-curing* could potentially raise both metrics above 95%.

*(Marks Distribution: 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including comparison table and conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain with a suitable **stress–strain–temperature diagram**, the four-step thermomechanical cycle of a thermo-stimulated SMP. Clearly indicate the loading, cooling, unloading, and recovery paths on the diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **chemically crosslinked** and **physically crosslinked** SMP networks. Discuss how the choice of crosslinking strategy influences the shape memory performance, recoverable strain, and re-processability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The thermomechanical cycle of a thermo-stimulated SMP involves four sequential paths:

**Path 1 — Isothermal Loading at $T > T_{sw}$ (Stress-Strain Curve):**
The polymer is in a rubbery state. Under tensile loading, it exhibits a characteristic non-linear hyperelastic response described by the neo-Hookean model:

$$
\sigma = n k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

The stress increases monotonically with strain, reaching a maximum at $\varepsilon_m$.

**Path 2 — Iso-strain Cooling (Stress vs. Temperature Curve):**
With strain held constant, the temperature is reduced below $T_{sw}$. The stress rises sharply as the switching phase vitrifies. This stress buildup represents the stored elastic energy.

**Path 3 — Isothermal Unloading at $T < T_{sw}$ (Stress-Strain Curve):**
The high modulus of the glassy phase results in a near-vertical unloading line — minimal strain recovery, fixing the temporary shape with high $R_f$.

**Path 4 — Iso-stress Free Recovery (Strain vs. Temperature Curve):**
On reheating, the strain drops sharply as $T$ crosses $T_{sw}$. The polymer returns to its original length as the entropic restoring force is unleashed.

```
   STRESS (sigma)
      ^
      |            / Path 1: Loading at T > Tsw
      |           /
      |          /
      |         /
      |        /
      |       /
      |      /
      |_____/____________> STRAIN (epsilon)
                ^
                | Path 3: Unloading at T < Tsw (near vertical)
```

```
   STRAIN (epsilon)
      ^
      |  _____________ Path 2: Iso-strain cooling (constant)
      |  |
      |  |\
      |  | \  Path 4: Free recovery on reheating
      |  |  \____
      |  |       \____
      |__|____________> TEMPERATURE (T)
         Tsw
```

*(Marks Distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for path explanations, 2 marks for correct identification of state changes.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Feature | Chemically Crosslinked SMP | Physically Crosslinked SMP |
|:--------|:---------------------------|:---------------------------|
| **Nature of netpoints** | Permanent covalent bonds (e.g., peroxide-cured, UV-cured) | Physical aggregates (crystalline domains, H-bonds, ionic clusters) |
| **Network stability** | Thermally irreversible (does not melt) | Thermally reversible (can be melt-processed) |
| **$T_{sw}$** | Set by $T_g$ of amorphous phase | Can be set by either $T_g$ or $T_m$ of soft/hard domains |
| **Recoverable strain** | Moderate (typically 50–200%) | High (up to 400–800%, e.g., polyurethanes) |
| **Re-processability** | Not re-processable (thermoset) | Re-processable (thermoplastic-like) |
| **Examples** | Cured polynorbornene, crosslinked polycaprolactone | Polyurethane (PU), block copolymers of PLA-PCL |
| **Recycling** | Difficult | Easy (re-meltable) |
| **$R_r$** | Typically higher (>95%) | Variable (depends on hard-segment stability) |

**Influence on Performance:**

- **Chemically crosslinked SMPs** have a *fixed* network topology, giving them superior long-term recovery ($R_r > 95\%$) and creep resistance, but they are *not recyclable* and cannot be melt-processed. They are ideal for high-performance aerospace and biomedical implants.

- **Physically crosslinked SMPs** derive their netpoints from reversible physical interactions. They exhibit *larger recoverable strain* and can be re-melted and re-processed. However, their $R_r$ may decrease over multiple cycles due to gradual disruption of physical aggregates. They are favored for sustainable and biomedical applications.

**Hybrid Strategy:** Modern SMP design increasingly uses *dual networks* — chemical netpoints for permanent shape memory and physical netpoints for additional toughness and reprocessability.

*(Marks Distribution: 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for performance discussion, 2 marks for examples and hybrid strategy.)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\varepsilon_m$ and $\varepsilon_u$** in formulas — $\varepsilon_m$ is the maximum applied strain, $\varepsilon_u$ is the strain after unloading. Swapping them is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using wrong $R_r$ formula** — for the first cycle, use $\varepsilon_p(N-1) = 0$; do not blindly apply the general formula.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert % to decimals** — when computing $R_f$ and $R_r$, the strain ratios are unitless, but multiplying by 100 gives a percentage.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners explicitly allocate **2 marks for the schematic** in shape memory questions.
> 5. **Confusing $T_g$ with $T_m$ as switching temperatures** — both are valid, but the mechanism differs (glass transition vs. melting). Always specify which one applies to your polymer system.
> 6. **Not mentioning entropy** as the thermodynamic driving force — this is the *core conceptual answer* expected for "why does the shape recover?" questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Thermo-Stimulated SMP**

- ✅ Thermo-stimulated SMPs recover their permanent shape upon heating above a **switching transition temperature ($T_{sw}$)**.
- ✅ Two structural prerequisites: **Netpoints** (permanent memory) and **Switching segments** (reversible fixation).
- ✅ Switching can be **$T_g$-based** (amorphous phase vitrification) or **$T_m$-based** (crystalline phase melting).
- ✅ The **four canonical states**: Original → Deformed (high T) → Cooled (fixed) → Recovered (reheated).
- ✅ The driving force is **entropy increase** of polymer chains from oriented (low-$S$) to coiled (high-$S$) conformations.
- ✅ **Shape fixity ($R_f$)** = $\varepsilon_u / \varepsilon_m \times 100\%$ — measures temporary shape retention.
- ✅ **Shape recovery ($R_r$)** = $(\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p) / \varepsilon_u \times 100\%$ — measures permanent shape recovery.
- ✅ **Cyclic $R_r(N)$** uses $\varepsilon_p(N-1)$ as the reference — accounts for accumulated plastic deformation.
- ✅ The neo-Hookean constitutive law $\sigma = n k_B T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ governs rubbery-state SMP behavior.
- ✅ The storage modulus drops by **2–3 orders of magnitude** at $T_g$, enabling the switching action.
- ✅ **Chemically crosslinked** SMPs: higher $R_r$, non-reprocessable; **Physically crosslinked**: larger recoverable strain, reprocessable.
- ✅ Recovery time follows **Arrhenius kinetics**: $\tau_{rec} \propto \exp(E_a / RT)$ — a 25–30°C increase above $T_g$ speeds up recovery by ~10×.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications** require $T_{sw} \approx 37$–$40°\text{C}$ (body-compatible).
- ✅ Compared to **SMAs**, SMPs offer lower density, higher recoverable strain (up to 800%), lower cost, and easier processing.
- ✅ **4D printing** uses SMPs as printable inks to create shape-changing structures post-printing.
- ✅ The first cycle typically has the lowest $R_r$ due to chain disentanglement; subsequent cycles stabilize (training effect).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric-Stimulated SMP
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric-Stimulated SMP

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electric-Stimulated Shape Memory Polymers (ES-SMPs)** are a class of *active* or *stimuli-responsive* smart polymeric composites that can recover their original (permanent) shape from a mechanically deformed (temporary) state upon the application of an **external electric field or electrical current**. Unlike conventional thermally-triggered SMPs which rely on external heating sources (hot air, oil bath, water), ES-SMPs are engineered by embedding **electrically conductive fillers** into an insulating SMP matrix, thereby converting electrical energy directly into thermal energy via **Joule heating**. This internal heat generation raises the local temperature above the **switching transition temperature ($T_{sw}$)** of the polymer, activating the entropic elasticity of the network chains and triggering the shape recovery mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Electric-Stimulated SMPs belong to the family of "Indirectly Heated" shape memory polymers. The heat is generated *within* the material itself, making them ideal for remote actuation, biomedical implants, and aerospace deployable structures where external thermal sources are impractical.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Self-Heating Sandwich"

Imagine a regular sandwich (the SMP matrix) wrapped in a thin layer of electrically conductive foil (the conductive filler network). Now, plug a small battery to this foil. When the current flows:

- The foil heats up **from the inside**, just like the heating coil inside an electric kettle.
- This internal heat softens the sandwich's "memory" — the bread becomes pliable.
- Once the current is switched off and the sandwich cools, it "remembers" and springs back to its original shape.

The filler network acts as an internal micro-heater, transforming the SMP into a self-heating, electricity-actuated smart material.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standards

- **$T_{sw}$ (Switching Temperature):** The critical thermal transition (glass transition $T_g$ or melting $T_m$) that activates the shape memory effect. Typical range: **40 °C – 120 °C**.
- **Percolation Threshold ($P_c$):** The minimum volume fraction of conductive filler required to form a continuous conductive network. Typical values: **0.1 wt% – 5 wt%** depending on filler geometry.
- **Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma$):** Expressed in **S/m (Siemens per meter)** or **S/cm**. SMPs without fillers: $\sigma \approx 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-16}$ S/m (insulators).
- **Shape Fixity Ratio ($R_f$) and Shape Recovery Ratio ($R_r$):** Quantify the ability to fix and recover the temporary shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always specify whether the conductive pathway is formed by *electronic conduction* (metallic/CNT fillers) or *ionic conduction* (electrolyte-doped polymers). This distinction is frequently tested in Part A questions.

## 1.4 Classification of Electric-Stimulated SMPs

| Type | Conductive Filler | Conduction Mechanism | Typical Conductivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon-Based Composite | CNTs, Carbon Black, Graphene | Electron tunneling/hopping | $10^{-4}$ to $10^2$ S/m |
| Metal-Particle Composite | Ag, Au, Ni nanoparticles | Direct metallic contact | $10^{-3}$ to $10^4$ S/m |
| Conductive Polymer Blend | PEDOT:PSS, Polypyrrole | Intrinsic electronic | $10^2$ to $10^4$ S/m |
| Hybrid Filler | CNT + AgNW, Graphene + Ni | Synergistic percolation | $10^{-1}$ to $10^3$ S/m |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Four-Stage Working Cycle

The electric-stimulated shape memory effect operates through a coordinated four-stage cycle:

**Stage 1: Programming (Above $T_{sw}$)**
The SMP composite is heated above its switching temperature using Joule heating ($I^2R$ dissipation). Above $T_{sw}$, the molecular chains in the switching segment become mobile and rubbery. An external mechanical load is applied to deform the material into the desired temporary shape.

**Stage 2: Cooling & Fixation (Below $T_{sw}$)**
While maintaining the deformation load, the electric current is switched off. The composite cools below $T_{sw}$ naturally (or with forced convection). The switching segments vitrify or crystallize, locking the molecular chains in the deformed configuration. The external load can now be removed safely — the shape is **fixed**.

**Stage 3: Electrical Re-Activation**
When shape recovery is desired, an external voltage is reapplied across the composite. Current flows through the percolating conductive network, generating Joule heat. The temperature rises above $T_{sw}$, restoring molecular mobility.

**Stage 4: Recovery**
The elastic entropy-driven recovery force, stored in the network during programming, drives the material back to its original permanent shape. Current is removed once recovery is complete.

## 2.2 Why Conductive Fillers Are Essential

Pure SMPs (e.g., polyurethane, epoxy, polynorbornene) are inherently **electrical insulators** with conductivities of $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-16}$ S/m. Passing current through such materials is impossible without inducing dielectric breakdown. Adding conductive fillers:

1. Forms a **percolation network** of electron-conducting pathways.
2. Provides a means for **Joule heating** — the conversion of electrical energy to thermal energy.
3. Often enhances **mechanical strength** and **thermal conductivity** of the composite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Percolation Phenomenon:** Below the percolation threshold $P_c$, filler particles are isolated, and conductivity remains low. At $P \geq P_c$, a continuous conductive pathway spans the entire matrix, causing a dramatic (often 8–10 orders of magnitude) jump in conductivity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Joule's Law (Power Dissipation)** | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$ | Electrical power converted to heat | W (Watts) |
| **Heat Generated (Energy Basis)** | $Q = V I t = I^2 R t$ | Total thermal energy generated | J (Joules) |
| **Temperature Rise Equation** | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m c_p} = \dfrac{V I t}{m c_p}$ | Temperature increase due to Joule heating | K or °C |
| **Power Density Form** | $q = \dfrac{V^2 \sigma}{L^2}$ | Volumetric heat generation rate | W/m³ |
| **Percolation Scaling Law** | $\sigma = \sigma_0 (P - P_c)^t$ | Conductivity above percolation threshold | S/m |
| **Resistivity of Composite** | $\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{P_c}{P}$ for $P > P_c$ | Effective composite resistivity | $\Omega \cdot$m |
| **Shape Recovery Ratio** | $R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_{p}}{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_{i}} \times 100\%$ | Ability to recover permanent shape | % |
| **Shape Fixity Ratio** | $R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_{load}} \times 100\%$ | Ability to fix temporary shape | % |
| **Newton's Cooling Law** | $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-ht/A}$ | Transient cooling profile | K |
| **Effective Medium Theory** | $\sigma_{eff} = \sigma_m \cdot \dfrac{(1+2\phi)/(1-\phi)}{1}$ | Conductivity with filler volume fraction $\phi$ | S/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention (Markdown Safe):** In all derivations below, mathematical absolute values are written as $\lvert x \rvert$ using `\lvert` and `\rvert` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. Always prefer $\lvert x \rvert$ over the plain vertical pipe $\vert x \vert$ inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Role of ES-SMP |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Deployable solar sails, antenna reflectors | Lightweight self-actuating hinges in space vacuum |
| **Biomedical** | Endovascular stents, drug delivery | Triggered via localized heating without surgery |
| **Soft Robotics** | Artificial muscles, grippers | Silent, precise, low-voltage actuation |
| **Smart Textiles** | Heat-responsive garments | Embedded conductive yarns for comfort |
| **Self-Healing Materials** | Micro-crack closure in composites | Localized Joule heating initiates repair |
| **4D Printing** | Adaptive structures | Post-deployment shape morphing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Joule Heating Temperature Rise

We start with the fundamental energy balance: the electrical energy dissipated as heat equals the thermal energy absorbed by the composite mass.

**Step 1 — State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a Solid (no phase change, no work):**
The electrical energy supplied in time $t$ equals the sensible heat absorbed:
$$Q_{electrical} = Q_{thermal}$$

**Step 2 — Write the electrical energy dissipation (Joule's Law):**
From Ohm's Law, $V = I R$. The power dissipated is $P = V I = I^2 R$. Integrating over time $t$:
$$Q_{electrical} = V I t = I^2 R t$$

**Step 3 — Write the sensible heat absorbed by the composite mass:**
For a composite of mass $m$ and specific heat capacity $c_p$, the temperature rise $\Delta T$ satisfies:
$$Q_{thermal} = m c_p \Delta T = m c_p (T - T_0)$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $\Delta T$:**
$$I^2 R t = m c_p (T - T_0)$$

$$T(t) = T_0 + \dfrac{I^2 R t}{m c_p}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of voltage and resistance:**
Using $I = V/R$, substitute:
$$T(t) = T_0 + \dfrac{V^2 t}{m c_p R}$$

This shows that **temperature rises linearly with time** under constant applied voltage, provided heat losses are negligible.

**Step 6 — Introduce heat losses (Newton's cooling) for the real case:**
When convective losses to the surroundings at $T_\infty$ are considered, the differential equation becomes:
$$\rho c_p V_{vol} \dfrac{dT}{dt} = I^2 R - h A_s (T - T_\infty)$$

where $\rho$ is density, $V_{vol}$ is volume, $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient, and $A_s$ is the surface area.

**Step 7 — Steady-state solution:**
At steady state, $dT/dt = 0$:
$$I^2 R = h A_s (T_{ss} - T_\infty)$$

$$T_{ss} = T_\infty + \dfrac{I^2 R}{h A_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To trigger shape recovery reliably, we require $T_{ss} \geq T_{sw} + 10$ °C for safety margin. Designers must balance voltage, filler concentration (resistance), and ambient cooling.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Percolation Conductivity Scaling

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
Let $P$ be the volume fraction (or weight fraction) of conductive filler, and $P_c$ be the percolation threshold — the critical concentration at which the first continuous conductive path forms.

**Step 2 — Statistical percolation model:**
Below $P_c$, the conductivity follows the dielectric-insulator regime. Above $P_c$, the conductivity follows a power-law scaling:
$$\sigma = \sigma_0 (P - P_c)^t \quad \text{for } P > P_c$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ is the intrinsic conductivity of the filler material (S/m).
- $t$ is the **critical exponent** — a universal constant depending on network dimensionality (typically $t \approx 1.95$ for 3D networks).

**Step 3 — Justification of the power-law:**
In the proximity of $P_c$, the correlation length $\xi$ of conductive clusters diverges as:
$$\xi \propto \lvert P - P_c \rvert^{-\nu}$$

with $\nu \approx 0.88$ for 3D. The conductivity scales as $\sigma \propto \xi^{-1}$ in this regime, leading to the power-law form.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example for KTU Problem:**
Given a CNT-epoxy SMP composite with:
- $\sigma_0 = 10^4$ S/m
- $P_c = 0.5$ wt%
- $t = 2.0$
- Actual filler loading $P = 2.0$ wt%

Compute the effective conductivity:
$$\sigma = 10^4 \times (2.0 - 0.5)^{2.0}$$
$$\sigma = 10^4 \times (1.5)^{2.0}$$
$$\sigma = 10^4 \times 2.25$$
$$\sigma = 2.25 \times 10^4 \text{ S/m}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**
A conductivity of $2.25 \times 10^4$ S/m is well above the insulator regime. The composite can now dissipate significant Joule heat at low applied voltages (typically 5–30 V).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shape Recovery Ratio ($R_r$) under Electrical Stimulation

**Step 1 — Define strain parameters:**
- $\varepsilon_{load}$: Strain immediately after loading (at $T > T_{sw}$).
- $\varepsilon_u$ (or $\varepsilon_{fixed}$): Strain after cooling and load removal (fixed temporary shape).
- $\varepsilon_p$ (or $\varepsilon_{recov}$): Residual strain after electrical recovery.

**Step 2 — Shape Fixity Ratio (after fixation):**
$$R_f = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u}{\varepsilon_{load}} \times 100\%$$

A perfect fixity yields $R_f = 100\%$, meaning no springback after load removal.

**Step 3 — Shape Recovery Ratio (after electrical recovery):**
$$R_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_u - \varepsilon_i} \times 100\%$$

where $\varepsilon_i$ is the initial strain (typically zero for unloaded original state).

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**
A SMP strip is bent from $\varepsilon_i = 0\%$ to $\varepsilon_{load} = 50\%$ at $T_{sw} + 20$ °C. After cooling, $\varepsilon_u = 49.8\%$. After electrical stimulation for 60 seconds, the residual strain is $\varepsilon_p = 2\%$.

Calculate:
$$R_f = \dfrac{49.8}{50} \times 100\% = 99.6\%$$

$$R_r = \dfrac{49.8 - 2}{49.8 - 0} \times 100\% = \dfrac{47.8}{49.8} \times 100\% \approx 95.98\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Note:** Recovery ratios above 95% are considered excellent for engineering deployment. Below 90% indicates poor network design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis

| Property | Thermally-Activated SMP | Electric-Stimulated SMP |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger Source | External heater (oven, IR) | Internal Joule heating |
| Response Time | Slow (minutes) | Fast (seconds) |
| Remote Operation | No | Yes (via wiring) |
| Spatial Control | Limited | Localized (per electrode) |
| Power Efficiency | Low | High (>70%) |
| Miniaturization | Difficult | Excellent |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate (circuit needed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
