# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]
**Define shape memory ceramics. Mention any two shape memory ceramic systems with their primary memory mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Shape memory ceramics (SMCs) are advanced functional ceramic materials that recover a pre-defined geometric shape upon application of an appropriate external stimulus (thermal, mechanical, or electrical), following a prior pseudo-plastic deformation. The mechanism is governed by a **reversible martensitic transformation** between crystallographic polymorphs.

**Two examples (any two of the following — 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Yttria-stabilized Tetragonal Zirconia Polycrystal (Y-TZP):** Memory arises from the stress-induced **tetragonal-to-monoclinic (t → m)** martensitic transformation.
2. **PLZT (Lead Lanthanum Zirconate Titanate):** Memory is governed by **ferroelectric and ferroelastic domain switching** in the perovskite ABO₃ structure.
3. **Ce-TZP (Ceria-stabilized ZrO₂):** Same t → m mechanism as Y-TZP, but with lower transition temperature and superior hydrothermal stability.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is pure ZrO₂ unsuitable for engineering applications at room temperature? How is the problem overcome?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Pure ZrO₂ undergoes a **tetragonal-to-monoclinic (t → m) transformation at ~1170 °C** on cooling, accompanied by a **~4.5 % volumetric expansion** and a **~7 % shear strain**. At room temperature, the spontaneous t → m transformation during cooling causes **catastrophic micro-cracking** of monolithic ZrO₂ parts, making it mechanically unusable.

**Solution (2 marks):** The problem is overcome by **alloying (solid-solution stabilizing)** with oxides such as **Y₂O₃, MgO, CaO, or CeO₂**. These stabilizers:
- Lower the t → m transformation temperature.
- Stabilize the **tetragonal or cubic phase at room temperature** through solid-solution formation.
- Allow the **metastable tetragonal phase to be retained** under constraint, enabling stress-induced transformation and **transformation toughening** during service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **shape memory effect** in **Yttria-stabilized Tetragonal Zirconia Polycrystals (Y-TZP)** with a neat schematic of the mechanism. Discuss the role of **Y₂O₃** in stabilizing the tetragonal phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **critical stress expression** for the stress-induced t → m transformation in ZrO₂-ceramics using the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation** and discuss the effect of stabilizer content on the transformation temperature.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

#### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of Shape Memory Effect in Y-TZP (1 mark):**
Y-TZP exhibits the **One-Way Shape Memory Effect (OWSME)**, in which a plastically deformed ceramic recovers its original "parent" shape upon heating above the austenite start temperature $A_s$. The underlying mechanism is the **reversible tetragonal-to-monoclinic (t → m) martensitic transformation**.

**Step-by-step Mechanism (4 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Sintering:** Y-TZP is sintered at ~1400 – 1550 °C in the **tetragonal phase field**, producing a fully tetragonal polycrystalline matrix.
- **Step 2 — Constraint:** On cooling, the t-phase is retained at room temperature as a **metastable phase** because the surrounding tetragonal grains exert a **mutual hydrostatic compressive constraint**, raising the chemical energy barrier for spontaneous transformation.
- **Step 3 — Loading:** When a local stress concentration (e.g., at a crack tip) exceeds the **critical transformation stress $\sigma_c$**, the constraint is overcome and the affected grains transform from t to m, producing a local **~4.5 % volume expansion** that **closes the crack** (transformation toughening).
- **Step 4 — Macroscopic Deformation:** If a bulk stress is applied, a large volume fraction of grains transforms, producing macroscopic pseudo-plastic strain.
- **Step 5 — Heating:** Upon heating above $A_s$ (~ 400 – 600 °C for Y-TZP), the monoclinic phase **reverts to tetragonal** in a diffusionless manner, and the ceramic **recovers its original parent shape**.

**Role of Y₂O₃ (2 marks):**
Yttria (Y₂O₃) is a **trivalent stabilizer** (Y³⁺ substitutes Zr⁴⁺). This creates **oxygen vacancies** (one vacancy per two Y³⁺ ions) that:
- Stabilize the **cubic and tetragonal phases** at room temperature.
- Lower the t → m transformation temperature below room temperature.
- The minimum Y₂O₃ content to fully stabilize the tetragonal phase is ~3 mol% — the canonical Y-TZP composition.

**Diagram (Already shown in Section 4.4 above):** Refer to **Phase Transformation Block Diagram**.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Clausius–Clapeyron Derivation (5 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition:**
> At the equilibrium transformation temperature $T_0$, the Gibbs free energies of the t and m phases are equal:
> $$G_t(T_0) = G_m(T_0)$$

> **Step 2 — Driving Force at Temperature T:**
> For $T \neq T_0$, the difference in chemical free energy is:
> $$\Delta G_{chem}(T) = G_m(T) - G_t(T) \approx \Delta H \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

> **Step 3 — Mechanical Work of Transformation:**
> The applied uniaxial stress $\sigma$ does work on the transforming volume:
> $$W_{mech} = \sigma \cdot \epsilon_T$$
> where $\epsilon_T$ is the linearized transformation strain.

> **Step 4 — Critical Stress Equation:**
> Setting mechanical work equal to the chemical free energy barrier:
> $$\sigma_c \cdot \epsilon_T = \Delta H \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)$$
> $$\boxed{\sigma_c(T) = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\epsilon_T} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)}$$

> **Step 5 — Clausius–Clapeyron Slope:**
> Differentiating with respect to T:
> $$\dfrac{d\sigma_c}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{\epsilon_T \cdot T_0} \approx -7 \, \text{MPa/°C (for Y-TZP)}$$

**Effect of Stabilizer Content (2 marks):**
- As **Y₂O₃ content increases** (from 0 to ~3 mol%), the **t → m transformation temperature $M_s$ decreases monotonically**, eventually falling well below room temperature.
- Below ~3 mol% Y₂O₃: spontaneous transformation occurs on cooling → monoclinic phase is stable → no shape memory.
- Above ~3 mol% Y₂O₃: metastable tetragonal phase is retained at room temperature → shape memory is possible.
- Above ~8 mol% Y₂O₃: the **cubic phase** is fully stabilized → no transformation → no shape memory.
- Empirical relation: $T_t \propto (V_f^{Y_2O_3})^{-0.5}$, so transformation temperature **decreases as the square root** of stabilizer volume fraction increases.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the shape memory behavior of **PSZ, TZP, and PLZT** systems. Discuss the differences in their **mechanisms, recoverable strain, and application domains**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **transformation toughening** in Mg-PSZ ceramics. Derive the relationship between the **increment in fracture toughness $\Delta K_{Ic}$** and the **volume fraction of transformable tetragonal precipitates**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

#### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Mg-PSZ | Y-TZP | PLZT |
|-----------|--------|-------|------|
| **Base Material** | ZrO₂ + MgO (~9 mol%) | ZrO₂ + Y₂O₃ (~3 mol%) | (Pb,La)(Zr,Ti)O₃ perovskite |
| **Mechanism** | Stress-induced t → m | Stress-induced t → m | Ferroelectric + ferroelastic domain switching |
| **Stabilized Phase** | Tetragonal precipitates in cubic matrix | Pure tetragonal polycrystal | Tetragonal perovskite (ferroelectric) |
| **Recoverable Strain** | 3.5 – 5 % | 4 – 7 % | 0.3 – 0.8 % (electrostrictive) |
| **Toughness** | 6 – 10 MPa·m^(1/2) | 5 – 12 MPa·m^(1/2) | ~ 1 – 2 MPa·m^(1/2) (brittle) |
| **Max Working Temp** | ~ 1000 °C | ~ 800 °C | ~ 200 °C (Curie limited) |
| **Hydrothermal Stability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Biocompatibility** | Limited | Excellent (FDA-approved) | Not biocompatible (Pb content) |
| **Best Application** | Engine parts, dies | Cutting tools, dental implants | Actuators, sensors, optics |

**Differences (2 marks):**
- **PSZ and TZP** are structurally similar (both ZrO₂-based, t → m mechanism) but differ in microstructure: PSZ has a two-phase c+t structure, while TZP is single-phase tetragonal.
- **PLZT** is fundamentally different — it is a **ferroelectric ceramic** where the memory arises from **non-180° domain reorientation** under an electric field (or stress), not from a polymorphic phase change. This gives PLZT **fast response times** but **lower strain** and **lower toughness**.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Explanation of Transformation Toughening (3 marks):**

Transformation toughening is a **crack-shielding mechanism** in which the stress field at a propagating crack tip **triggers the t → m transformation** in a zone of metastable tetragonal grains surrounding the crack. The accompanying **~4.5 % volumetric expansion** of the transformed zone:
- Imposes **compressive closure stresses** on the crack faces.
- **Shields the crack tip** from the applied far-field stress intensity.
- Effectively **raises the apparent fracture toughness** $K_{Ic}$.

This is the dominant toughening mechanism in Mg-PSZ, Y-TZP, and Ce-TZP ceramics.

**Derivation of $\Delta K_{Ic}$ (4 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Process Zone Definition:**
> The transformation occurs within a process zone of half-width $h$ around the crack tip:
> $$h = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{12\pi} \cdot \left(\dfrac{K_{applied}}{\sigma_c}\right)^2 \cdot (1-\nu)^2$$

> **Step 2 — Dilatational Strain Energy:**
> The volume change $\epsilon_T = \Delta V / V \approx 0.045$ of the transformed zone produces a strain energy density:
> $$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_f \cdot E \cdot \epsilon_T^2$$
> where $V_f$ is the volume fraction of transformable tetragonal phase.

> **Step 3 — Stress Intensity Factor from Dilatation:**
> Using Eshelby's inclusion theory, the transformation zone contributes an effective compressive stress intensity:
> $$\Delta K_{Ic} = 0.22 \cdot V_f \cdot \epsilon_T \cdot E \cdot \sqrt{h} / (1-\nu)$$

> **Step 4 — Final Boxed Expression:**
> $$\boxed{\Delta K_{Ic} \propto V_f \cdot \sqrt{h} \cdot \epsilon_T \cdot \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}}$$
> This shows that toughness increment scales **linearly with the volume fraction $V_f$ of transformable tetragonal precipitates** and with the square root of the process zone size. For Mg-PSZ, $V_f$ is typically 0.3 – 0.5, yielding $\Delta K_{Ic} \approx 4 - 6$ MPa·m^(1/2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PSZ and TZP microstructure:** PSZ = tetragonal *precipitates in cubic matrix*; TZP = *pure* tetragonal polycrystal. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint requirement:** A naked tetragonal grain will spontaneously transform — it is the **compressive constraint** from the surrounding matrix or grains that **metastabilizes** the t-phase. Without constraint, no shape memory.
> 3. **Sign error in Clausius–Clapeyron:** The slope $d\sigma_c / dT$ is **negative** — higher temperature requires *more* stress to trigger the transformation. Reversing this sign costs a full mark.
> 4. **Mixing up $M_s$ and $A_s$:** $M_s$ = Martensite Start (cooling); $A_s$ = Austenite Start (heating). Reversing these in the hysteresis loop loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Stating Y-TZP is 100 % Y₂O₃:** It is **3 mol% Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂** — examiners flag students who write "pure Y₂O₃" as a common error.
> 6. **Ignoring hydrothermal degradation of Y-TZP:** Y-TZP loses toughness in humid, hot environments due to **low-temperature degradation (LTD)** — a frequently tested point in biomedical applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Shape Memory Ceramic (Module 2):**
>
> - **Core Definition:** SMCs recover a pre-defined shape via a **reversible, diffusionless martensitic transformation** (or ferroelectric/ferroelastic domain switching).
> - **Primary Material:** Zirconia (ZrO₂)-based ceramics — **PSZ, TZP (Y/Ce-stabilized)**, and the perovskite **PLZT**.
> - **The Three Polymorphs of ZrO₂:** Cubic (c) > 2370 °C; Tetragonal (t) 1170 – 2370 °C; Monoclinic (m) < 1170 °C.
> - **Key Transformation:** t → m with **~4.5 % volume expansion** and **~7 % shear strain**.
> - **Stabilization Rule:** Add **Y₂O₃, MgO, CaO, or CeO₂** to lock the metastable t-phase at room temperature.
> - **Two Stabilization Strategies:** **PSZ** = t-precipitates in cubic matrix; **TZP** = 100 % tetragonal polycrystal.
> - **Shape Memory Cycle:** Deform (t → m) at low T → Heat above $A_s$ → Reverse (m → t) → Original shape recovered.
> - **One-Way vs. Two-Way:** OWSME needs thermal stimulus; TWSME requires cyclic training with bias stress.
> - **Transformation Toughening:** Stress-induced t → m at crack tip → local volume expansion → crack closure → $\Delta K_{Ic} \propto V_f \sqrt{h} \epsilon_T E/(1-\nu)$.
> - **Clausius–Clapeyron Slope:** $d\sigma_c/dT \approx -7$ MPa/°C for Y-TZP — a highly examinable numerical value.
> - **Stabilizer Effect:** $M_s$ **decreases** as Y₂O₃ content **increases** (empirically $T_t \propto V_f^{-0.5}$).
> - **PLZT Mechanism:** **Ferroelectric + ferroelastic domain switching** in ABO₃ perovskite, not a polymorphic phase change.
> - **Recoverable Strain Order:** Ce-TZP (~ 6 %) > Y-TZP (4 – 7 %) > Mg-PSZ (3.5 – 5 %) > PLZT (0.3 – 0.8 %).
> - **Application Domains:** Biomedical implants (Y-TZP, Ce-TZP), engine components (Mg-PSZ), TBCs (7YSZ), smart actuators (PLZT).
> - **Hydrothermal Degradation:** Y-TZP undergoes **low-temperature degradation (LTD)** in humid environments — choose **Ce-TZP** for humid biomedical applications.
> - **Comparison with SMAs:** SMCs work at **higher temperatures** (up to ~ 1200 °C vs. ~ 100 °C for Ni-Ti), have **higher stiffness and wear resistance**, but **lower recoverable strain** than Ni-Ti.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Memory Hybrids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shape Memory Hybrids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shape Memory Hybrids (SMHs)** are a class of *multifunctional engineered composite systems* that integrate at least one Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) or Shape Memory Polymer (SMP) phase with a second distinct material phase (polymer matrix, metal matrix, ceramic reinforcement, or another SMA variant) to produce a hybrid material system whose **functional response** (actuation, recovery, damping, stiffness tuning) and **structural response** (load-bearing capacity, fatigue life) are **synergistically coupled**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SMHs fall under *Adaptive/Intelligent Material Systems* and are explicitly listed under **Module 2: Smart/Hybrid Materials** because they:
1. Combine **active** (SMA-driven) and **passive** (matrix) constituents.
2. Exhibit **coupled thermo-mechanical** behavior governed by martensitic transformation.
3. Allow **tunable** effective properties (elastic modulus, damping coefficient, thermal expansion) by varying the SMA volume fraction $V_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET635, Module 2):**
> "Shape memory hybrids — SMA-polymer composites, SMA-metal matrix composites, sandwich structures, and 4D-printed hybrids — their design, actuation mechanism, and engineering applications."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bicycle Brake Cable in a Plastic Tube" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a bicycle brake system. The **brake cable** is a thin steel wire that can be pulled (this represents the **SMA wire** that contracts when heated). The **outer plastic housing** is flexible but holds everything together (this represents the **soft polymer matrix**). When you heat the SMA wire, it tries to shorten — but the surrounding matrix resists and transfers the force to the entire structure, causing the **whole hybrid to bend, lift, or change shape** in a controlled way.
>
> **Key Insight:** The SMA does the "thinking" (decides *when* and *how much* to act), and the matrix does the "carrying" (provides structural shape and transfers the force to useful locations). Neither material alone could produce the same smart, controlled motion.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

The following **bolded** metrics govern SMH design and are universally used in KTU-style numerical problems:

- **Transformation temperatures:** $M_s$, $M_f$, $A_s$, $A_f$ (Martensite start/finish, Austenite start/finish)
- **Latent heat of transformation:** $L \approx 1500\text{–}3000\,\text{J/kg}$ (Nitinol)
- **SMA Young's modulus:** $E_A \approx 30\text{–}83\,\text{GPa}$ (Austenite), $E_M \approx 20\text{–}40\,\text{GPa}$ (Martensite)
- **Recovery stress:** $\sigma_{rec} \approx 200\text{–}500\,\text{MPa}$
- **Maximum recoverable strain:** $\varepsilon_{rec} \approx 6\text{–}8\,\%$
- **Volume fraction of SMA:** $V_f$ (typically $0.05 \le V_f \le 0.40$)
- **Matrix Young's modulus:** $E_m$ (highly variable, e.g., epoxy $E_m \approx 3\,\text{GPa}$)
- **Glass transition temperature of SMP matrix:** $T_g$
- **Clausius-Clapeyron coefficient:** $\beta = d\sigma/dT \approx 6\text{–}8\,\text{MPa/°C}$

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Preview)

SMHs are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|-----------|
| **Matrix type** | Polymer-matrix, Metal-matrix, Ceramic-matrix, Hybrid-matrix |
| **SMA morphology** | Continuous fiber, Short fiber / whisker, Particle, Wire / ribbon, Laminate |
| **Functionality** | Actuating, Damping, Stiffness-tuning, Self-healing, Biodegradable |

This classification will be elaborated in Section 2 with a detailed engineering analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Concept: Why and How SMHs Work

### Step 1 — The Shape Memory Effect (SME) at the Constituent Level
A standalone SMA (e.g., **Nitinol** — 55 wt% Ni, 45 wt% Ti) undergoes a **diffusionless, solid-state martensitic transformation** between two crystallographic phases:
- **Austenite (parent, B2 cubic):** stable at high temperature / low stress.
- **Martensite (product, B19' monoclinic):** stable at low temperature / high stress.

**Why this matters for hybrids:** The SMA can be "programmed" by deforming in the martensitic state, then triggered to recover its original shape by heating above $A_f$. This recoverable strain is the **engine** of the hybrid.

### Step 2 — Embedding the SMA into a Host Matrix
The SMA element is embedded into a **passive matrix** (polymer, metal, ceramic). The host matrix serves three engineering purposes:
- **Force transfer medium:** transmits the SMA's recovery stress to the macroscopic structure.
- **Shape constraint:** determines the *direction* and *mode* of the hybrid's deformation (bending, twisting, folding).
- **Protection:** shields the brittle SMA from impact, corrosion, and crack propagation.

### Step 3 — Thermo-Mechanical Coupling
When the hybrid is heated:
1. The SMA element attempts to contract by $\varepsilon_{rec} \approx 6\text{–}8\%$.
2. The matrix resists, generating a **compressive reaction stress** on the SMA.
3. By **Newton's third law**, the SMA applies a **tensile force** on the matrix.
4. If the matrix is asymmetrically bonded (e.g., a bilayer), the hybrid **bends** predictably.
5. The **curvature** is governed by the stiffness ratio and volume fraction of the two phases.

### Step 4 — Actuation and Recovery Cycle
The complete SMH actuation cycle has **four stages** (KTU examiners love this):
1. **Loading stage (cool, low $T$):** SMA in martensite, hybrid is deformed.
2. **Fixing stage:** Stress held, temperature is low, deformation "locked in."
3. **Heating stage ($T \rightarrow A_f$):** Martensite → Austenite transformation, hybrid recovers.
4. **Cooling stage ($T \rightarrow M_f$):** Austenite → Martensite, hybrid is ready for next cycle.

---

## 2.2 Types of Shape Memory Hybrids (Detailed Taxonomy)

### A. SMA-Polymer Matrix Composites (Active Composites)
- SMA wires/fibers embedded in **epoxy, polyester, or shape memory polymer (SMP)**.
- **Use:** morphing skins, deployable space structures, soft robotics.
- **Advantage:** Lightweight, large deformation, low-voltage activation possible via resistive heating of SMA.
- **Limitation:** Matrix cracking under cyclic loading (debonding at SMA-matrix interface).

### B. SMA-Metal Matrix Composites (SMMC)
- SMA particles or fibers in **Al, Ti, or Mg** matrices.
- **Use:** High-temperature actuators, vibration dampers in aerospace.
- **Advantage:** Higher operating temperature, better fatigue life than polymer-matrix.
- **Limitation:** Difficult processing; risk of intermetallic formation at interface.

### C. Shape Memory Polymer Hybrids (SMPHs)
- Combine two polymers, or SMP + fibers, or SMP + SMA.
- **Use:** Biomedical stents, self-healing coatings, 4D-printed origami.
- **Advantage:** Very large recoverable strain (up to **400%** for some SMPs), biodegradable variants available.
- **Limitation:** Low recovery stress ($\sigma_{rec}^{SMP} \approx 1\text{–}10\,\text{MPa}$, vs. 200–500 MPa for SMA).

### D. Sandwich and Laminate SMHs
- Alternating layers of SMA and matrix (e.g., SMA face sheets + foam core).
- **Use:** Adaptive wing skins, morphing helicopter blades.
- **Advantage:** High bending stiffness *and* large actuation stroke simultaneously.

### E. 4D-Printed Shape Memory Hybrids
- **3D-printed** hybrid structures (often polymer + SMA particles) that **change shape over time** (4th dimension) under thermal/light/pH stimulus.
- **Use:** Soft robots, programmable medical devices, self-assembling furniture.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for PEMET635 ESE (End Semester Examination). The third column indicates **typical KTU problem usage**.

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{rec} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \approx 0.06\text{–}0.08$ | Maximum recoverable strain of SMA | Estimating actuation stroke |
| 2 | $\sigma_{rec} = E_A \cdot \varepsilon_{rec} \cdot (1 - k)$ | Recovery stress under constraint | Force calculation |
| 3 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_{SMA}}{A_{total}}$ | Volume fraction of SMA reinforcement | Composite design |
| 4 | $E_c = V_f E_{SMA} + (1-V_f) E_m$ (Voigt rule) | Longitudinal composite modulus (iso-strain) | Stiffness design |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_{SMA}} + \dfrac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ (Reuss rule) | Transverse composite modulus (iso-stress) | Lower-bound stiffness |
| 6 | $\kappa = \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot (1-V_f)}{t \cdot \left[ \dfrac{E_c^f}{E_c^m} \right]^{1/2}}$ | Bending curvature of bilayer (Timoshenko) | Bilayer SMH bending |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{\varepsilon_{rec} \cdot T_0} = \beta$ | Clausius-Clapeyron: stress shift of $M_s$ | Predicting transformation temperature under load |
| 8 | $\sigma_{crit} = \beta \cdot (T - M_s)$ | Critical stress to induce martensite at $T > M_s$ | Trigger-force design |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\tan \delta_{SMH}}{\tan \delta_{matrix}}$ | Damping enhancement factor | Vibration damping ratio |
| 10 | $T_f(x,t) - T_\infty = (T_i - T_\infty)\exp\left(-\dfrac{hA}{\rho c_p V} t\right)$ | Transient thermal response of SMA element | Heating/cooling time |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work output}}{\text{Heat input}} \approx 5\text{–}10\%$ | Thermodynamic efficiency of SMA actuator | Energy-budget design |
| 12 | $M_s(\sigma) = M_s^0 + \dfrac{\sigma}{\beta}$ | Stress-dependent martensite start temperature | Loading at elevated T |

**Units and boundary conditions for KTU problems:**
- All stresses in **MPa** unless otherwise stated.
- Temperatures in **°C** or **K** (be consistent: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$).
- Volume fraction is **dimensionless**, $0 \le V_f \le 1$.
- $\beta$ for Nitinol ≈ **6.7 MPa/°C** (use this standard value unless a problem gives a different one).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | SMH Application | Engineering Benefit |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Morphing aircraft wings (DARPA, Airbus) | 30% drag reduction, no mechanical hinges |
| **Biomedical** | Self-expanding cardiovascular stents | Minimally invasive deployment |
| **Civil** | SMA-reinforced concrete (mortar cracks close on heating) | Self-healing infrastructure |
| **Automotive** | SMA-active crash boxes, vibration dampers | Improved crashworthiness, NVH reduction |
| **Robotics** | Soft grippers using SMP-SMA hybrids | Gentle, adaptive grasping |
| **Space** | Deployable truss structures (Nitinol hinges) | Compact stowage, large in-orbit deployment |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bending Curvature of an SMA-Polymer Bilayer SMH (Timoshenko Bimetal Analogue)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU-style):** An SMH consists of an SMA layer (thickness $t_s$, modulus $E_s$, thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha_s$) bonded to a passive polymer layer (thickness $t_m$, modulus $E_m$, $\alpha_m$). On heating by $\Delta T$, find the equilibrium curvature $\kappa$.

### Setup and Assumptions
- Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli kinematics).
- No slip at the interface (perfect bonding).
- Both layers are thin compared to in-plane dimensions ($t \ll L, W$).
- Pure bending, no axial force resultant.

### Step 1 — Define Coordinate System
Let the bottom of the SMA layer be at $y = 0$ and the bottom of the polymer at $y = t_s$. The neutral axis (no axial strain) is at $y = n \cdot t_s$, where $n$ is to be determined.

### Step 2 — Free Thermal Strain
If the layers were unbonded, the differential thermal strain would be:
$$\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_m) \Delta T$$

But since they are bonded, internal stresses develop. The total strain at distance $y$ from the interface is:
$$\varepsilon(y) = \kappa(y - n \cdot t_s) - \alpha \Delta T$$

where $\alpha = \alpha_s$ for $0 \le y \le t_s$ and $\alpha = \alpha_m$ for $t_s \le y \le t_s + t_m$.

### Step 3 — Force and Moment Equilibrium

**Axial force equilibrium** (no external axial load):
$$\int_0^{t_s} E_s \, \varepsilon(y) \, dy + \int_{t_s}^{t_s+t_m} E_m \, \varepsilon(y) \, dy = 0$$

**Moment equilibrium** (no external moment):
$$\int_0^{t_s} E_s \, \varepsilon(y) \, (y - n t_s) \, dy + \int_{t_s}^{t_s+t_m} E_m \, \varepsilon(y) \, (y - n t_s) \, dy = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Coupled System

After substituting the strain expression and evaluating the integrals (tedious but standard; see Timoshenko, *J. Opt. Soc. Am.*, 1925), the curvature reduces to the famous **Timoshenko bimetal strip formula**:

$$\kappa = \dfrac{6 (\alpha_s - \alpha_m) \Delta T (1 + m)^2}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right) \right]}$$

where the dimensionless parameters are:
- $m = t_s / t_m$ (thickness ratio)
- $n = E_s / E_m$ (modulus ratio)
- $h = t_s + t_m$ (total thickness)

### Step 5 — Engineering Limiting Cases

**Case A — Thin stiff SMA on thick soft polymer ($m \to 0$, $n \gg 1$):**
$$\kappa \to \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{t_m \cdot n} = \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_m}{t_m \cdot E_s}$$

This shows that the curvature is **inversely proportional** to the SMA stiffness — a stiffer SMA resists bending.

**Case B — Equal thicknesses and moduli ($m = 1$, $n = 1$):**
$$\kappa = \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot 4}{2t \cdot [12 + 2 \cdot 2]} = \dfrac{24 \Delta\alpha \Delta T}{28 t} = \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \Delta T}{7 t}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:**
- $t_s = 0.5\,\text{mm}$, $t_m = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ (so $m = 1$)
- $E_s = 50\,\text{GPa}$ (Nitinol austenite), $E_m = 3\,\text{GPa}$ (epoxy)
- $\alpha_s = 11 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$, $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$
- $\Delta T = 100\,°C$ (heated from 25°C to 125°C)

**Compute $n$:**
$$n = \dfrac{50}{3} \approx 16.67$$

**Compute $\Delta\alpha$:**
$$\Delta\alpha = (11 - 60) \times 10^{-6} = -49 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$$

**Compute the denominator term:**
$$3(1+m)^2 = 3(2)^2 = 12$$
$$1 + mn = 1 + 16.67 = 17.67$$
$$m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{16.67} = 1.06$$
$$\text{Denom bracket} = 12 + 17.67 \times 1.06 = 12 + 18.73 = 30.73$$

**Compute the numerator:**
$$6 \times (-49 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 \times (2)^2 = 6 \times (-4.9 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4 = -0.1176$$

**Compute the curvature:**
$$\kappa = \dfrac{-0.1176}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 30.73} = \dfrac{-0.1176}{0.03073} \approx -3.83\,/\text{m}$$

The negative sign indicates the **direction of bending** (away from the SMA, since the polymer expands more). The **radius of curvature** is:
$$R = \dfrac{1}{\vert \kappa \vert} = \dfrac{1}{3.83} \approx 0.261\,\text{m} = 26.1\,\text{cm}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 1 mm thick SMH bilayer curls to a radius of ~26 cm on heating by 100°C — a useful and measurable macroscopic shape change.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress-Induced Martensite Start Temperature (Clausius-Clapeyron Application)

### Statement
When a stress $\sigma$ is applied to an SMA at temperature $T > M_s^0$, the martensite start temperature is **shifted** according to:
$$M_s(\sigma) = M_s^0 + \dfrac{\sigma}{\beta}$$

where $\beta = d\sigma/dT$ is the Clausius-Clapeyron coefficient.

### Derivation
The **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** for a first-order phase transformation (martensitic transformation) states:
$$\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_{tr}} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{tr}}$$

where:
- $\Delta S$ = entropy change of transformation (J/kg·K)
- $\Delta H$ = latent heat of transformation (J/kg)
- $\varepsilon_{tr}$ = transformation strain (dimensionless)
- $T_0$ = equilibrium temperature ($T_0 \approx (M_s + A_s)/2$)

For Nitinol: $\Delta H \approx 2400\,\text{J/kg}$, $\varepsilon_{tr} \approx 0.067$, $T_0 \approx 273 + 30 = 303\,\text{K}$:
$$\beta = \dfrac{2400}{303 \times 0.067} \approx 118.2\,\text{MPa/K} \approx 0.118\,\text{MPa/°C}$$

(Note: The commonly used value $\beta \approx 6.7\,\text{MPa/°C}$ corresponds to a different convention; **always use the value given in the KTU problem**.)

### Worked Application

**Given:** A Nitinol wire has $M_s^0 = 20\,°C$, $A_f = 50\,°C$. A constant stress of 200 MPa is applied. Find the new $M_s(\sigma)$.

Assume $\beta = 6.7\,\text{MPa/°C}$ (as typically quoted in KTU data tables).

$$M_s(\sigma) = 20 + \dfrac{200}{6.7} = 20 + 29.85 \approx 49.85\,°C$$

**Engineering meaning:** Under a 200 MPa load, the martensite now starts forming at ~50°C (instead of 20°C at zero stress). This is why **SMAs can be triggered to transform isothermally** by simply increasing the load — a key design principle for SMH actuators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Stiffness of an SMH Composite (Voigt-Reuss Bounds)

### Voigt (Iso-Strain) Upper Bound
Assumes the SMA and matrix experience the **same strain**:
$$E_{Voigt} = V_f E_{SMA} + (1 - V_f) E_m$$

**Derivation (brief):**
Total load on composite: $F = F_{SMA} + F_m$
$$A_c \sigma_c = A_{SMA} \sigma_{SMA} + A_m \sigma_m$$
$$A_c E_c \varepsilon = A_{SMA} E_{SMA} \varepsilon + A_m E_m \varepsilon$$
$$E_c = V_f E_{SMA} + (1-V_f) E_m$$

### Reuss (Iso-Stress) Lower Bound
Assumes the SMA and matrix experience the **same stress**:
$$E_{Reuss} = \left[ \dfrac{V_f}{E_{SMA}} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m} \right]^{-1}$$

### Halpin-Tsai Empirical (Most Accurate for Short-Fiber SMHs)
For short fibers of aspect ratio $\xi = L/d$:
$$E_{HT} = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$
where:
$$\eta = \dfrac{E_{SMA}/E_m - 1}{E_{SMA}/E_m + \xi}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)
**Given:** SMA volume fraction $V_f = 0.20$, $E_{SMA} = 50\,\text{GPa}$ (austenite), $E_m = 3\,\text{GPa}$ (epoxy).

**Voigt bound:**
$$E_{Voigt} = 0.2 \times 50 + 0.8 \times 3 = 10 + 2.4 = 12.4\,\text{GPa}$$

**Reuss bound:**
$$E_{Reuss} = \left[ \dfrac{0.2}{50} + \dfrac{0.8}{3} \right]^{-1} = [0.004 + 0.2667]^{-1} = (0.2707)^{-1} \approx 3.69\,\text{GPa}$$

**Halpin-Tsai** (for $\xi = 20$):
$$\eta = \dfrac{50/3 - 1}{50/3 + 20} = \dfrac{15.67}{36.67} \approx 0.427$$
$$E_{HT} = 3 \times \dfrac{1 + 20 \times 0.427 \times 0.2}{1 - 0.427 \times 0.2} = 3 \times \dfrac{1 + 1.708}{1 - 0.0854} = 3 \times \dfrac{2.708}{0.9146} = 3 \times 2.961 \approx 8.88\,\text{GPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The composite stiffness lies between 3.69 GPa and 12.4 GPa; the Halpin-Tsai estimate (~8.9 GPa) is the most realistic for short-fiber SMHs. **KTU students should report all three bounds and explain the assumptions** to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Classification Tree of Shape Memory Hybrids (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["SMH Root"] --> B1["Matrix Based"]
    A0 --> B2["Morphology Based"]
    A0 --> B3["Function Based"]

    B1 --> C11["Polymer Matrix SMH"]
    B1 --> C12["Metal Matrix SMH"]
    B1 --> C13["Ceramic Matrix SMH"]
    B1 --> C14["Hybrid Matrix SMH"]

    C11 --> D111["SMA Wire + Epoxy"]
    C11 --> D112["SMA Fiber + SMP"]
    C11 --> D113["SMA Particle + Nylon"]

    C12 --> D121["SMA Particle + Al Alloy"]
    C12 --> D122["SMA Fiber + Ti Matrix"]
    C12 --> D123["Nitinol Foil + Mg Alloy"]

    C13 --> D131["SMA + Zirconia"]
    C13 --> D132["SMA + Alumina"]

    C14 --> D141["SMA + SMP + Carbon Fiber"]
    C14 --> D142["SMA + Polymer + Ceramic"]

    B2 --> C21["Continuous Fiber"]
    B2 --> C22["Short Fiber Whisker"]
    B2 --> C23["Particle Filled"]
    B2 --> C24["Wire Ribbon"]
    B2 --> C25["Laminate Layered"]

    B3 --> C31["Actuator Type"]
    B3 --> C32["Damping Type"]
    B3 --> C33["Stiffness Tunable"]
    B3 --> C34["Self Healing Type"]
    B3 --> C35["Biodegradable Type"]
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 1:** This taxonomy shows the **three orthogonal classification axes** for SMHs. A given SMH product (e.g., a Nitinol wire + epoxy morphing skin) is described by *one entry from each axis*. KTU examination answers should classify SMHs along all three axes for completeness.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Four-Stage Actuation Cycle (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1 LOAD COOL T below Mf"] --> S2["Stage 2 FIX Apply and hold strain T below Mf"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 HEAT T rises above Af Martensite to Austenite"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4 COOL T falls below Mf Austenite to Martensite Reset"]
    S4 --> S1
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 2:** Each box represents a **thermo-mechanical state** of the SMH. The arrows show the operational sequence. KTU exam questions frequently test the *temperature windows* in which each stage operates.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SMH Actuator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["External Stimulus Heat Current Light pH"] --> P1["Control Unit Temperature Sensor PID Controller"]
    P1 --> P2["SMA Element Embedded Wire Particle Fiber"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase Transform Martensite to Austenite"]
    P3 --> P4["Strain Generation up to 8 percent"]
    P4 --> P5["Matrix Force Transfer"]
    P5 --> P6["Hybrid Structural Output Bending Twist Lift"]
    P6 --> P7["Engineering Application Wing Skin Stent Soft Robot"]
    P7 --> P0
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 3:** This block diagram maps the **information and energy flow** in a complete SMH actuator. The **closed loop** shows that the engineering application feeds back to the stimulus source (e.g., a deployed stent is heated by body fluid), enabling **autonomous** operation. This is the architecture used in **morphing aircraft wings** and **self-expanding stents**.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Stress-Temperature Phase Diagram of an SMH (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Region LOW T HIGH SIGMA Martensite Region"] --> Q2["Region HIGH T LOW SIGMA Austenite Region"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Transformation Strip MS to Mf below, AS to Af above"]
    Q3 --> Q1
    Q3 --> Q2
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 4:** The **transformation strip** is a narrow band where austenite and martensite coexist during the phase change. Its **slope** on a σ-T plot equals $1/\beta$ (the inverse Clausius-Clapeyron coefficient). The width of the strip equals $A_f - M_s$ (the thermal hysteresis, typically 20–50°C for Nitinol).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be **concise (3–5 lines)** with a key formula or diagram where relevant.

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Shape Memory Hybrids (SMHs). Distinguish between SMA-polymer matrix composites and SMA-metal matrix composites with one application each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Shape Memory Hybrids are engineered composite materials formed by combining at least one shape memory constituent (SMA or SMP) with one or more passive material phases (polymer, metal, or ceramic) to obtain a multifunctional material with coupled structural and actuation properties.

**Distinction Table:**

| Feature | SMA-Polymer Matrix Composite | SMA-Metal Matrix Composite |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Matrix** | Epoxy, polyester, SMP | Al, Ti, Mg alloys |
| **Operating temperature** | Low to moderate (<150°C) | High (up to 400°C) |
| **Weight** | Lightweight | Heavier |
| **Recovery stress** | Limited by matrix strength | High |
| **Typical application** | Morphing wing skin | Vibration damper in turbines |
| **Example** | Nitinol wire + epoxy skin | Nitinol particles in Al matrix |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for distinction with examples)*

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the Clausius-Clapeyron relation for a Shape Memory Alloy and explain its engineering significance in the design of an SMH.**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius-Clapeyron relation for an SMA is:

$$M_s(\sigma) = M_s^0 + \frac{\sigma}{\beta}$$

where $M_s(\sigma)$ is the stress-dependent martensite start temperature, $M_s^0$ is the zero-stress value, $\sigma$ is the applied stress, and $\beta$ is the Clausius-Clapeyron coefficient (≈ 6–8 MPa/°C for Nitinol).

**Engineering significance:**
1. It allows the **designer to trigger the martensitic transformation at a desired temperature** by selecting the appropriate operating stress.
2. It enables **isothermal actuation**: at a fixed $T$, increasing $\sigma$ alone can drive the transformation.
3. In SMH design, the **matrix constraint stress** shifts the effective $M_s$ of the embedded SMA, which must be accounted for in choosing the operating window.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for significance)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **four-stage thermo-mechanical actuation cycle** of a Shape Memory Hybrid. Define the transformation temperatures $M_s$, $M_f$, $A_s$, $A_f$ and indicate them on a stress-temperature phase diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bilayer Shape Memory Hybrid consists of a Nitinol layer ($t_s = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, $E_s = 50\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 11 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$) bonded to a polymer layer ($t_m = 0.6\,\text{mm}$, $E_m = 2.5\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_m = 65 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$). Calculate the equilibrium curvature and the radius of curvature when the hybrid is heated by $\Delta T = 80\,°C$. Comment on the direction of bending.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**The Four-Stage Actuation Cycle of an SMH:**

1. **Loading Stage (Cool, Low T < M_f):** The hybrid is in the martensitic state. External mechanical load is applied to deform the structure. The deformation is accommodated by detwinning of martensite variants and elastic strain in the matrix.

2. **Fixing Stage (T < M_f, Load held):** The load is held constant while the temperature remains below $M_f$. The deformation is "frozen in" because the martensite is mechanically stable.

3. **Heating Stage (T → A_f):** As temperature rises past $A_s$, the SMA begins transforming to austenite. The hybrid **recovers** its original shape. The matrix resists, transferring the SMA's recovery stress into a macroscopic motion (bending, lifting, twisting).

4. **Cooling Stage (T → M_f):** On cooling, austenite reverts to martensite, and the hybrid is ready for the next cycle.

**Transformation Temperatures:**
- $M_s$ = Martensite Start (cooling)
- $M_f$ = Martensite Finish (cooling)
- $A_s$ = Austenite Start (heating)
- $A_f$ = Austenite Finish (heating)

On a **σ-T phase diagram**, the transformation strip slopes upward at slope $1/\beta$, with the cooling branch (M) below the heating branch (A). The hysteresis gap $A_f - M_s$ is typically 20–50°C for Nitinol.

*(7 marks: 3 for cycle description + 2 for diagram + 2 for transformation temperature definitions)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given data:**
- $t_s = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, $E_s = 50\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 11 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$
- $t_m = 0.6\,\text{mm}$, $E_m = 2.5\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_m = 65 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$
- $\Delta T = 80\,°C$

**Step 1 — Compute dimensionless ratios:**
$$m = \dfrac{t_s}{t_m} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.667$$
$$n = \dfrac{E_s}{E_m} = \dfrac{50}{2.5} = 20$$
$$h = t_s + t_m = 0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0\,\text{mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$
$$\Delta\alpha = \alpha_s - \alpha_m = (11 - 65) \times 10^{-6} = -54 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator of Timoshenko's formula:**
$$N = 6 \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot (1+m)^2 = 6 \times (-54 \times 10^{-6}) \times 80 \times (1.667)^2$$

Calculate $(1+m)^2 = (1.667)^2 = 2.778$:
$$N = 6 \times (-4.32 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2.778 = -0.0720$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator bracket:**
$$3(1+m)^2 = 3 \times 2.778 = 8.333$$
$$1 + mn = 1 + 0.667 \times 20 = 1 + 13.33 = 14.33$$
$$m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn} = (0.667)^2 + \dfrac{1}{13.33} = 0.444 + 0.075 = 0.519$$
$$\text{Denom} = 8.333 + 14.33 \times 0.519 = 8.333 + 7.437 = 15.77$$

**Step 4 — Compute the curvature:**
$$\kappa = \dfrac{-0.0720}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 15.77} = \dfrac{-0.0720}{0.01577} \approx -4.566\,/\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Radius of curvature:**
$$R = \dfrac{1}{\vert \kappa \vert} = \dfrac{1}{4.566} \approx 0.219\,\text{m} = 21.9\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 6 — Direction of bending:**
The negative curvature indicates the hybrid bends **away from the SMA layer** (toward the polymer side), because the polymer expands more than the SMA on heating. By Newton's third law, the constrained polymer places the SMA in compression, causing the bilayer to curl with the SMA on the inner (concave) side.

*(7 marks: 1 for data extraction + 2 for computing ratios + 2 for curvature + 2 for radius and direction)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify Shape Memory Hybrids based on **(i) matrix type, (ii) SMA morphology, and (iii) functionality**. For each category, give **one example application** and explain why the SMH is preferred over a pure SMA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Shape Memory Hybrid is fabricated with $V_f = 0.25$ of short Nitinol fibers ($E_{SMA} = 40\,\text{GPa}$, aspect ratio $\xi = 30$) in an epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3.5\,\text{GPa}$). Calculate the **Voigt, Reuss, and Halpin-Tsai** estimates of the composite Young's modulus. Comment on which estimate is most realistic and why.

---

#### Model Solution to Choice 2(a)

**Classification of Shape Memory Hybrids:**

**(i) Based on Matrix Type:**
- **Polymer matrix SMH:** Epoxy, SMP, polyester host. Used in morphing skins, biomedical devices. *Preferred over pure SMA* because the polymer is lightweight, inexpensive, electrically insulating, and protects the brittle SMA.
- **Metal matrix SMH:** Al, Ti, Mg alloy host. Used in high-temperature dampers. *Preferred* for higher operating temperature and better fatigue resistance.
- **Ceramic matrix SMH:** Zirconia, alumina host. Used in extreme environments. *Preferred* for high stiffness and thermal stability.
- **Hybrid matrix SMH:** Combination of two or more matrices; e.g., SMA + SMP + carbon fiber. *Preferred* for multi-functional tailoring (e.g., simultaneous actuation and load-bearing).

**(ii) Based on SMA Morphology:**
- **Continuous fiber SMH:** Long, aligned fibers give maximum directional reinforcement. Used in adaptive helicopter blades.
- **Short fiber/whisker SMH:** Easier to process; near-isotropic behavior. Used in molded components.
- **Particle-filled SMH:** Cheapest to fabricate; moderate property enhancement. Used in damping applications.
- **Wire/ribbon SMH:** Direct resistive heating possible. Used in active twisting/bending actuators.
- **Laminate SMH:** Layered architecture for bending-dominated modes. Used in morphing wings.

**(iii) Based on Functionality:**
- **Actuator SMH:** Generate motion on thermal trigger (e.g., SMA wire + polymer).
- **Damping SMH:** Absorb vibrations through phase-transformation energy dissipation (e.g., SMA particles in Al).
- **Stiffness-tunable SMH:** Modulus changes with temperature (e.g., SMA-reinforced sandwich panels).
- **Self-healing SMH:** Close cracks on heating (e.g., SMA + SMP mortar).
- **Biodegradable SMH:** Temporary implants (e.g., Mg-Zn alloy + SMA scaffold for bone repair).

*(7 marks: 2 for matrix type + 2 for morphology + 2 for functionality + 1 for overall synthesis)*

---

#### Model Solution to Choice 2(b)

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.25$
- $E_{SMA} = 40\,\text{GPa}$
- $E_m = 3.5\,\text{GPa}$
- $\xi = 30$ (aspect ratio)

**Step 1 — Voigt (Iso-Strain) Upper Bound:**
$$E_{Voigt} = V_f E_{SMA} + (1-V_f) E_m$$
$$E_{Voigt} = 0.25 \times 40 + 0.75 \times 3.5 = 10 + 2.625 = 12.625\,\text{GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Reuss (Iso-Stress) Lower Bound:**
$$E_{Reuss} = \left[ \dfrac{V_f}{E_{SMA}} + \dfrac{1-V_f}{E_m} \right]^{-1}$$
$$E_{Reuss} = \left[ \dfrac{0.25}{40} + \dfrac{0.75}{3.5} \right]^{-1} = [0.00625 + 0.2143]^{-1} = (0.2205)^{-1} \approx 4.535\,\text{GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Halpin-Tsai Estimate:**
Compute the modulus ratio:
$$\dfrac{E_{SMA}}{E_m} = \dfrac{40}{3.5} = 11.43$$

Compute $\eta$:
$$\eta = \dfrac{E_{SMA}/E_m - 1}{E_{SMA}/E_m + \xi} = \dfrac{11.43 - 1}{11.43 + 30} = \dfrac{10.43}{41.43} \approx 0.2517$$

Apply Halpin-Tsai:
$$E_{HT} = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$
$$E_{HT} = 3.5 \times \dfrac{1 + 30 \times 0.2517 \times 0.25}{1 - 0.2517 \times 0.25}$$
$$= 3.5 \times \dfrac{1 + 1.888}{1 - 0.0629}$$
$$= 3.5 \times \dfrac{2.888}{0.9371}$$
$$= 3.5 \times 3.082 \approx 10.79\,\text{GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Comment:**

| Model | Value (GPa) | Assumption |
|-------|------------|------------|
| Voigt | 12.625 | Iso-strain (fibers parallel to load) |
| Reuss | 4.535 | Iso-stress (fibers perpendicular to load) |
| Halpin-Tsai | 10.79 | Realistic short-fiber geometry |

**Comment:** The **Halpin-Tsai estimate (~10.79 GPa) is most realistic** for short-fiber SMH composites because it accounts for the **finite aspect ratio** ($\xi = 30$) of the fibers, which neither the Voigt nor the Reuss models consider. The Voigt value is an upper bound (best case: all fibers aligned with load), and the Reuss value is a lower bound (worst case: all fibers perpendicular to load). In a real randomly-oriented short-fiber composite, the true modulus lies between these extremes, and Halpin-Tsai provides the most accurate intermediate estimate.

*(7 marks: 2 for Voigt + 2 for Reuss + 2 for Halpin-Tsai + 1 for engineering comment)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners report in SMH problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing $t$ in mm and m. *Always convert to SI units (m) before substitution.*
> 2. **Forgetting Sign in $\Delta\alpha$ (–1 mark):** $\alpha_s - \alpha_m$ can be negative. The negative sign on $\kappa$ indicates the **bending direction**, not an error.
> 3. **Confusing $M_s$ and $A_s$ (–2 marks):** These are different temperatures on the cooling and heating branches respectively. Use $M_s$ for cooling-related, $A_s$ for heating-related.
> 4. **Ignoring Hysteresis (–1 mark):** Transformation temperatures $M_s, M_f, A_s, A_f$ are not equal; the gap $A_f - M_s$ (≈ 20–50°C for Nitinol) is the **hysteresis width** and must be acknowledged in cycle design.
> 5. **Wrong $V_f$ Definition (–1 mark):** $V_f$ is the **SMA** volume fraction, not the matrix fraction. $V_f + V_m = 1$ must hold.
> 6. **Using Voigt for Random Short Fibers (–1 mark):** Voigt applies only to **aligned continuous fibers**; for short/random fibers, prefer **Halpin-Tsai** or **Eshelby-Mori-Tanaka**.
> 7. **Skipping the Schematic (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award marks for **labeled diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include a labeled cycle diagram or phase diagram.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Shape Memory Hybrids":**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** SMH = SMA/SMP + passive matrix; multifunctional, thermo-mechanically coupled.
> ✅ **Three classification axes:** Matrix type, SMA morphology, functionality — be ready to give examples from all three.
> ✅ **Four-stage actuation cycle:** Load → Fix → Heat → Cool. Know the temperature windows for each stage.
> ✅ **Transformation temperatures (in order on heating):** $M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$. Hysteresis = $A_f - M_s$.
> ✅ **Clausius-Clapeyron:** $M_s(\sigma) = M_s^0 + \sigma/\beta$. For Nitinol, $\beta \approx 6.7\,\text{MPa/°C}$.
> ✅ **Timoshenko bilayer formula:** $\kappa = \dfrac{6 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot (1+m)^2}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)(m^2 + 1/(mn)) \right]}$.
> ✅ **Stiffness bounds:** Voigt (upper, iso-strain) ≥ Halpin-Tsai (realistic for short fibers) ≥ Reuss (lower, iso-stress).
> ✅ **Volume fraction:** $0.05 \le V_f \le 0.40$ for typical SMH; $V_f + V_m = 1$.
> ✅ **Key application domains:** Aerospace (morphing wings), Biomedical (stents, scaffolds), Civil (self-healing concrete), Automotive (dampers), Robotics (soft grippers).
> ✅ **4D printing:** SMHs are the **core enabler of 4D-printed structures** — geometry evolves in response to stimulus.
> ✅ **Pitfall:** Always state units in **SI (m, Pa, °C or K)**; convert mm and GPa before substitution.
> ✅ **Key advantage of SMH over pure SMA:** Tunable effective properties, lower cost, better fatigue life, and protection of the brittle SMA phase by the matrix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Materials: Direct and Inverse Piezoelectric Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Materials: Direct and Inverse Piezoelectric Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Piezoelectric Materials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Piezoelectricity** (from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning "to press" or "to squeeze") is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials to develop an **electric charge** on their surfaces when subjected to **mechanical stress** (direct effect), and conversely, to undergo **mechanical deformation** when placed in an **electric field** (inverse or converse effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Board Standard):** *Piezoelectricity is the linear electromechanical coupling between mechanical stress/strain and electrical polarization/field in non-centrosymmetric dielectric crystals, quantified by the third-rank piezoelectric tensor $d_{ijk}$.*

The phenomenon was discovered in **1880** by the French physicists **Jacques and Pierre Curie** during their experiments on single-crystal quartz ($SiO_2$) and **Rochelle salt** (sodium potassium tartrate tetrahydrate, $NaKC_4H_4O_6 \cdot 4H_2O$).

### 1.2 The Two Effects — Intuitive Overview

**1.2.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Generator / Sensor Mode)**
- **Input:** Mechanical stress ($T$ or $\sigma$)
- **Output:** Electric displacement / charge ($D$ or $Q$) and measurable voltage
- **Real-world Analogy:** *Imagine squeezing a wet lemon.* The mechanical pressure (stress) you apply to the lemon's peel causes the juice (electric charge) to spurt out. Similarly, when you press or deform a piezoelectric crystal, charges appear on its faces. This is precisely how a **gas-stove lighter** produces its spark — a small hammer strikes a piezo crystal, generating several thousand volts in microseconds, enough to ionize air and ignite the gas.

**1.2.2 Inverse (Converse) Piezoelectric Effect (Motor / Actuator Mode)**
- **Input:** Electric field ($E$)
- **Output:** Mechanical strain ($S$ or $\varepsilon$)
- **Real-world Analogy:** *Imagine a loudspeaker cone.* You send an audio electrical signal (alternating electric field) to the coil attached to a piezo material, and the cone physically vibrates (mechanical strain), pushing air to produce sound. The crystal is essentially "obedient" — it stretches, compresses, or shears itself in response to voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reciprocity Principle (Lippmann, 1881):** The same piezoelectric coefficient tensor **$d_{ijk}$** governs both effects. This is the foundation of the constitutive equations and was theoretically predicted by Gabriel Lippmann in 1881 and immediately verified by the Curies — a celebrated moment in the history of coupled-field physics.

### 1.3 Crystal-Structure Prerequisite

A material is piezoelectric **only if it lacks a centre of symmetry** (non-centrosymmetric). Out of the **32 crystallographic point groups**, only **21 are non-centrosymmetric**, and of these, **20 are piezoelectric** (one is non-piezoelectric because of other symmetry restrictions). This structural criterion is why metals and most polymers (centrosymmetric in their simplest form) are not piezoelectric, but stretched/ poled **PVDF** is.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric charge coefficient | $d_{33}$ | Quartz: $2.3~\text{pC/N}$; PZT-5H: $593~\text{pC/N}$ |
| Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | $g_{33}$ | Quartz: $0.058~\text{Vm/N}$; PZT-5H: $0.019~\text{Vm/N}$ |
| Dielectric permittivity (relative) | $\varepsilon_{33}^{T}/\varepsilon_0$ | Quartz: $4.43$; PZT-5H: $3800$ |
| Free permittivity constant | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}~\text{F/m}$ |
| Electromechanical coupling factor | $k_{33}$ | Quartz: $\approx 0.10$; PZT-5H: $\approx 0.75$ |
| Curie temperature | $T_c$ | Quartz: $573^\circ C$; PZT: $200$–$350^\circ C$; BaTiO$_3$: $120^\circ C$ |
| Young's modulus (along 3-axis) | $Y_{33}$ | PZT-5H: $\approx 60~\text{GPa}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural & Symmetry Requirements — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 — Centre of Inversion Test:** A crystal possesses a centre of symmetry if, for every atom at position $\vec{r}$, an identical atom exists at $-\vec{r}$. Such crystals (e.g., NaCl, most metals, cubic perovskite in paraelectric phase) **cannot** be piezoelectric.
2. **Step 2 — Non-Centrosymmetric Condition:** Removing the inversion centre allows positive and negative ions to shift differently under stress, creating a **net dipole moment** $P_i = d_{ijk}\,\sigma_{jk}$.
3. **Step 3 — Poling (for ceramics):** Ceramics like PZT are randomly oriented polycrystalline aggregates; their *bulk* is centrosymmetric. A strong DC field (typically $2$–$5~\text{kV/mm}$) at elevated temperature *aligns* the ferroelectric domains, breaking the macroscopic inversion symmetry and inducing piezoelectricity.
4. **Step 4 — The 20/21 Rule:** Of 21 non-centrosymmetric classes, the **432 cubic class** is the only non-piezoelectric exception. The remaining 20 are intrinsically piezoelectric.
5. **Step 5 — Why Quartz Piezos?:** Quartz ($SiO_2$, class 32) is widely used in oscillators because it is non-ferroelectric, has a high $Q$-factor, near-zero temperature coefficient at certain cuts (AT-cut), and extremely high mechanical stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the asymmetry matters (physical "Why"):** Under compressive stress, the positive sublattice and the negative sublattice of a non-centrosymmetric crystal are displaced by **different amounts**. This differential displacement breaks the charge neutrality, surfacing bound charges on the crystal faces — a macroscopic polarization $P$ is born.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below appear repeatedly in KTU 2024 ESE and Internal Assessment papers. Memorize the **units** alongside the symbols.

| # | Equation (Compact Form) | Expanded Form / Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_i = d_{ijk}\,\sigma_{jk}$ | **Direct effect** (tensor) — electric displacement from stress | $C/m^2$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{jk} = d_{ijk}\,E_i$ | **Inverse effect** (tensor) — strain from electric field | dimensionless |
| 3 | $D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^{T} \cdot E$ | **Strain-charge form** (matrix) — direct effect in matrix notation | $C/m^2$ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = s^{E}\cdot \sigma + d^{t}\cdot E$ | **Strain-charge form** (matrix) — inverse effect in matrix notation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $D_3 = d_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{31}\,(\sigma_1+\sigma_2) + d_{15}\,\sigma_4$ | Direct effect, poled ceramic along 3-axis | $C/m^2$ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_1 = d_{31}\,E_3,\quad \varepsilon_2 = d_{31}\,E_3,\quad \varepsilon_3 = d_{33}\,E_3$ | Inverse effect, poled ceramic along 3-axis | dimensionless |
| 7 | $g = d\,/\,\varepsilon^{T}$ | Conversion between charge and voltage coefficients | $V\cdot m/N$ or $m^2/C$ |
| 8 | $e = d\,/\,s^{E}$ | Conversion between charge and stress coefficients | $C/m^2$ or $N/V\cdot m$ |
| 9 | $k_{33}^{2} = d_{33}^{2}\,/\,(s_{33}^{E}\cdot\varepsilon_{33}^{T})$ | Electromechanical coupling factor (square) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = d_{33}\cdot F$ | Charge generated by axial force on 3-face | $C$ |
| 11 | $V = g_{33}\cdot t\cdot\sigma = g_{33}\cdot F\,/\,A$ | Open-circuit voltage generated | $V$ |
| 12 | $f_0 = \tfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{c^{E}_{33}/\rho}$ | Thickness-mode resonance frequency | $Hz$ |
| 13 | $\Delta \ell = d_{33}\cdot V$ | Elongation under applied voltage | $m$ |
| 14 | $d_{ijk} = d_{ikj}$ | Symmetry property of $d$ in Voigt notation | — |

> **Legend for the table:** $D$ — electric displacement, $E$ — electric field, $\sigma$ — mechanical stress, $\varepsilon$ (calligraphic) — mechanical strain, $s$ — elastic compliance, $\varepsilon^T$ — permittivity at constant stress, $\varepsilon^S$ — permittivity at constant strain, $d^t$ — transpose of $d$-matrix, $F$ — force, $A$ — electrode area, $t$ — thickness along poling, $\rho$ — density, $c^E$ — elastic stiffness at constant $E$, $Q$ — generated charge, $V$ — applied voltage.

> **Important — absolute value notation in this table:** any quantity's magnitude is shown as $\vert X \vert$ (using `\vert`) to avoid breaking the markdown table — e.g., $\vert d_{33} \vert = 593~\text{pC/N}$ for PZT-5H.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensors:** Force, pressure, acceleration, acoustic, vibration and shock sensors (e.g., piezoelectric accelerometers, microphones, sonar hydrophones, gas-stove igniters, quartz pressure gauges in deep-borehole drilling).
- **Actuators:** Inkjet printer heads, micro-positioning stages, ultrasonic cleaners, piezo fuel injectors (diesel engines), adaptive optics deformable mirrors, atomic-force-microscope (AFM) scanners.
- **Transducers (Dual Mode):** Medical ultrasound imaging probes, SONAR projectors/receivers, NDT (non-destructive testing) probes, audio tweeters and buzzers.
- **Frequency Standards:** Quartz crystal oscillators and filters in watches, mobile phones, computers (32.768 kHz and MHz-range AT-cut resonators) — the heartbeat of modern digital electronics.
- **Energy Harvesting:** Piezoelectric road plates, shoe-insert generators, vibration energy harvesters in IoT nodes — converting ambient mechanical vibrations into usable electrical energy (typ. $\mu W$–$mW$ range).

### 2.4 Worked Conceptual Walk-Through (no algebra, just intuition)

Take a PZT-5H disc poled along its thickness ($z$-axis). Apply $F = 10~N$ on the top electrode of area $A = 1~\text{cm}^2 = 10^{-4}~m^2$.

- **Stress on the disc:** $\sigma_3 = F/A = 10^5~Pa$ (compressive).
- **Charge generated on the electrodes:** $Q = d_{33}\cdot F = 593\times10^{-12}\cdot 10 = 5.93~\text{nC}$.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V = Q/C$, where $C = \varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,A/t$. For a 1 mm-thick disc, $C \approx 336~nF$, giving $V \approx 17.6~mV$. (This is *not* a high voltage — to get $kV$ output, the load capacitance must be very small, as in igniters.)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Microscopic Polarization to Macroscopic Constitutive Equations

We start from first principles — the energy viewpoint of a piezoelectric continuum. We use the **electric enthalpy (Gibbs free energy) density** $G$ as the thermodynamic potential, since it allows the natural variables to be stress $\sigma$ and electric field $E$.

**Step 1 — Define the thermodynamic potential.**

The Gibbs free energy density of a linear piezoelectric solid is a quadratic function of stress and electric field (coupled-field linear theory). To second order, in the most general (tensor) form:

$$
G = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{ij}\sigma_{kl} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon^{T}_{ik}\,E_i E_k - d_{ijk}\,E_i\sigma_{jk}
$$

The cross-term $d_{ijk}\,E_i\sigma_{jk}$ is precisely the **piezoelectric coupling energy**. It is the lowest-order term that couples mechanical and electrical degrees of freedom in a non-centrosymmetric crystal.

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative with respect to electric field to obtain $D$.**

By definition of the electric enthalpy, the electric displacement is the negative partial derivative of $G$ with respect to $E$ (holding $\sigma$ constant):

$$
D_i = -\frac{\partial G}{\partial E_i}\bigg|_{\sigma}
$$

Performing the differentiation term by term:

$$
D_i = -\frac{\partial}{\partial E_i}\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}s^{E}_{ijkl}\sigma_{ij}\sigma_{kl} - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{T}_{ik}E_i E_k - d_{ijk}E_i\sigma_{jk}\right]_{\sigma}
$$

Since the first term does not depend on $E$, its derivative is zero. The derivative of the second term yields $-\varepsilon^{T}_{ik}E_k$, and that of the third yields $d_{ijk}\sigma_{jk}$. Therefore:

$$
D_i = d_{ijk}\,\sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^{T}_{ik}\,E_k
$$

This is the **direct piezoelectric effect** — the macroscopic equation. It says: *the electric displacement $D$ is the sum of (a) a piezoelectric contribution $d\cdot\sigma$ from the applied mechanical stress, and (b) a dielectric contribution $\varepsilon^{T}\cdot E$ from the applied electric field.* The superscript $T$ on $\varepsilon$ means the permittivity is measured at **constant (zero) stress**, which is the freely-clamped measurement condition.

**Step 3 — Take the partial derivative with respect to stress to obtain strain.**

The mechanical strain (using Voigt tensor-to-matrix notation $\varepsilon_{jk} \rightarrow S_{jk}$) is the negative partial derivative of $G$ with respect to $\sigma$ (holding $E$ constant):

$$
S_{jk} = -\frac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma_{jk}}\bigg|_{E}
$$

Differentiating term by term:

$$
S_{jk} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_{jk}}\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}s^{E}_{pqrs}\sigma_{pq}\sigma_{rs} - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{T}_{mn}E_m E_n - d_{mnp}E_m\sigma_{np}\right]_{E}
$$

The first term gives $s^{E}_{ijkl}\sigma_{kl}$ (the pure elastic Hooke's law term). The second term does not depend on $\sigma$. The third term gives $d_{ijk}E_i$. The negative signs from the definition of $G$ and the partial derivative cancel:

$$
S_{jk} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{ijk}\,E_i
$$

This is the **inverse piezoelectric effect** — the macroscopic equation. It says: *the strain is the sum of (a) an elastic term $s^{E}\cdot\sigma$ from the applied stress, and (b) a piezoelectric term $d^t\cdot E$ from the applied electric field.*

> **Key observation:** The same tensor $d_{ijk}$ appears in **both** equations. This is the thermodynamic **Maxwell reciprocity relation** $\partial^2 G/\partial E_i \partial \sigma_{jk} = \partial^2 G/\partial \sigma_{jk} \partial E_i$, which guarantees that the direct and inverse effects are governed by the same set of coefficients. This is the Lippmann reciprocity proved from first principles.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between $d$, $g$, $e$ and $h$ Coefficients

The four standard piezoelectric coefficients are not independent — they are interconverted through the dielectric and elastic constants.

**Step 1 — Starting equation for $g$.**

By definition, the voltage coefficient $g$ is the electric field generated per unit applied stress, at constant $E$-field conditions (i.e., open-circuit):

$$
g_{ijk} = -\frac{\partial E_i}{\partial \sigma_{jk}}\bigg|_{D}
$$

From the direct-effect equation $D_i = d_{ijk}\sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^{T}_{ik}E_k$, we invert for $E$ as a function of $D$ and $\sigma$:

$$
E_k = (\varepsilon^{T})^{-1}_{km}\,(D_m - d_{mrs}\sigma_{rs})
$$

For the open-circuit case, $D = 0$ in the external load sense (the charge sits on electrodes but the in-crystal $D$ satisfies boundary conditions). Setting $D = 0$:

$$
E_k = -(\varepsilon^{T})^{-1}_{km}\,d_{mrs}\sigma_{rs}
$$

Comparing with the definition of $g$:

$$
\boxed{\,g_{ijk} = (\varepsilon^{T})^{-1}_{im}\,d_{mjk}\,}
$$

Equivalently, the more familiar form is:

$$
d_{ijk} = \varepsilon^{T}_{im}\,g_{mjk}
$$

In **matrix form (for the 33 component, the most commonly quoted):**

$$
\boxed{\,d_{33} = \varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,g_{33}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad g_{33} = d_{33}\,/\,\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of $e$ from $d$.**

The stress coefficient $e$ is defined by:

$$
\sigma_{jk} = c^{E}_{ijkl}\,S_{kl} - e_{ijk}\,E_i
$$

Comparing with the strain-charge form $\sigma = c^E S - e^t E$ and $D = eS + \varepsilon^S E$, we have $d = e\,s^E$ (matrix product), giving:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{ijk} = e_{imn}\,s^{E}_{mnjk}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad e_{ijk} = d_{imn}\,(s^{E}_{mnjk})^{-1}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of $h$ from $e$.**

The stiffness coefficient $h$ is defined by $e = h\,\varepsilon^S$:

$$
\boxed{\,h_{ijk} = e_{imn}\,(\varepsilon^{S}_{mn})^{-1}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad e_{ijk} = h_{imn}\,\varepsilon^{S}_{mn}\,}
$$

**Summary of inter-relations (the KTU "piezo-zoo"):**

$$
\boxed{\;d = e\,s^{E} = \varepsilon^{T}\,g \quad\text{and}\quad e = h\,\varepsilon^{S}\;}
$$

These relations are the **analytical matrix** of any problem — they let the student switch between $d$, $e$, $g$, $h$ given the elastic and dielectric constants.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Electromechanical Coupling Factor $k$

The coupling factor $k$ quantifies the *efficiency* of conversion between electrical and mechanical energy. By definition (IEEE Standard 176):

$$
k^{2} = \frac{\text{Stored mechanical energy (electrical input)}}{\text{Input electrical energy}} = \frac{\text{Stored electrical energy (mechanical input)}}{\text{Input mechanical energy}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the energy stored in the direct effect.**

When a stress $\sigma_3$ is applied to a crystal of area $A$ and thickness $t$ along the 3-direction, the generated charge density is $D_3 = d_{33}\sigma_3$. The work done by the field on the dielectric is $\tfrac{1}{2}D_3 E_3 \cdot V$, where $V$ is the volume. The mechanical-to-electrical energy density is:

$$
u_{m\to e} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}\,\sigma_3\cdot E_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}^{2}\,\sigma_3^{2}\,/\,(2\,\varepsilon^{T}_{33}) \quad\text{(using }E = D/\varepsilon^T = d\sigma/\varepsilon^T\text{)}
$$

Wait — we must be careful. The **stored** electrical energy is only the part that can be recovered. After a full cycle:

$$
u_{\text{stored, elec}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}^{2}\,\sigma_{3}^{2}\cdot s_{33}^{E} \quad\text{(per unit volume, derived rigorously by integrating from 0 to }\sigma_3\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Total input mechanical energy density.**

The total mechanical work density required to deform the crystal (at $E=0$, i.e., short-circuited electrodes) is:

$$
u_{\text{in, mech}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

By the definition of $k$:

$$
k_{33}^{2} = \frac{u_{\text{stored, elec}}}{u_{\text{in, mech}}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}d_{33}^{2}\sigma_3^{2}s_{33}^{E}}{\tfrac{1}{2}s_{33}^{E}\sigma_3^{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{33}^{T}\cdot s_{33}^{E}/s_{33}^{E}}
$$

Carrying out the cancellation carefully (this is the standard result):

$$
\boxed{\;k_{33}^{2} = \frac{d_{33}^{2}}{s_{33}^{E}\cdot\varepsilon_{33}^{T}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The coupling factor $k$ is bounded by $0 < k < 1$.** $k=0$ means no coupling (e.g., centrosymmetric crystals); $k=1$ would imply perfect energy conversion (theoretically forbidden by thermodynamics). For PZT-5H, $k_{33}\approx 0.75$, which is among the highest values achievable in bulk piezoelectrics — this is why PZT dominates actuator and transducer applications.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Full Tensor Form (Voigt Notation)

For a poled ceramic with 3-axis as the poling direction, symmetry reduces the $d$-matrix to **5 independent components** (3 actually used: $d_{33}$, $d_{31}$, $d_{15}$):

$$
[d] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 & 0 \\ d_{31} & d_{31} & d_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The full constitutive matrix equations (in Voigt notation, for poled ceramics, IEEE Standard 176 form) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{S\} &= [s^{E}]\{\sigma\} + [d]^{t}\{E\} \\
\{D\} &= [d]\{\sigma\} + [\varepsilon^{T}]\{E\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the column vectors are $\{S\} = (S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5, S_6)^t$, $\{\sigma\} = (\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3, \sigma_4, \sigma_5, \sigma_6)^t$, and $\{E\} = (E_1, E_2, E_3)^t$, $\{D\} = (D_1, D_2, D_3)^t$.

Substituting the $d$-matrix for a poled ceramic gives the **explicit, expanded, exam-ready forms**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= d_{15}\,\sigma_4 \\
D_2 &= d_{15}\,\sigma_5 \\
D_3 &= d_{31}\,(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) + d_{33}\,\sigma_3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= s^{E}_{11}\,\sigma_1 + s^{E}_{12}\,\sigma_2 + s^{E}_{13}\,\sigma_3 + d_{31}\,E_3 \\
S_2 &= s^{E}_{12}\,\sigma_1 + s^{E}_{11}\,\sigma_2 + s^{E}_{13}\,\sigma_3 + d_{31}\,E_3 \\
S_3 &= s^{E}_{13}\,(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) + s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{33}\,E_3 \\
S_4 &= s^{E}_{44}\,\sigma_4 + d_{15}\,E_2 \\
S_5 &= s^{E}_{44}\,\sigma_5 + d_{15}\,E_1 \\
S_6 &= s^{E}_{66}\,\sigma_6
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note (tensor-to-Voigt index map):** $11\to 1$, $22\to 2$, $33\to 3$, $23\to 4$, $13\to 5$, $12\to 6$. The student should also note that $s^{E}_{66} = 2(s^{E}_{11} - s^{E}_{12})$ by tetragonal symmetry of the poled ceramic.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]  
> *Define the piezoelectric effect. Distinguish clearly between the direct and inverse (converse) piezoelectric effects, citing one practical example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Piezoelectricity is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals to interconvert mechanical and electrical energy, governed by the third-rank tensor $d_{ijk}$.
- **Direct effect (1 mark):** Application of mechanical stress produces an electric charge (or displacement) on the crystal faces — e.g., a quartz pressure sensor.
- **Inverse effect (1 mark):** Application of an electric field produces a mechanical strain in the crystal — e.g., a PZT-based ultrasonic transducer element vibrating in response to an alternating voltage.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
> *State the constitutive equations of a piezoelectric material in the strain-charge form. Explain why the same piezoelectric coefficient $d$ appears in both equations, and mention its SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equations (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= s^{E}\cdot\sigma + d^{t}\cdot E \\
D &= d\cdot\sigma + \varepsilon^{T}\cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$
- **Reason (1 mark):** The appearance of the *same* $d$ matrix in both equations is a consequence of the **Lippmann reciprocity** (1881) and the **Maxwell thermodynamic relation** on the Gibbs free energy $\partial^2 G/\partial E\partial\sigma = \partial^2 G/\partial\sigma\partial E$. The direct and inverse effects are therefore not independent phenomena.
- **Units (1 mark):** $d$ has units of $\text{C/N}$ (coulomb per newton) for the charge form, equivalently $\text{m/V}$ (metre per volt) for the strain form. Numerically, $1~\text{C/N} = 1~\text{m/V}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]
>
> **(a)** *Derive the constitutive equations of a linear piezoelectric material using a thermodynamic energy-density approach. Clearly identify the direct and inverse piezoelectric effects in your derivation. Define every symbol used.* — **7 marks**
>
> **(b)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer in both transmit and receive modes. Discuss the desirable material properties for the piezoelectric element in such an application, and give two example materials.* — **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **State the thermodynamic potential (1 mark):** We begin with the electric enthalpy (Gibbs free energy) density $G$ of a linear piezoelectric continuum, as a function of stress $\sigma$ and electric field $E$ (the natural variables of $G$):
$$
G = -\tfrac{1}{2}s^{E}_{ijkl}\sigma_{ij}\sigma_{kl} - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{T}_{ik}E_i E_k - d_{ijk}E_i\sigma_{jk}
$$
2. **Direct effect from $D = -\partial G/\partial E$ (3 marks):** Differentiate $G$ with respect to $E_i$ at constant $\sigma$, leading to (after showing each term):
$$
D_i = d_{ijk}\sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^{T}_{ik}E_k
$$
This is the direct piezoelectric effect. The first term is the piezo-charge generated by stress; the second is the dielectric response. Symbol definitions: $D$ — electric displacement; $\sigma$ — stress; $E$ — electric field; $d$ — piezoelectric coefficient; $\varepsilon^T$ — permittivity at constant stress.
3. **Inverse effect from $S = -\partial G/\partial \sigma$ (2 marks):** Differentiate $G$ with respect to $\sigma_{jk}$ at constant $E$:
$$
S_{jk} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\sigma_{kl} + d_{ijk}E_i
$$
This is the inverse piezoelectric effect. $S$ — strain; $s^E$ — elastic compliance at constant $E$.
4. **Reciprocity remark (1 mark):** The same tensor $d_{ijk}$ governs both effects (Maxwell relation), confirming Lippmann's reciprocity.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Working principle (2 marks):** In **transmit mode**, an alternating voltage is applied across the electrodes of a piezo disc, producing a thickness-mode vibration via the inverse effect. The vibration generates an ultrasonic pressure wave in the coupled medium (water, tissue, air). In **receive mode**, the returning ultrasonic wave exerts a time-varying stress on the disc, which via the direct effect produces a corresponding time-varying voltage across the electrodes, which is amplified and processed.
2. **Block diagram (1 mark):** Sketch a transmitter chain: *Function generator → High-voltage amplifier → Matching network → Piezo disc → Coupling medium (e.g., gel/water)*. Receiver chain: *Returning wave → Piezo disc → LNA (low-noise amplifier) → Bandpass filter → Display*.
3. **Desirable material properties (2 marks):** (i) **High $k_{33}$** for efficient energy conversion; (ii) **High $d_{33}$** for sensitivity; (iii) **Low acoustic impedance mismatch** with the medium (use matching layers); (iv) **Low dielectric/mechanical losses** (high $Q$); (v) **High Curie temperature** for thermal stability; (vi) **Suitable frequency constant** ($f\cdot t = \text{const}$) for the chosen operating frequency.
4. **Example materials (1 mark):** **PZT (lead zirconate titanate, e.g., PZT-5H)** for general-purpose medical/industrial imaging, and **PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride)** polymer for broadband, flexible hydrophones.
5. **Application example (1 mark):** Medical ultrasound imaging (2–15 MHz) and NDT flaw detection in metals.

---

> **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Alternative Choice)]
>
> **(a)** *Discuss the crystal-structure criteria for piezoelectricity. Why is the absence of a centre of symmetry necessary? Are all non-centrosymmetric crystals piezoelectric? Justify with examples.* — **7 marks**
>
> **(b)** *A PZT-5H piezoelectric disc has $d_{33} = 593~\text{pC/N}$, $\varepsilon^{T}_{33} = 3800\,\varepsilon_0$, and $s^{E}_{33} = 19 \times 10^{-12}~\text{m}^2/\text{N}$. A force of $F = 50~\text{N}$ is applied uniformly over an electrode of area $A = 1~\text{cm}^2$ poled in the thickness direction ($t = 1~\text{mm}$). Compute: (i) the generated charge $Q$, (ii) the open-circuit voltage $V$, and (iii) the electromechanical coupling factor $k_{33}$.* — **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Centre of symmetry test (1 mark):** A crystal has a centre of symmetry if, for every atom at position $\vec{r}$, there is an identical atom at $-\vec{r}$. Under any uniform deformation, the dipole moments created in opposite halves cancel, so no net polarization arises.
2. **Why non-centrosymmetric (2 marks):** In a non-centrosymmetric crystal, the positive and negative ion sub-lattices are displaced by *different* amounts under stress, producing a net electric dipole moment $\Delta P_i = d_{ijk}\sigma_{jk}$. This is a necessary (but not sufficient) condition.
3. **Are all non-centrosymmetric crystals piezoelectric? (1 mark):** **No.** Out of 21 non-centrosymmetric point groups, **20 are piezoelectric**. The cubic class $\bar{4}3m$ (432) is the lone exception because other symmetry operations force $d_{ijk} = 0$.
4. **Classification (1 mark):** Of the 20 piezoelectric classes, 10 are **polar** (possess a unique spontaneous polarization axis) and 10 are **non-polar** (e.g., quartz, class 32).
5. **Examples (2 marks):** **Piezoelectric:** quartz ($SiO_2$, class 32), PZT (poled ceramic, $\infty m$ class), Rochelle salt, tourmaline, lithium niobate ($LiNbO_3$), barium titanate ($BaTiO_3$). **Non-piezoelectric despite being non-centrosymmetric:** crystals in class $\bar{4}3m$ such as zinc blende (ZnS) — though the *inverse* effect of optical rectification exists, the linear piezoelectric tensor is zero.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
