# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define pyrolysis in the context of nanocarbon synthesis. Mention any two nanocarbons produced by this method.**

**Model Answer:**
Pyrolysis is the thermal decomposition of carbonaceous precursors in the complete absence (or near absence) of oxygen, typically at temperatures between 400 °C and 3000 °C, yielding a carbon-rich solid residue that can be engineered into nanocarbon architectures. The absence of oxygen prevents combustion and allows carbon to reorganise into sp²- or sp³-rich nanodomains.

Two nanocarbons produced:
1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)** — via catalytic chemical vapour deposition (a form of pyrolysis) using Fe, Co, or Ni catalysts with hydrocarbon feedstocks such as C₂H₂ or CH₄.
2. **Activated Carbon / Biochar** — via slow pyrolysis of biomass (coconut shell, rice husk) followed by physical or chemical activation with steam, KOH, or ZnCl₂.

> *(Examiner expects: definition, oxygen-free condition, temperature, and at least one specific example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between carbonisation and graphitisation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Carbonisation | Graphitisation |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 600 – 1000 °C | >2000 °C |
| Structure | Turbostratic, disordered | Ordered hexagonal (ABAB stacking) |
| sp²/sp³ | Mixed, low order | Predominantly sp² |
| Product | Char, biochar, activated carbon | Graphite, highly crystalline CNTs |
| d₀₀₂ spacing | 0.35 – 0.40 nm | 0.3354 nm |
| Catalysts | Not required | Fe, Si, B often used |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In pyrolyzed-nanocarbon answers, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by failing to (a) state the inert atmosphere used, (b) quote the correct temperature range, (c) justify why pyrolysis is preferred over arc-discharge or laser ablation, and (d) present the property–application linkage in a structured table or flowchart. Always close with an applications table.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the chemical vapour deposition (CVD) method for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes. Discuss the role of catalyst, temperature, and hydrocarbon source.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe four key properties of CNTs and explain their applications in energy storage and structural composites.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Introduction to CVD:**
Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) is the most scalable and industrially adopted form of pyrolysis for carbon nanotube synthesis. A volatile hydrocarbon precursor (CH₄, C₂H₂, C₂H₄, or xylene) is introduced into a reactor heated to **600 – 900 °C** in the presence of a **transition metal catalyst** (Fe, Co, Ni, or alloys) supported on a substrate (SiO₂, Al₂O₃, MgO). The hydrocarbon decomposes on the catalyst surface, liberating carbon atoms that dissolve into the metal nanoparticle and precipitate as a tubular graphitic structure.

**Step 2 — Growth Mechanism (VLS Model):**
The Vapour–Liquid–Solid (VLS) mechanism governs CNT growth:
- **Vapour phase:** Hydrocarbon decomposes → carbon atoms
- **Liquid phase:** Carbon dissolves into molten catalyst nanoparticle
- **Solid phase:** Carbon precipitates out as a graphitic cylinder

**Step 3 — Role of Catalyst:**
- Catalyst size dictates CNT diameter: smaller nanoparticles (~1–3 nm) yield **SWCNTs**; larger ones (~10–50 nm) yield **MWCNTs**.
- Catalyst composition affects chirality and defect density.
- Common catalysts: Fe (most common), Co, Ni, Fe-Mo, Co-Mo bimetallics.

**Step 4 — Role of Temperature:**
- Below 500 °C: poor crystallisation, amorphous carbon dominates.
- 600 – 800 °C: optimal for **SWCNT** growth (e.g., CH₄/H₂ on Fe).
- 700 – 900 °C: optimal for **MWCNT** growth (e.g., C₂H₂ on Fe).
- Above 1000 °C: catalyst deactivation, etching, and over-graphitisation occur.

**Step 5 — Role of Hydrocarbon Source:**
- **Acetylene (C₂H₂):** Most reactive; favours MWCNT growth at low temperatures.
- **Methane (CH₄):** Less reactive; requires higher T; produces high-quality SWCNTs.
- **Ethylene (C₂H₄):** Intermediate reactivity.
- **Xylene / ferrocene mixture:** Floating catalyst CVD for continuous CNT yarn/spinning.

**Step 6 — Supporting/Inert Gas:**
- **H₂:** Etches amorphous carbon, prolongs catalyst life.
- **Ar / N₂:** Carrier gas to maintain inert atmosphere.
- Typical pressure: 100 – 760 Torr.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Property 1: Extraordinary Mechanical Strength**
- Young's modulus of SWCNT ≈ **1 TPa** (≈ 5× steel by weight).
- Tensile strength ≈ **100 GPa** (≈ 100× steel).
- **Application in structural composites:** CNTs dispersed in epoxy, polypropylene, or aluminium matrices yield lightweight composites used in aerospace, automotive (BMW i3 CFRP), and sports equipment (tennis rackets, bicycles).

**Property 2: High Electrical Conductivity**
- Metallic CNTs carry current densities of **10⁹ A/cm²** (copper: 10⁶ A/cm²).
- **Application in energy storage:** CNTs serve as conductive additives in Li-ion battery anodes and as free-standing electrodes in supercapacitors. They also form binder-free, flexible supercapacitor electrodes.

**Property 3: High Thermal Conductivity**
- SWCNT: **~3500 W/m·K** along the tube axis.
- **Application in energy storage:** CNT-based thermal interface materials (TIMs) in battery packs to manage heat dissipation; critical in EV battery modules.

**Property 4: High Specific Surface Area + Tunable Porosity**
- SWCNT: theoretical SSA ≈ **1300 m²/g**; activated MWCNT: 500 – 1000 m²/g.
- **Application:** CNT scaffolds as anode materials in Li-ion, Li-S, and Na-ion batteries; CNT-COOH functionalised electrodes for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells.

**Application Summary Table:**

| Application | Nanocarbon Used | Function | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|---|
| Li-ion battery anode | MWCNT, mesoporous C | Li⁺ storage | High SSA, conductivity |
| Supercapacitor electrode | SWCNT, N-doped CNT | EDLC + pseudocapacitance | Conductivity, N-active sites |
| Structural composite | CNT-epoxy | Reinforcement | Mechanical strength |
| Fuel cell catalyst support | N-doped CNT | ORR | Conductivity, N-doping |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the synthesis of graphene by pyrolysis-based methods. Compare the merits of thermal CVD graphene with reduced graphene oxide (rGO).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the synthesis, unique properties, and emerging applications of carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Why Pyrolysis-Based Graphene?**
Pyrolysis-based routes (chiefly **thermal CVD**) produce **continuous, large-area, high-quality graphene films**, unlike solution-based routes (GO/rGO) which yield defective, polycrystalline flakes. This makes CVD graphene the industrial standard for transparent electrodes, sensors, and electronics.

**Step 2 — Thermal CVD of Graphene:**
- **Substrate:** Copper (Cu) or nickel (Ni) foil.
- **Precursor:** Methane (CH₄) at **1000 – 1050 °C**.
- **Process:** CH₄ decomposes on the metal surface; carbon atoms dissolve (Ni) or adsorb (Cu) and form a monolayer sp² carbon.
- **Cooling:** Controlled crystallisation yields monolayer graphene.
- **Transfer:** PMMA-assisted wet transfer to target substrate (SiO₂/Si, PET).

**Step 3 — Mechanistic Distinction (Cu vs Ni):**
- On **Cu:** Self-limiting surface adsorption → predominantly **monolayer** graphene.
- On **Ni:** Precipitation from bulk → multilayer, non-uniform graphene.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table: CVD Graphene vs rGO:**

| Property | CVD Graphene | rGO |
|---|---|---|
| Synthesis | Pyrolysis (CVD) | Wet chemical reduction of GO |
| Crystallinity | Very high | Low (defective) |
| Sheet resistance | 30 – 1000 Ω/□ | 10³ – 10⁶ Ω/□ |
| Carrier mobility | up to 200,000 cm²/V·s | 1 – 100 cm²/V·s |
| Optical transparency | 97.7 % per layer | Variable (brownish) |
| Scalability | Roll-to-roll possible | Easily scalable |
| Cost | High (Cu foil, vacuum) | Low |
| Best use | Electronics, photonics | Composites, energy storage |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
CVD graphene is the gold standard for **electronic-grade** applications, while rGO is preferred for **bulk applications** (composites, supercapacitors) where cost dominates over electronic perfection.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Step 1 — Definition:**
**Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)** are **zero-dimensional** carbon nanoparticles (typically <10 nm) with partial sp²/sp³ carbon cores, surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂), and strong **photoluminescence** under UV excitation.

**Step 2 — Pyrolysis-Based Synthesis of CQDs:**
- **Precursors:** Citric acid, glucose, urea, chitosan, plant extracts, lignocellulosic biomass.
- **Method:** Thermal pyrolysis at **180 – 300 °C** for 1 – 4 hours (sometimes in autoclave → **hydrothermal** combination).
- **Mechanism:** Dehydration, polymerisation, and carbonisation of the precursor produce carbonaceous cores; surface oxidation yields –COOH/–OH groups responsible for water solubility and emission tunability.

**Step 3 — Alternative Pyrolytic Routes:**
- Microwave-assisted pyrolysis (rapid, uniform heating).
- Solid-state pyrolysis of organics (e.g., ammonium citrate) in a furnace.

**Step 4 — Unique Properties of CQDs:**
- **Photoluminescence:** Excitation-dependent emission spanning UV to NIR.
- **Quantum yield:** Up to **80 %** (recently reported for N-doped CQDs).
- **Biocompatibility:** Low toxicity; suitable for in vivo imaging.
- **Chemical inertness:** Stable in physiological environments.
- **Tunable surface chemistry:** Easy functionalisation for targeting.
- **Size tunability:** Emission wavelength depends on size (2 – 10 nm).

**Step 5 — Applications:**

| Application | Mechanism / Function |
|---|---|
| Bioimaging | Photoluminescent tracking of cells, tumours |
| Drug delivery | Surface functionalised CQDs carry chemotherapeutics |
| Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) | Down-conversion phosphors |
| Photocatalysis | CQDs/TiO₂ composites for H₂ evolution |
| Chemical sensors | PL quenching by metal ions (Fe³⁺, Hg²⁺) |
| Anti-counterfeiting inks | UV-activated luminescent security patterns |
| Solar cells | Down-shifting layer in perovskite/Si solar cells |

**Step 6 — Concluding Statement:**
CQDs are rapidly emerging as **low-toxicity, low-cost alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdSe)** and represent one of the fastest-growing applications of pyrolyzed nanocarbons in biomedical and optoelectronic industries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Pyrolyzed Nanocarbon Answers:**
> 1. **Omitting inert atmosphere** — Always state "oxygen-free / N₂ or Ar atmosphere" when describing pyrolysis. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
> 2. **Vague temperature ranges** — Quote specific values (e.g., 600 – 900 °C for CVD CNTs, 1000 – 1050 °C for CVD graphene, 2000+ °C for graphitisation). Avoid "high temperature".
> 3. **No catalyst mention** — For CNT/CNF synthesis, always identify the catalyst (Fe, Co, Ni) and its role (VLS mechanism).
> 4. **Forgetting the property–application link** — Don't list properties and applications separately. Always tie them (e.g., "high SSA → supercapacitor electrode").
> 5. **Ignoring safety/yield** — Note that low yields at high temperature, catalyst recovery, and defect control are real industrial challenges.
> 6. **Not using diagrams** — A simple flowchart of CVD/CVD-graphene process earns 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Final High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**

- **Pyrolysis** = thermal decomposition of carbon precursors in the **absence of oxygen**; temperatures range from **400 °C to 3000 °C**.
- **Carbonisation** occurs at **600 – 1000 °C**; **graphitisation** requires **>2000 °C**.
- The **d₀₀₂ spacing** distinguishes disordered (≈ 0.35 – 0.40 nm) from graphitic carbon (0.3354 nm).
- **Raman R-value (I_D/I_G)** is the gold standard for defect quantification in nanocarbons.
- **Scherrer equation** gives crystallite size L_c from XRD peak broadening.
- **CVD** is the industrial-scale pyrolysis method for CNTs and graphene.
- **VLS mechanism** (Vapour–Liquid–Solid) explains catalytic CNT/CNF growth on Fe, Co, Ni.
- **0D:** CQDs, CNOs, fullerenes — used in **bioimaging, sensing, LEDs**.
- **1D:** CNTs, CNFs — used in **composites, supercapacitors, sensors**.
- **2D:** Graphene, rGO, GO — used in **electronics, transparent electrodes, energy storage**.
- **3D:** Activated carbon, biochar, carbon aerogels — used in **adsorption, catalysis, EMI shielding**.
- **CVD graphene** uses Cu (monolayer, self-limiting) or Ni (multilayer, precipitation).
- **rGO** is defect-rich but cheap; ideal for composites and bulk energy storage.
- **N-doping** of CNTs and graphene (via N₂ precursor or NH₃ plasma) introduces ORR active sites for fuel cell catalysis.
- **Specific capacitance** of pyrolyzed carbon supercapacitor electrodes can reach **15,000 F/g** (theoretical) with N-doped graphene.
- **Thermal conductivity** of SWCNT (~3500 W/m·K) makes them excellent thermal interface materials.
- **Mechanical strength** of SWCNT (~1 TPa Young's modulus, ~100 GPa tensile strength) enables aerospace composites.
- **Carbonisation < Graphitisation** in crystallinity, conductivity, and temperature.
- Always specify the **inert atmosphere (Ar / N₂)**, **temperature**, **catalyst**, and **property–application linkage** in long answers.
- The three golden Raman peaks: **D-band (~1350 cm⁻¹)**, **G-band (~1580 cm⁻¹)**, and **2D-band (~2700 cm⁻¹)**.
- The **(002) XRD peak** is the signature of stacked graphene layers; its position and width define d₀₀₂ and L_c.
- BET surface area of activated carbon can exceed **3000 m²/g** — central to adsorption and EDLC energy storage.
- Pyrolysis is **scalable, low-cost, and versatile** — preferred over arc-discharge and laser ablation for industrial nanocarbon production.
- **Biomass-derived** nanocarbons (biochar) are sustainable and economically attractive for environmental remediation.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — Pyrolyzed Nanocarbon Materials: Properties and Applications**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging 2D Materials (Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN), Metal Chalcogenides, Metal Oxides, Metal Halides, Metal Carbides/Nitrides (MXenes), and Organic Semiconductors (OSCs)): Properties and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging 2D Materials (Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN), Metal Chalcogenides, Metal Oxides, Metal Halides, Metal Carbides/Nitrides (MXenes), and Organic Semiconductors (OSCs)): Properties and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Emerging 2D Materials?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Emerging 2D materials are atomically thin crystalline solids (typically 1–10 atomic layers thick, < 10 nm) that exhibit strong in-plane covalent/ionic bonding and weak out-of-plane van der Waals (vdW) interactions. Unlike the semi-metallic graphene, this new generation includes **insulating** (h-BN), **semiconducting** (TMDCs, MXenes, OSCs), and **dielectric/piezoelectric** (metal oxides, halides) members, expanding the 2D material toolkit beyond conductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly moves beyond graphene to emphasize **functional diversity** — students must recognize how each subclass of 2D materials addresses a specific engineering gap (e.g., h-BN as a substrate, MXenes for EMI shielding, OSCs for flexible electronics).

### 1.2 The Six Subclasses at a Glance

| Subclass | Representative Formula | Electrical Nature | Key Engineering Promise |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hexagonal Boron Nitride | h-BN | Insulator (band gap ~5.9 eV) | Atomically flat substrate for graphene devices |
| Metal Chalcogenides (TMDCs) | MoS₂, WS₂, MoSe₂, WSe₂ | Semiconductor (1–2 eV) | Transistors, photodetectors, valleytronics |
| Metal Oxides | MoO₃, MnO₂, TiO₂ nanosheets | Semiconductor / Dielectric | Catalysis, supercapacitors, gas sensors |
| Metal Halides (Perovskites) | CsPbI₃, MAPbBr₃ nanosheets | Semiconductor | Solar cells, LEDs, lasers |
| MXenes | Ti₃C₂Tₓ, Ti₂CTₓ | Metallic / Semiconducting | EMI shielding, supercapacitors, sensors |
| Organic Semiconductors | Pentacene, PDPP-TVT, C8-BTBT | Semiconductor | Flexible displays, OFETs, bio-electronics |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Postage Stamp Family"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine graphene as the **plain white postcard**. h-BN is the **kraft cardboard backing** (insulating, stiff), TMDCs are the **printed colorful cards** (semiconducting), MXenes are the **aluminum foil** (conducting and reflective), and OSCs are the **transparent plastic sheets** (flexible, processable in roll-to-roll). Together, they form a complete "postage stamp family" that can be stacked layer-by-layer (heterostructure engineering) just like Lego® blocks — the foundation of modern **van der Waals heterostructure (vdWH) design**.

### 1.4 Why "Emerging"?

A material is classified as *emerging* in the 2024 KTU context if it satisfies **all three** criteria:
1. **Atomic thinness** verified via AFM/Raman (thickness < 10 nm, ideally 0.5–3 nm).
2. **Exfoliability** — producible by mechanical, liquid-phase, or CVD routes.
3. **Functional property anomaly** — exhibits behavior markedly different from its 3D bulk counterpart (e.g., direct band gap in monolayer MoS₂ versus indirect in bulk).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN) — "White Graphene"

**Structural Concept:**
- Isoelectronic to graphene: alternating **B and N atoms** in a honeycomb lattice.
- In-plane: strong **sp² B–N σ bonds** (length ≈ 1.45 Å); out-of-plane: weak vdW gap (interlayer spacing ~ 3.33 Å).
- Lattice mismatch with graphene is only **~1.7%** — making it the perfect substrate.

**Key Properties (Board Exam Favorites):**
- **Band gap:** ~5.9 eV (theoretical) / ~5.5 eV (measured in monolayer) — the widest among 2D insulators.
- **Dielectric constant:** εᵣ ≈ 3–4 (low-k dielectric).
- **Thermal conductivity:** in-plane κ ≈ 600 W/m·K (comparable to graphene).
- **Chemical inertness:** stable up to ~1000 °C in air; resistant to acid/alkali attack.

**Applications:** Dielectric substrate for graphene FETs, deep-UV emitter (~215 nm), oxidation-resistant coating, neutron-detector material (¹⁰B has high neutron capture cross-section: σ = 3840 barns).

### 2.2 Metal Chalcogenides (TMDCs) — Workhorse 2D Semiconductors

**General Formula:** MX₂, where M = Mo, W, Nb, Re, Ti, etc. and X = S, Se, Te.

**Critical Band Structure Transition:**
Bulk 2H-MoS₂ has an **indirect** band gap (~1.2 eV) with the valence band maximum (VBM) at the Γ point and conduction band minimum (CBM) midway between Γ and K. As layers are reduced to a **monolayer**, quantum confinement and the absence of interlayer coupling shift the CBM from Γ–K to the **K point**, producing a **direct band gap of ~1.8 eV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The indirect-to-direct crossover in MoS₂ and WS₂ is THE most frequently asked conceptual question in the 2024 scheme (worth 3–7 marks).

**Excitonic Effects in 2D TMDCs:**

$$\boxed{E_b^{\text{2D}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{\text{eff}})^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(n - 1/2)^2}}$$

where μ is the reduced exciton mass, ε_eff is the effective dielectric constant (environment-dependent), and n is the principal quantum number. The reduced dimensionality enhances Coulomb interaction, leading to **giant exciton binding energies (~0.5 eV)** — an order of magnitude larger than bulk 3D semiconductors.

**Applications:** Field-effect transistors (MoS₂ FET with on/off ratio > 10⁸), photodetectors, electrocatalysts (HER), valleytronic memory, biosensors.

### 2.3 Metal Oxides — Functional 2D Sheets Beyond Graphene

Examples: MoO₃ nanosheets, MnO₂, V₂O₅, TiO₂, LaCoO₃ perovskite nanosheets.

**Pseudocapacitive Charge Storage (KTU Exam Hot Topic):**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$

For 2D oxide nanosheets, the **accessible surface area (A)** is maximized because every atom is a surface atom, and the diffusion length (d) is reduced to nanometers — yielding gravimetric capacitances of **400–1500 F/g** in MnO₂ and V₂O₅.

**Applications:** Electrocatalysis (2D MoO₃ for oxygen evolution), photodegradation of dyes (TiO₂ nanosheets), battery electrodes, chemiresistive gas sensors (SnO₂, ZnO).

### 2.4 Metal Halides (2D Halide Perovskites)

**General Formula:** ABX₃ or A₂BX₄ (where A = Cs⁺, MA⁺, FA⁺; B = Pb²⁺, Sn²⁺; X = Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻).

**Quantum Confinement in 2D Perovskite:**

$$\boxed{\Delta E_g^{\text{conf}} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu L^2}}$$

where L is the thickness of the 2D layer. Reducing L from 3D bulk to 2D (n = 1 Ruddlesden-Popper phase, L ≈ 1.2 nm) **widens** the band gap and **increases** exciton binding energy (often > 300 meV), enabling room-temperature LEDs and lasing.

**Applications:** High-efficiency solar cells (2D/3D perovskite stacks), light-emitting diodes (external quantum efficiency > 20%), X-ray detectors, spin-optoelectronic devices.

### 2.5 MXenes — The Conducting 2D Ceramics

**General Formula:** Mₙ₊₁XₙTₓ (n = 1, 2, 3, 4), where M = early transition metal (Ti, V, Nb, Cr, Mo), X = C and/or N, and Tₓ = surface terminations (–OH, –F, –O, –Cl).

**Synthesis:** Selective etching of the "A" layer (typically Al) from MAX phase precursors using HF, LiF/HCl (MILD method), or molten salts.

**Conductivity Origin:** Metallic Ti–C backbone + surface terminations create a tunable density of states at the Fermi level.

**EMI Shielding Effectiveness (KTU Exam Tag — Ti₃C₂Tₓ is the benchmark):**

$$\boxed{\text{SE}_T = \text{SE}_R + \text{SE}_A = 50 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma}{f \cdot \mu_r}\right) + 8.68 t \sqrt{\pi f \mu_r \sigma}}$$

A 45-μm-thick Ti₃C₂Tₓ film achieves **SE ≈ 92 dB**, exceeding commercial metal shields.

**Applications:** EMI shielding, supercapacitors (volumetric capacitance > 1500 F/cm³), Li-ion battery anodes, water purification membranes, photothermal therapy, triboelectric nanogenerators.

### 2.6 Organic Semiconductors (OSCs) — π-Conjugated Molecules & Polymers

**Two Material Families:**
- **Small-molecule OSCs:** pentacene, C8-BTBT, TIPS-pentacene, rubrene.
- **Polymer OSCs:** P3HT, PBDB-T, PDPP-TVT, PCDTBT.

**Charge Transport Mechanism:** Hopping transport between localized states described by the Miller-Abrahams model:

$$\boxed{\nu_{ij} = \nu_0 \exp(-2\alpha R_{ij}) \exp\left(-\frac{E_j - E_i}{k_BT}\right) \quad \text{for } E_j > E_i}$$

where ν₀ is the attempt-to-escape frequency, α is the localization parameter, R_ij is the intermolecular distance, and E_i, E_j are site energies.

**Key Metrics:** Charge carrier mobility (μ) in single-crystal C8-BTBT reaches **> 40 cm²/V·s**; optical band gap is tunable from 1.0 eV (PTB7-IE) to 3.5 eV (wide-gap emitters).

**Applications:** Organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), organic photovoltaics (OPVs, PCE > 19%), organic field-effect transistors (OFETs), bio-compatible sensors, neuromorphic devices.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g^{\text{2D}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu L^2}$ | Quantum-confined band gap | TMDC, perovskite |
| 2 | $E_b^{\text{2D}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{\text{eff}})^2 (n-1/2)^2}$ | 2D exciton binding energy | TMDC excitonics |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | 2D oxide supercapacitors |
| 4 | $\rho_{2D} = \dfrac{m^{*}}{e^2 \tau n_{2D}}$ | 2D sheet resistivity | TMDC, MXene transport |
| 5 | $\text{SE}_T = 50 + 10 \log_{10}(\sigma / f \mu_r) + 8.68 t \sqrt{\pi f \mu_r \sigma}$ | EMI shielding effectiveness | MXene shielding |
| 6 | $\nu_{ij} = \nu_0 \exp(-2\alpha R_{ij}) \exp[-(E_j - E_i)/k_BT]$ | Miller-Abrahams hopping | OSC transport |
| 7 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 \exp(-E_a / k_BT)$ | Arrhenius mobility (OSC) | OFETs |
| 8 | $n_{2D} = \dfrac{C_g (V_g - V_{th})}{e}$ | 2D carrier density (FET) | All 2D-FETs |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})}$ nm | Optical absorption edge | Photodetectors, OPVs |
| 10 | $J_{SC} = e \int \Phi(\lambda) \, \eta_{\text{EQE}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Short-circuit current | Solar cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every formula above maps directly to a commercial device figure-of-merit. Mastering these 10 equations covers > 80% of numerical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE for this module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Indirect-to-Direct Band Gap Crossover in Monolayer MoS₂

This derivation explains WHY monolayer MoS₂ is a direct band gap semiconductor while bulk 2H-MoS₂ is indirect — the single most important conceptual question in this module.

**Starting Assumptions (from DFT, KTU reference values):**
- Bulk 2H-MoS₂: $E_g^{\text{ind}} = 1.29$ eV (Γ → Λ transition, where Λ is midway between Γ and K).
- Monolayer MoS₂: $E_g^{\text{dir}} = 1.90$ eV (K → K transition).
- CBM in bulk lies at Λ, midway between Γ and K.
- Interlayer coupling in bulk splits the d-orbital-derived CBM by $\Delta E_c \approx 0.6$ eV.

**Step 1 — Identify the orbital origin of the band edges:**
The VBM at K is derived from the localized **Mo d_(x²-y²) + d_(xy)** orbitals, which are **insensitive** to interlayer coupling because they lie in-plane and have no overlap with the S atoms of the adjacent layer.

The CBM in bulk lies at Λ, derived from a combination of **Mo d_(z²)** and **S p_z** orbitals. These orbitals have lobes pointing **out-of-plane**, allowing strong interlayer coupling.

**Step 2 — Write the CBM energy as a function of layer number N:**

$$E_C(\Lambda) = E_C^{(1)} + 2\gamma_c \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{N+1}\right)$$

where $\gamma_c$ is the interlayer hopping amplitude at the Λ point and N is the number of layers.

**Step 3 — Apply the monolayer limit (N = 1):**

$$E_C(\Lambda)\big\vert_{N=1} = E_C^{(1)} + 2\gamma_c \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = E_C^{(1)} + 0 = E_C^{(1)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the bulk limit (N → ∞):**

$$E_C(\Lambda)\big\vert_{N\to\infty} = E_C^{(1)} + 2\gamma_c \cos(0) = E_C^{(1)} + 2\gamma_c$$

**Step 5 — Determine the energy shift:**
From Step 3 and Step 4, the CBM at Λ shifts **upward** by $2\gamma_c$ when going from bulk to monolayer.

$$\Delta E_C = E_C^{\text{mono}}(\Lambda) - E_C^{\text{bulk}}(\Lambda) = +2\gamma_c$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**
Using the experimental data: $E_g^{\text{ind}} = 1.29$ eV (bulk), $E_g^{\text{dir}} = 1.90$ eV (monolayer).

$$\Delta E_C = E_g^{\text{dir}} - E_g^{\text{ind}} = 1.90 - 1.29 = 0.61 \text{ eV}$$

This implies $2\gamma_c = 0.61$ eV, hence $\gamma_c \approx 0.305$ eV.

**Step 7 — Conclude the crossover:**

$$\boxed{E_C(\Lambda) \xrightarrow{\text{as } N \to 1} E_C(K), \quad \text{producing direct band gap at } K}$$

**Engineering implication:** This crossover is why **monolayer** MoS₂ is the active material in high-efficiency photodetectors and LEDs, while **few-layer** MoS₂ is reserved for catalytic and electronic-switching applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: 2D Exciton Binding Energy in TMDC Monolayers

**Why 2D excitons are "giant":** Reduced dielectric screening in atomically thin materials.

**Step 1 — Start from the 3D hydrogenic exciton model:**

$$E_b^{\text{3D}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)^2 (n)^2} = \frac{R_y^\ast}{n^2}$$

**Step 2 — Modify for 2D using the Keldysh potential:**
In a 2D sheet surrounded by dielectric media (substrate ε_s and environment ε_e), the screened Coulomb potential becomes the **Keldysh potential** instead of the simple 1/r form:

$$V(r) = -\frac{\pi e^2}{2(\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_e) r_0} \left[H_0\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right) - Y_0\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)\right]$$

where $r_0 = d \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{2D}} / (\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_e)$ is the screening length, d is the layer thickness, and $H_0, Y_0$ are Struve and Bessel functions.

**Step 3 — The effective 2D Rydberg:**

$$\boxed{R_y^{\ast \text{2D}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 (4\pi\varepsilon_0)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_e)^2 / 2\varepsilon_{\text{2D}}^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical estimate for monolayer MoS₂ on SiO₂:**
- $\mu \approx 0.35 \, m_0$ (reduced mass)
- $\varepsilon_{\text{2D}} \approx 4.5$ (2D dielectric)
- $\varepsilon_s = 3.9$ (SiO₂), $\varepsilon_e = 1.0$ (vacuum)

$$E_b^{\text{2D}} \approx \frac{0.35 \times 13.6}{(2 \cdot 4.5^2 / (3.9 + 1.0)^2)} \approx 0.5 \text{ eV}$$

This is **~20× larger** than the 3D exciton binding energy (~25 meV) and is responsible for the room-temperature stability of excitons in 2D TMDCs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: 2D Sheet Carrier Density in a TMDC FET

**Step 1 — Parallel-plate FET analogy:**
A 2D FET is essentially a parallel-plate capacitor with the 2D channel as the top plate and the back gate as the bottom plate, separated by a dielectric of thickness $t_{\text{ox}}$ and permittivity $\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}$.

**Step 2 — Gate capacitance per unit area:**

$$C_g = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{\text{ox}}}{t_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 3 — Induced sheet carrier density:**

$$\boxed{n_{2D} = \frac{C_g (V_g - V_{th})}{e} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{\text{ox}} (V_g - V_{th})}{e \, t_{\text{ox}}}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering example (KTU numerical):**
Given a MoS₂ FET with $t_{\text{ox}} = 90$ nm SiO₂, $V_g - V_{th} = 10$ V, find $n_{2D}$.

$$n_{2D} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(3.9)(10)}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(90 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$n_{2D} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-10}}{1.44 \times 10^{-26}} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

Convert to cm⁻²: $n_{2D} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{12}$ cm⁻².

**Step 5 — Mobility extraction from transfer curve:**

$$\mu_{\text{FE}} = \frac{L}{W \, C_g \, V_{ds}} \cdot \frac{dI_{ds}}{dV_g}$$

This is the standard field-effect mobility equation tested in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Property Table for Quick Recall

| Property | h-BN | TMDC (MoS₂) | 2D Oxide (MnO₂) | 2D Halide (CsPbBr₃) | MXene (Ti₃C₂Tₓ) | OSC (Pentacene) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Band gap | ~5.9 eV | ~1.8 eV (dir.) | ~2.5 eV | ~2.3 eV | 0 (metallic) | ~1.85 eV |
| Carrier mobility | Insulator | ~200 cm²/V·s (theory) | Low | ~100 cm²/V·s | ~10⁴ S/m (cond.) | ~40 cm²/V·s (single-crystal) |
| Conductivity type | Insulator | n-type (int.) | n/p (tunable) | Ambipolar | Metallic | p-type (typical) |
| Dielectric const. | 3–4 | ~4.5 | ~10–100 | ~6 | — | ~3 |
| Stability in air | Excellent | Good (S-based) | Excellent | Poor (moisture) | Moderate (oxidation) | Moderate |
| Synthesis route | CVD / exfoliation | CVD / exfoliation | Hydrothermal | Solution (spin) | HF-etching of MAX | Vacuum / solution |
| Killer application | Substrate | Transistor | Supercapacitor | LED | EMI shield | OLED / OFET |
| KTU mark weight (typical) | 5–7 | 7–10 | 3–5 | 5–7 | 5–7 | 5–7 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Taxonomy of Emerging 2D Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Emerging 2D Materials] --> B[Insulators]
    A --> C[Semiconductors]
    A --> D[Metallic / Conducting]
    A --> E[Hybrid Organic]

    B --> B1[h-BN]
    B1 --> B1a[Substrate for graphene]
    B1 --> B1b[Deep-UV emitter]

    C --> C1[Metal Chalcogenides TMDC]
    C --> C2[Metal Oxides]
    C --> C3[Metal Halide Perovskites]
    C --> C4[2D Organic Semiconductors]

    C1 --> C1a[MoS2 / WS2 / MoSe2]
    C1a --> C1a1[Field-effect transistor]
    C1a --> C1a2[Photodetector]
    C1a --> C1a3[Electrocatalyst HER]

    C2 --> C2a[MoO3 / MnO2 / V2O5]
    C2a --> C2a1[Supercapacitor]
    C2a --> C2a2[Gas sensor]
    C2a --> C2a3[Electrocatalyst OER]

    C3 --> C3a[CsPbBr3 / MAPbI3]
    C3a --> C3a1[Solar cell]
    C3a --> C3a2[LED / Laser]
    C3a --> C3a3[X-ray detector]

    C4 --> C4a[Pentacene / P3HT / PDPP-TVT]
    C4a --> C4a1[OLED display]
    C4a --> C4a2[Organic PV]
    C4a --> C4a3[OFET flexible]

    D --> D1[MXenes Ti3C2Tx]
    D1 --> D1a[EMI Shielding]
    D1 --> D1b[Supercapacitor]
    D1 --> D1c[Photothermal Therapy]
    D1 --> D1d[Water Purification]

    E --> E1[2D COFs MOFs]
    E1 --> E1a[Gas storage]
    E1 --> E1b[Sensing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: vdW Heterostructure Stacking (Lego Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Top Layer h-BN Encapsulation] --> L2[Active Channel MoS2 Monolayer]
    L2 --> L3[Bottom Layer h-BN Dielectric]
    L3 --> L4[Graphene Back Gate]
    L4 --> L5[SiO2 Si Substrate]

    L2 -. Bandgap 1.8 eV direct .-> A1[Photoluminescence 660 nm]
    L2 -. Mobility 200 cm2/Vs .-> A2[High-speed FET]
    L1 -. Surface protection .-> A3[Air-stable device]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: MXene Synthesis (MILD Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Max Phase Precursor Ti3AlC2] --> P2[Selective Etching LiF HCl 35 °C 24 h]
    P2 --> P3[Multilayer MXene MXene sediment]
    P3 --> P4[Washing pH > 6]
    P4 --> P5[Delamination by sonication]
    P5 --> P6[Centrifugation 3500 rpm]
    P6 --> P7[Supernatant Few-layer Ti3C2Tx]
    P7 --> P8[Vacuum filtration Ti3C2Tx film]
    P8 --> P9[Applications EMI Shielding 92 dB]
    P8 --> P10[Applications Supercapacitor 1500 F/cm3]
    P8 --> P11[Applications Li-ion Anode]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison of Band Structures (Bulk vs Monolayer MoS₂)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bulk2H
        B1[VB Gamma point 0 eV ref] --> B2[VB-K offset 0.05 eV]
        B2 --> B3[CB-Lambda midpoint 1.29 eV indirect]
        B3 --> B4[CB-K 1.35 eV]
    end

    subgraph Monolayer
        M1[VB-K 0 eV] --> M2[CB-K 1.90 eV direct]
    end

    Bulk2H -. Exfoliation reduces N from inf to 1 .-> Monolayer
    M2 -. Result .-> R1[Strong photoluminescence 660 nm room temp]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). Why is it called "white graphene"? List any two of its key applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) is a 2D layered material with the chemical formula BN, isostructural to graphene, consisting of alternating boron and nitrogen atoms arranged in a planar honeycomb sp²-bonded lattice.
2. **"White graphene" justification (1 mark):** It is called "white graphene" because of its structural similarity to graphene (isoelectronic, same hexagonal lattice) combined with its white/transparent appearance (unlike the black/colored graphene), arising from its wide band gap of ~5.9 eV.
3. **Two applications (1 mark):**
   - Atomically flat dielectric substrate for graphene and TMDC heterostructure devices.
   - Deep-ultraviolet light emitter (emission ~215 nm) for sterilization and photolithography.
   - *(Optional alt)*: Oxidation-resistant protective coating for metals at high temperatures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is an MXene? Write the general formula and give any two of its applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** MXenes are a family of 2D transition metal carbides, nitrides, and carbonitrides produced by selectively etching the "A" layer (typically Al) from a MAX-phase precursor (Mₙ₊₁AXₙ).
2. **General formula (1 mark):** Mₙ₊₁XₙTₓ, where M is an early transition metal, X is C and/or N, n = 1, 2, 3, 4, and Tₓ denotes surface terminations (–OH, –F, –O, –Cl).
3. **Two applications (1 mark):**
   - Electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding — Ti₃C₂Tₓ films achieve > 90 dB SE at 45 μm thickness.
   - *(Optional alt)*: Electrode material for supercapacitors; photothermal therapy; water purification; Li-ion battery anodes.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Explain the structure of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) with the general formula MX₂. Discuss in detail the **indirect-to-direct band gap transition** observed in MoS₂ when reduced from bulk to monolayer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various **synthesis routes** for producing 2D TMDCs, comparing the merits of mechanical exfoliation, liquid-phase exfoliation, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Structure of TMDCs (2 marks):**
- TMDCs have the general formula **MX₂** (M = Mo, W, Nb, Re, Ti, Hf; X = S, Se, Te).
- Each layer is a **sandwich X–M–X** of one M-atom plane covalently bonded between two X-atom planes.
- Coordination: trigonal prismatic (2H phase) or octahedral (1T phase).
- Within a layer, strong covalent M–X bonds; between layers, weak van der Waals forces (interlayer gap ~ 0.65 nm).

**Indirect-to-direct band gap transition in MoS₂ (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Bulk 2H-MoS₂ | Monolayer MoS₂ |
|---|---|---|
| Band gap value | 1.29 eV | 1.90 eV |
| Nature | Indirect (Γ → Λ) | Direct (K → K) |
| CBM location | Λ (midway Γ–K) | K point |
| VBM location | Γ (or near it) | K point |
| PL intensity | Very weak | Strong (~10⁴× brighter) |

- The **CBM at Λ** is derived from out-of-plane **Mo d_(z²) + S p_z** orbitals → highly sensitive to **interlayer coupling**.
- The **CBM at K** is derived from in-plane **Mo d_(x²-y²), d_(xy)** orbitals → insensitive to interlayer coupling.
- As the number of layers N decreases from bulk to 1, the Λ-CBM shifts **upward in energy** by $2\gamma_c$ (~0.6 eV) due to the loss of interlayer hopping, while the K-CBM remains relatively unchanged.
- Eventually (at monolayer), the K-CBM becomes lower than the Λ-CBM, and the system acquires a **direct band gap** at K.
- This crossover is the physical origin of the **strong photoluminescence** observed in monolayer MoS₂ (~660 nm, room temperature), enabling applications in **LEDs, lasers, and photodetectors**.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks:**

| Method | Principle | Merits | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Exfoliation (Scotch tape)** | Peel off thin layers using adhesive tape | Highest-quality monolayers; defect-free; preserves intrinsic properties (μ ~ 200 cm²/V·s) | Low yield; non-scalable; random flake size/location |
| **Liquid-Phase Exfoliation (LPE)** | Sonicate bulk crystals in solvents (NMP, water + surfactant); centrifuge | Scalable; solution-processable; suitable for printable electronics | Smaller flake size (100s of nm); residual solvent/surfactant contamination; lower mobility |
| **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** | Vaporize MoO₃ + S precursors on SiO₂/Si at 700–900 °C | Wafer-scale continuous films; controllable layer number; high mobility (~100 cm²/V·s) | High temperature; grain boundaries; transfer process needed |

**Comparative Summary (KTU Exam Tip):**
- For **fundamental research** → mechanical exfoliation.
- For **large-area, high-performance electronics** → CVD.
- For **printed/flexible devices and bulk applications** → LPE.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** *(Internal Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the **structure, properties, and applications** of **MXenes** with special reference to Ti₃C₂Tₓ. Explain why MXenes are considered the best 2D materials for electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **organic semiconductors (OSCs)**: classify them, discuss the **hopping transport mechanism** using the Miller-Abrahams model, and explain at least two important applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
- MXenes are 2D transition metal carbides/nitrides with formula **Mₙ₊₁XₙTₓ**.
- Derived from MAX phases Mₙ₊₁AXₙ by selectively etching the A-layer (Al, Si, Ga) using HF or LiF/HCl.
- For Ti₃C₂Tₓ (n=2): three Ti layers interleaved with two C layers, sandwiched between the etched-out Al layers; surface –OH, –F, –O terminations.

**Key Properties (2 marks):**
- Metallic conductivity (~10⁴ S/m, comparable to graphite).
- Hydrophilic surface (unlike graphene) → water-processable.
- High volumetric capacitance (> 1500 F/cm³).
- Tunable surface chemistry via choice of etchant.
- Mechanical flexibility; layered morphology.

**EMI Shielding Mechanism (2 marks):**
- The high electrical conductivity of Ti₃C₂Tₓ films provides strong **reflection** (SE_R) of incident EM waves.
- The layered structure with terminations and interlayer water also provides **absorption** (SE_A) through polarization losses.
- The shielding effectiveness follows: $\text{SE}_T = 50 + 10 \log_{10}(\sigma/f\mu_r) + 8.68 t \sqrt{\pi f \mu_r \sigma}$.
- A 45-μm-thick Ti₃C₂Tₓ film gives **SE ≈ 92 dB**, which **exceeds** the commercial requirement (> 60 dB for military electronics) and surpasses copper foils of the same thickness.
- Compared to graphene, MXenes are easier to process, are flexible, and achieve comparable or higher SE at much lower thickness.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- EMI shielding for flexible electronics, aerospace, and 5G devices; supercapacitors; Li-ion battery anodes; water purification; photothermal cancer therapy.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Classification of OSCs (2 marks):**
- **Small-molecule OSCs** (vacuum-deposited): pentacene, rubrene, C8-BTBT, TIPS-pentacene.
- **Polymer OSCs** (solution-processed): P3HT, PBDB-T, PCDTBT, PDPP-TVT.
- Both possess extended **π-conjugated backbones** that allow delocalization of π-electrons and facilitate charge transport.

**Hopping Transport — Miller-Abrahams Model (3 marks):**
- In OSCs, charge carriers are **localized** on individual molecules or conjugated segments due to weak intermolecular van der Waals coupling and energetic disorder.
- Transport occurs via **phonon-assisted hopping** from site i to site j, described by:
$$\nu_{ij} = \nu_0 \exp(-2\alpha R_{ij}) \exp[-(E_j - E_i)/k_BT] \text{ for } E_j > E_i$$
- The first exponential captures **spatial tunneling** (α = inverse localization length, R_ij = intermolecular distance).
- The second exponential captures the **activation barrier** (Boltzmann factor).
- The temperature dependence of mobility is **Arrhenius-like**: $\mu \propto \exp(-E_a / k_BT)$.

**Two Applications (2 marks):**
1. **OLED displays** — Used as the emissive layer in Samsung/Apple displays; small-molecule and polymer emitters enable red/green/blue pixels.
2. **Organic photovoltaics (OPVs)** — Bulk heterojunction solar cells (e.g., PM6:Y6 blend) achieve PCE > 19% as of 2024.
3. *(Optional alt)*: **OFETs** for flexible RFID tags; biosensors (enzyme-functionalized OSCs); neuromorphic memristors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Band gap crossover (Question A-a):** Students often confuse the *location* of the crossover (CBM shifts from Λ to K), but the VBM stays at K. Marks are lost for stating "VBM moves from K to Λ" or "both bands shift" — these are WRONG.
> 2. **MXene synthesis (Q-B-a):** Many students incorrectly write that MXenes are obtained by exfoliation. They are produced by **selective chemical etching** of the A-layer from a MAX phase — the "A" must be chemically removed, not peeled.
> 3. **OSC transport (Q-B-b):** The Miller-Abrahams model is for **disordered/organic** systems. Do NOT confuse it with band transport in crystalline silicon or GaAs. Writing "Bloch waves" or "band mobility" for OSCs is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Units & significant figures:** KTU 2024 valuation is strict on **units** in numerics. Always report $E_g$ in eV, κ in W/m·K, σ in S/m, μ in cm²/V·s.
> 5. **Direct/indirect band gap definition:** Always pair the band gap value with the **k-space transition**, e.g., "1.29 eV, indirect (Γ → Λ)" — not just "1.29 eV".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- [ ] **h-BN = "white graphene"**, isoelectronic to graphene, band gap **~5.9 eV**, εᵣ ≈ 3–4, lattice mismatch with graphene ~ 1.7%, used as **substrate** and **deep-UV emitter**.
- [ ] **TMDCs = MX₂**, monolayer MoS₂ has **direct** band gap of 1.8 eV at K; bulk is **indirect** (1.29 eV, Γ → Λ); **exciton binding energy ~ 0.5 eV** (giant).
- [ ] **2D Metal oxides** (MoO₃, MnO₂, V₂O₅) → high surface area → supercapacitors (up to 1500 F/g), electrocatalysts, gas sensors.
- [ ] **2D Halide perovskites** (CsPbBr₃, MAPbI₃ Ruddlesden–Popper) → strong quantum confinement, large exciton binding energy, used in **LEDs and solar cells**.
- [ ] **MXenes = Mₙ₊₁XₙTₓ** derived from MAX phases; **metallic**, hydrophilic, EMI shielding **> 90 dB** at 45 μm; supercapacitors, photothermal therapy.
- [ ] **OSCs** are **π-conjugated** small molecules or polymers; transport by **Miller-Abrahams hopping**; key applications: OLEDs, OPVs, OFETs.
- [ ] All 2D materials share **van der Waals interlayer coupling** and can be stacked into **heterostructures** (vdWH).
- [ ] **Carrier density in 2D FET:** $n_{2D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{ox} (V_g - V_{th})/(e \, t_{ox})$.
- [ ] **EMI shielding equation** is a 14-mark favorite: know all three terms (SE_R + SE_A) and the units.
- [ ] **Synthesis methods to remember:** mechanical exfoliation (best quality), LPE (scalable), CVD (wafer-scale), HF-etching (for MXenes only).

**Mnemonic for the 2D family: "B-TOM-MO"**
- **B**oron nitride (insulator)
- **T**MDCs (semiconductor)
- **O**xides (functional)
- **M**Xenes (metallic)
- **O**rganic semiconductors (flexible)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Photovoltaic Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET635 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging Photovoltaic Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emerging Photovoltaic Materials** are a class of light-harvesting semiconductors and organic/inorganic hybrid compounds designed to convert solar radiation into electrical energy through the photovoltaic effect, but using non-conventional, next-generation chemistries that move beyond bulk crystalline silicon (c-Si) and traditional III–V multijunction architectures. They are characterized by **tunable bandgaps**, **solution-processable fabrication routes**, **earth-abundant elemental composition**, and **flexible form-factor compatibility**, all of which collectively target a reduction in the **levelized cost of energy (LCOE)** and an expansion of deployment scenarios (BIPV, wearable, indoor IoT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Emerging photovoltaic materials are thin-film and nano-structured semiconductors — including perovskites, organic polymers, dye-sensitized titania, quantum dots, and CZTS — that exhibit strong optical absorption, carrier generation, and charge transport properties suitable for the next generation of cost-effective, lightweight, and flexible solar cells."

The classification into **first-generation** (c-Si), **second-generation** (amorphous Si, CdTe, CIGS), and **third-generation** (emerging) photovoltaics is essential for board-level answers. The first two generations are commercially mature; the third generation is the focus of this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Sponge" Intuition

Imagine a **sponge in a swimming pool**. A traditional silicon solar cell is like a rigid, heavy ceramic tile — it works, but it is expensive to manufacture, fragile, and only absorbs a narrow band of "water droplets" (photons). An **emerging photovoltaic material** is more like a flexible, brightly colored sponge that can be dyed to soak up many different colors of water (different photon wavelengths), can be cut into any shape, is dirt-cheap to produce, and can be stuck onto a backpack, a car roof, or a window pane. The dye in the sponge corresponds to a **tunable bandgap**; the porous structure corresponds to a **nanostructured morphology** that maximizes surface area; and the cheap raw materials correspond to **earth-abundant chemistries** like carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, and tin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE, $\eta$):** The ratio of electrical power output to incident solar power, expressed as a percentage.
> - **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$):** Maximum voltage when no current flows.
> - **Short-Circuit Current Density ($J_{SC}$):** Current density when terminals are shorted.
> - **Fill Factor (FF):** Measure of the "squareness" of the J–V curve.
> - **External Quantum Efficiency (EQE):** Ratio of collected electrons to incident photons at each wavelength.

### 1.3 Why These Materials Are "Emerging"

| Driver | Traditional c-Si | Emerging Materials |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw material cost** | High-purity Si requires >99.9999% purity | Solution-processable, abundant elements |
| **Energy payback time** | 2–5 years | Months to ~1 year (perovskite, OPV) |
| **Substrate flexibility** | Rigid wafers | Roll-to-roll compatible |
| **Tunability** | Fixed bandgap ~1.1 eV | Bandgap engineered from ~0.7 to 2.5 eV |
| **Module efficiency (2024)** | ~22–26% | Perovskite ~26%, OPV ~19%, DSSC ~14% |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question says *"list the advantages/disadvantages"* of emerging PV materials, always present a **paired comparison** (c-Si vs emerging) rather than an absolute list. This demonstrates CO2 (Design/Development) and CO3 (Modern Tool Usage) cognitive levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Universal Photovoltaic Mechanism

Regardless of the chemistry, every emerging PV device converts photons into electricity through a **four-step cascade**:

1. **Photon Absorption:** An incoming photon with energy $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap) is absorbed by the active layer, exciting an electron from the **valence band (VB)** to the **conduction band (CB)**, leaving a hole in the VB. The excess energy ($E - E_g$) is lost as **thermalization**.
2. **Exciton Generation (in organics):** In organic and some hybrid systems, the photoexcited electron–hole pair remains Coulombically bound as an **exciton**, with binding energy $E_b$ typically 0.1–1.0 eV.
3. **Exciton Dissociation:** Excitons must be split into free carriers at a **heterojunction, donor–acceptor interface, or built-in electric field** before they recombine.
4. **Charge Transport & Collection:** Free electrons travel through the electron-transport layer (ETL) to the cathode; holes travel through the hole-transport layer (HTL) to the anode, generating external current.

### 2.2 Material-by-Material Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs)
- **Structure:** ABX₃ — typically CH₃NH₃PbI₃ (MAPbI₃) or FA/MA/Cs mixed cation with I/Br/Cl mixed halide.
- **Why it works:** The 3D corner-sharing **BX₆ octahedra** form a direct bandgap semiconductor with extremely high absorption coefficient ($\alpha > 10^5$ cm⁻¹), allowing ~300 nm thick films to absorb the full visible spectrum.
- **Architectures:** Mesoporous (n-i-p) and planar (regular n-i-p or inverted p-i-n).
- **Key challenges:** **Lead (Pb) toxicity**, **moisture/thermal instability**, **ion migration**, **hysteresis in J-V curves**.

#### 2.2.2 Organic Photovoltaics (OPV)
- **Active layer:** Bulk heterojunction (BHJ) blend of a **p-type donor polymer** (e.g., P3HT, PTB7, PM6) and an **n-type acceptor** (e.g., PC₆₁BM, ITIC, Y6 series non-fullerene).
- **Why it works:** The donor–acceptor phase separation at the nanoscale creates a bicontinuous interpenetrating network for exciton dissociation and charge transport.
- **Key challenges:** **Low carrier mobility**, **limited long-term stability** under UV, oxygen, and heat.

#### 2.2.3 Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs)
- **Architecture:** Photoanode (mesoporous TiO₂ on FTO) + **molecular dye sensitizer** (e.g., N719, N3, or ruthenium polypyridyl) + liquid electrolyte (I⁻/I₃⁻ redox couple) + Pt counter electrode.
- **Why it works:** The dye absorbs light and injects electrons into the TiO₂ conduction band; the electrolyte regenerates the dye.
- **Key challenges:** **Liquid electrolyte leakage**, **sealing issues**, **corrosion**.

#### 2.2.4 Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSCs)
- **Active layer:** Colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals (CdSe, PbS, CdTe, perovskite QDs) of size 2–10 nm.
- **Why it works:** **Quantum confinement** shifts the effective bandgap with size: $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$, where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass and $R$ is the nanoparticle radius.
- **Key challenges:** Surface trap states, ligand exchange requirements, batch-to-batch variability.

#### 2.2.5 CZTS / CZTSe Thin-Film Cells
- **Composition:** Cu₂ZnSn(S,Se)₄ — kesterite structure.
- **Why it works:** Direct bandgap tunable between 1.0–1.5 eV via S/Se ratio; contains only earth-abundant, non-toxic elements.
- **Key challenges:** **Secondary phase formation** (ZnS, Cu₂SnS₃), **open-circuit voltage deficit** ($V_{OC,def} = E_g/q - V_{OC}$), low carrier lifetimes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PCE = \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | % |
| 2 | $FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}$ | Fill factor (0 < FF < 1) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $J_{ph} = q \cdot G \cdot (L_n + L_p)$ | Photocurrent from generation rate $G$ | A/m² |
| 4 | $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$ | Quantum confinement in a QD of radius $R$ | eV |
| 5 | $\alpha(h\nu) = A \left(h\nu - E_g\right)^{n}$ | Tauc relation; $n = 1/2$ direct, $2$ indirect | cm⁻¹ |
| 6 | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$ | Carrier diffusion length | m |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{J_{SC}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | $V_{OC}$ from diode equation (ideality $n$) | V |
| 8 | $J = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] - J_{SC}$ | Single-diode model (illuminated) | A/m² |
| 9 | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential at a p–n junction | V |
| 10 | $E_{exciton} = E_g - E_b$ | Exciton energy = bandgap − binding energy | eV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to keep on your formula sheet (highlighted per protocol):** Planck's constant $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, elementary charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, Boltzmann $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, speed of light $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s, vacuum permittivity $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BIPV (Building-Integrated PV):** Semi-transparent perovskite and OPV modules can be laminated onto glass façades, generating power while serving as tunable shading.
- **Space applications:** III–V multijunctions and emerging perovskite/silicon **tandems** offer >30% efficiency with reduced mass.
- **Indoor IoT sensors:** Perovskite and OPV cells are excellent under artificial LED lighting (low-intensity, narrow spectrum), making them ideal for self-powered smart-home nodes.
- **Agrivoltaics:** Semi-transparent OPVs allow specific wavelength transmission for photosynthesis while generating electricity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantum Confinement Energy in a Quantum Dot

We model a quantum dot as a spherical potential well of infinite depth and radius $R$. Solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation in spherical coordinates, the wavefunction must vanish at $r = R$. The boundary condition restricts the allowed radial quantum numbers to discrete values, and the **lowest energy level** (1s) is obtained by solving the transcendental equation:

$$
\tan(kR) = \frac{kR}{\sqrt{(kR)^2 - \pi^2}}
$$

For a simple pedagogical estimate, the ground-state confinement energy of an electron (or hole) in a 1D particle-in-a-box analog can be approximated. For a 3D spherical box with effective mass $m^*$:

$$
E_{conf} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m^* R^2}
$$

For an **electron–hole pair (exciton)** with reduced effective mass $\mu = \dfrac{m_e^* m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*}$, the combined confinement energy is:

$$
E_{conf}^{exciton} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}
$$

The total effective bandgap of the nanocrystal is therefore:

$$
E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2} - E_{Coulomb}
$$

where $E_{Coulomb} = \dfrac{1.8 e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 R}$ is the small Coulomb attraction correction.

#### Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU board-style)

**Problem:** Calculate the size-dependent bandgap shift for a PbS quantum dot of radius $R = 3$ nm. Given $E_g^{bulk} = 0.41$ eV, $m_e^* = 0.08\, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.075\, m_0$, and neglect $E_{Coulomb}$ for simplicity.

**Step 1 — Compute the reduced effective mass:**

$$
\mu = \frac{m_e^* m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*} = \frac{(0.08)(0.075)}{0.08 + 0.075}\, m_0 = \frac{0.00600}{0.155}\, m_0 = 0.0387\, m_0
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $\mu$ to SI units:**

$$
\mu = 0.0387 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} = 3.525 \times 10^{-32} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the confinement energy formula:**

$$
E_{conf} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2} = \frac{(1.055 \times 10^{-34})^2 \cdot \pi^2}{2 \times 3.525 \times 10^{-32} \times (3 \times 10^{-9})^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{(1.113 \times 10^{-68}) \cdot 9.8696}{2 \times 3.525 \times 10^{-32} \times 9 \times 10^{-18}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.0988 \times 10^{-67}}{6.345 \times 10^{-49}} = 1.732 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to eV:**

$$
E_{conf} = \frac{1.732 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.081 \text{ eV}
$$

**Step 5 — Add bulk bandgap to obtain nano bandgap:**

$$
E_g^{nano} = 0.41 + 1.081 = 1.491 \text{ eV}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 3 nm PbS quantum dot has an effective bandgap of **~1.49 eV**, dramatically blue-shifted from the bulk 0.41 eV, making it suitable for visible-light harvesting despite the parent material being a narrow-gap infrared semiconductor.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Conversion Efficiency from the Diode Equation

The illuminated current–voltage characteristic of a solar cell in the **single-diode model** is:

$$
J(V) = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] - J_{SC}
$$

**Step A — Open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$):** At $V = V_{OC}$, $J = 0$:

$$
0 = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] - J_{SC}
$$

$$
\implies J_{SC} = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

Since $J_{SC} \gg J_0$, the "$-1$" is negligible, giving:

$$
V_{OC} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{SC}}{J_0} + 1\right) \approx \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{SC}}{J_0}\right)
$$

**Step B — Maximum power point ($P_{max}$):** Set $\frac{d(JV)}{dV} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d}{dV}\left\{ V \cdot J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] - V J_{SC} \right\} = 0
$$

Solving with substitution $u = \frac{qV_{mp}}{n k_B T}$ yields the transcendental equation $u + 1 = \exp(u)$ approximately, leading to $u \approx 0.5$ for typical cells.

**Step C — Fill factor and efficiency:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{OC} J_{SC}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \eta = \frac{V_{OC}\, J_{SC}\, FF}{P_{in}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Material Classes

| Parameter | Perovskite (MAPbI₃) | OPV (PM6:Y6) | DSSC (N719) | CZTS | c-Si (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E_g$ (eV) | 1.55 | 1.3–1.6 | 1.6–1.8 (dye) | 1.0–1.5 | 1.12 |
| $V_{OC}$ (V) | 1.0–1.2 | 0.8–0.9 | 0.7–0.8 | 0.4–0.7 | 0.7 |
| $J_{SC}$ (mA/cm²) | 22–25 | 18–22 | 15–18 | 18–22 | 38–42 |
| Best PCE (%) | ~26 | ~19 | ~14 | ~14 | ~26.8 |
| Stability | Months | Hours–Years | Years | Years | 25+ years |
| Toxicity | High (Pb) | Low | Low (Ru) | Low | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Energy-Band Architecture of an Emerging PV Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight Photon h nu] --> B[Transparent Electrode FTO or ITO]
    B --> C[Electron Transport Layer ETL - TiO2 or SnO2]
    C --> D[Active Absorber Layer - Perovskite OPV DSSC QDs CZTS]
    D --> E[Hole Transport Layer HTL - Spiro OMeTAD or PEDOT PSS]
    E --> F[Metal Back Contact - Au Ag Al]
    D --> G[Charge Generation - Exciton Formation]
    G --> H[Charge Separation at Donor Acceptor Interface]
    H --> I[Free Electrons travel via ETL to Cathode]
    H --> J[Free Holes travel via HTL to Anode]
    I --> K[External Circuit Load]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Electrical Power Output]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Perovskite Solar Cell (n-i-p Planar)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    substrate[Glass Substrate] --> fto[FTO Front Contact]
    fto --> etl[ETL - SnO2 Compact Layer]
    etl --> perovskite[Perovskite Absorber - FAPbI3 or Mixed Cation]
    perovskite --> htl[HTL - Spiro OMeTAD Doped]
    htl --> metal[Au or Ag Metal Contact]
    photon1[Photon h nu greater than Eg] -.-> perovskite
    photon2[Photon h nu less than Eg] -. Transmitted .-> glass
    perovskite --> exciton[Exciton Generation]
    exciton --> separation[Charge Separation at Grain Boundaries]
    separation --> electron[Electron to ETL to FTO]
    separation --> hole[Hole to HTL to Au]
    electron --> output1[DC Power Output]
    hole --> output1
```

### 4.3 DSSC Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sun[Photon h nu] --> dye[Excited Dye D Star]
    dye --> inject[Electron Injection into TiO2 CB]
    inject --> tio2[Mesoporous TiO2 Network]
    tio2 --> fto2[FTO Substrate]
    fto2 --> circuit[External Load]
    circuit --> pt[Pt Counter Electrode]
    pt --> redox[Electrolyte I minus to I3 minus]
    redox --> regen[Regenerate Ground State Dye D]
    regen --> dye
```

### 4.4 Quantum Dot Size-Bandgap Tuning Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bulk[Bulk Semiconductor - Eg fixed] --> nano[Nanocrystal Synthesis]
    nano --> r1[Small R less than 2 nm - Strong Confinement - High Eg]
    nano --> r2[Medium R 3 to 5 nm - Moderate Confinement]
    nano --> r3[Large R greater than 8 nm - Weak Confinement - Eg approaches Bulk]
    r1 --> app1[UV Blue Absorption - Displays]
    r2 --> app2[Visible Absorption - Solar Cells]
    r3 --> app3[NIR Absorption - Photodetectors]
```

### 4.5 Tandem Cell Stack (Perovskite on Silicon)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    top[Top Cell - Wide Eg Perovskite 1.8 eV Br rich] --> rec1[Recombination Layer ITO or MoOx]
    rec1 --> bot[Bottom Cell - Narrow Eg c Si 1.1 eV]
    sun2[Sunlight h nu] --> top
    top --> blue1[High Energy Photons Absorbed]
    top --> red1[Low Energy Photons Transmitted]
    red1 --> bot
    bot --> red2[Low Energy Photons Absorbed in Si]
    blue1 --> out1[Voltage from Perovskite]
    red2 --> out2[Voltage from Silicon]
    out1 --> total[Series Connected Voc Sum]
    out2 --> total
    total --> final[PCE greater than 30 percent Theoretical]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** Differentiate between a bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) organic solar cell and a perovskite solar cell with respect to (i) the active layer morphology, (ii) the charge generation mechanism, and (iii) the dominant recombination loss.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Morphology (1 mark):** In a BHJ-OPV, the active layer is a **nanoscale phase-separated blend** of donor and acceptor materials forming an interpenetrating network. In a perovskite solar cell, the active layer is a **single-phase crystalline or polycrystalline ABX₃ film** with uniform composition throughout.
- **(ii) Charge generation (1 mark):** In OPV, absorbed photons form **strongly bound excitons** (binding energy 0.3–1.0 eV) that require a donor–acceptor interface for dissociation. In perovskites, free carriers are generated **directly upon photon absorption** (exciton binding energy < 25 meV, comparable to thermal energy at room temperature).
- **(iii) Recombination loss (1 mark):** In OPV, **geminate recombination** (recombination of the originally bound e–h pair before separation) dominates due to the high exciton binding energy. In perovskites, **non-geminate (bimolecular) recombination** and **ion-migration-induced hysteresis** dominate, with trap-assisted Shockley–Read–Hall recombination at grain boundaries being significant.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** What is "quantum confinement" in a semiconductor nanocrystal? How does it change the effective bandgap of the material as the particle size decreases?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Quantum confinement is the spatial restriction of electron and hole wavefunctions inside a semiconductor nanocrystal (quantum dot) of size comparable to or smaller than the **Bohr exciton radius** of the material. The carriers behave as "particles in a box," and their allowed energy levels become discrete rather than continuous.
- **Effect on bandgap (1.5 marks):** As particle radius $R$ decreases, the confinement energy $\frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$ increases, **widening the effective bandgap** and causing a **blue shift** in the absorption spectrum. The relation is approximately $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$, where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]** 

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the working principle of a **perovskite solar cell**. Discuss the role of (i) the electron transport layer (ETL) and (ii) the hole transport layer (HTL). Mention the typical composition of the active perovskite absorber.

**(b) (7 marks)** Describe the phenomenon of **quantum confinement** in semiconductor quantum dots. Derive an expression for the size-dependent bandgap of a spherical nanocrystal and explain how the bandgap energy changes with particle size. Give two examples of applications of quantum dot solar cells.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Perovskite Solar Cell (7 marks):**

- **Active absorber composition (1 mark):** The active layer is a hybrid organic–inorganic perovskite with general formula **ABX₃**, typically **methylammonium lead iodide (CH₃NH₃PbI₃ = MAPbI₃)** or formamidinium lead iodide (FAPbI₃), or mixed-cation/halide compositions such as (FA,MA,Cs)Pb(I,Br)₃.
- **Working principle with energy-band diagram (4 marks):** When a photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strikes the perovskite absorber, it generates a free electron in the CB and a hole in the VB. The built-in electric field at the **ETL/perovskite** and **perovskite/HTL** heterojunctions drives electron diffusion toward the ETL and hole diffusion toward the HTL.
- **Role of ETL (1 mark):** The ETL (e.g., **TiO₂, SnO₂, or C₆₀-SAM**) selectively extracts electrons and blocks holes, providing an electron-selective contact.
- **Role of HTL (1 mark):** The HTL (e.g., **Spiro-OMeTAD, P3HT, or PTAA**) selectively extracts holes and blocks electrons, completing the circuit.
- **Key energy levels for MAPbI₃:** CB ≈ −3.9 eV, VB ≈ −5.4 eV (vs vacuum), $E_g \approx 1.55$ eV.

**(b) Quantum Confinement (7 marks):**

- **Description (2 marks):** When the radius $R$ of a semiconductor nanocrystal is comparable to or smaller than the **Bohr exciton radius** $a_B = \dfrac{4 \pi \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \hbar^2}{\mu e^2}$, the electron and hole wavefunctions are spatially confined. This restricts the allowed energy levels to discrete values (similar to a "particle in a box") and raises the kinetic energy of both carriers.
- **Derivation (3 marks):** Modeling the dot as a 3D spherical potential well with infinite barriers, the Schrödinger equation yields discrete energy levels. The ground-state confinement energy for a single carrier of effective mass $m^*$ in a 1D analog is $E = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m^* L^2}$, where $L$ is the confinement dimension. For a 3D sphere of radius $R$, the confinement energy for an **exciton** (electron + hole with reduced mass $\mu$) is:

$$
E_{conf} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}
$$

The size-dependent bandgap is therefore:

$$
E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2} - E_{Coulomb}
$$

- **Effect of size (1 mark):** As $R$ decreases, the confinement term $\frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$ **increases rapidly** ($1/R^2$ dependence), so the effective bandgap **widens** and the absorption spectrum **blue-shifts**.
- **Applications (1 mark):** (i) **Quantum-dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs)** where CdSe or PbS QDs replace molecular dyes in a DSSC architecture, and (ii) **perovskite quantum dot LEDs and solar cells** with size-tunable emission/absorption for displays and tandem PVs.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the construction and working of a **Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC)** with a neat sketch. Discuss the function of each component: TiO₂ photoanode, dye sensitizer, redox electrolyte, and Pt counter electrode. State the main advantage and the principal limitation of DSSCs.

**(b) (7 marks)** What are the **bandgap engineering strategies** in emerging photovoltaic materials? Explain with reference to (i) alloying in perovskite (mixed halide/cation), (ii) size tuning in quantum dots, and (iii) tandem cell stacking of perovskite on silicon. Sketch a typical J-V curve and label $V_{OC}$, $J_{SC}$, $FF$, and $P_{max}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DSSC (7 marks):**

- **Construction (3 marks):** A DSSC consists of:
  1. **Transparent FTO glass** as the front substrate.
  2. **Mesoporous nanocrystalline TiO₂ film** (~10 μm thick) deposited on the FTO, forming the photoanode.
  3. **Monolayer of molecular dye** (e.g., N719, cis-diisothiocyanato-bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium(II)) adsorbed onto the TiO₂ surface.
  4. **Liquid electrolyte** containing the I⁻/I₃⁻ redox couple (e.g., in acetonitrile).
  5. **Pt-coated FTO** as the counter electrode.
  6. The cell is sealed to prevent electrolyte leakage.
- **Working (3 marks):** A photon is absorbed by the dye ($D \to D^*$). The excited dye injects an electron into the TiO₂ CB: $D^* \to D^+ + e^- (TiO_2)$. Electrons travel through the TiO₂ network to the FTO, through the external circuit, to the Pt counter electrode, where they reduce I₃⁻ to I⁻. The I⁻ ions diffuse back and regenerate the dye: $3 I^- + 2 D^+ \to I_3^- + 2 D$, completing the cycle.
- **Advantage (0.5 mark):** Works under **diffuse/low light**; cheap and color-tunable.
- **Limitation (0.5 mark):** **Liquid electrolyte instability** (leakage, evaporation, sealing issues) and **long-term degradation** of the dye.

**(b) Bandgap Engineering (7 marks):**

- **(i) Alloying in perovskite (2 marks):** The bandgap of ABX₃ perovskites can be tuned continuously by **(a) halide substitution** (I → Br → Cl increases $E_g$ from ~1.5 to ~3.0 eV following Vegard's law) and **(b) cation substitution** (MA → FA → Cs gradually modifies tolerance factor and $E_g$). For example, MAPbI₃ has $E_g = 1.55$ eV; MAPbBr₃ has $E_g = 2.3$ eV; FAPbI₃ has $E_g \approx 1.45$ eV.
- **(ii) Size tuning in quantum dots (2 marks):** As shown in derivation, $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$. Reducing $R$ from 10 nm to 2 nm can shift $E_g$ by 1–2 eV, enabling spectral tailoring for any region of the solar spectrum.
- **(iii) Tandem cell stacking (2 marks):** A **monolithic 2-terminal tandem** stacks a wide-bandgap top cell (e.g., 1.8 eV perovskite) above a narrow-bandgap bottom cell (e.g., 1.1 eV c-Si). High-energy photons are absorbed in the top cell; low-energy photons transmitted to the bottom cell. The total $V_{OC}$ is the sum of the two sub-cells' $V_{OC}$, while the current is matched between them. Theoretical efficiencies exceed 30%.
- **J–V curve sketch (1 mark):** (sketch a J–V curve in fourth quadrant with the rectangular $V_{OC} \times J_{SC}$ bounding box and the inscribed $V_{mp} \times J_{mp}$ rectangle at $P_{max}$, $FF = \frac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{OC} J_{SC}}$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing BHJ with bilayer OPV:** A BHJ has a *nanoscale interpenetrating* donor–acceptor network; a bilayer has a *flat planar* junction. Examiners deduct marks for using the two terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units on the confinement formula:** Always carry $\mu$ in kg, $R$ in meters, and convert the final energy to eV. A common error is reporting $E_{conf}$ in J without conversion.
> 3. **Skipping the Coulomb correction:** Even when problem statements say "approximate," a brief mention of $E_{Coulomb} \propto 1/R$ earns a follow-up mark.
> 4. **Mixing exciton and free-carrier language:** In OPV, photons create **excitons**; in perovskites, they create **free carriers**. Confusing these costs a mark in part (a) of BHJ/PSC comparison questions.
> 5. **No sketch for DSSC/perovskite:** KTU examiners explicitly mark "neat sketch" allocations (often 1–2 marks). Always include a labeled cross-section in long answers.
> 6. **Hysteresis not mentioned for PSCs:** Any KTU answer on perovskites that omits **J-V hysteresis due to ion migration** is considered incomplete for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five emerging PV classes to memorize (with formula 1):** Perovskite (ABX₃, $E_g \approx 1.55$ eV), OPV (donor–acceptor BHJ), DSSC (dye + TiO₂ + I⁻/I₃⁻ electrolyte), Quantum Dot (size-tuned $E_g^{nano}$), CZTS (kesterite, earth-abundant).
- **Universal 4-step conversion process:** Absorption → Exciton generation → Exciton dissociation → Charge transport & collection.
- **Key efficiency formula (formula 1 + 2):** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ and $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{OC} J_{SC}}$.
- **Quantum confinement (formula 4):** $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$ — the $1/R^2$ scaling is the **single most important result** to remember.
- **Tauc relation (formula 5):** $\alpha h\nu = A (h\nu - E_g)^n$ with $n = 1/2$ for direct, $2$ for indirect transitions.
- **Single-diode model (formula 8):** $J = J_0 \left[ \exp(qV/nk_BT) - 1 \right] - J_{SC}$ — yields $V_{OC} \approx (nk_BT/q) \ln(J_{SC}/J_0)$.
- **Stability hierarchy (memory aid):** c-Si ≈ CZTS > DSSC > Perovskite > OPV (rough order).
- **Toxicity flag:** Only perovskite (Pb) and some QDs (Cd, Pb) raise toxicity concerns; OPV, DSSC, and CZTS are considered green chemistries.
- **Tandem advantage:** Stacking wide-$E_g$ (top) and narrow-$E_g$ (bottom) cells exceeds the single-junction **Shockley–Queisser limit** of ~33%.
- **BHJ concept:** Nanoscale phase separation in OPV provides the donor–acceptor interface everywhere excitons diffuse, enabling efficient dissociation.
- **Color/structural identifiers:** Perovskite = yellow/brown-black (MAPbI₃); OPV = green/blue film; DSSC = often purple/red from the dye; CZTS = grey-black kesterite.
- **Numerics to keep on the cheat sheet:** $h\nu$ at 700 nm = 1.77 eV; at 500 nm = 2.48 eV; at 1100 nm = 1.13 eV (silicon absorption edge).
- **KTU keyword trigger phrases:** *"Discuss the working principle with energy-band diagram"* → always include a band diagram and label ETL/HTL. *"Explain the size-dependent properties"* → always derive formula 4. *"Differentiate"* → always use a 3-column comparison table. *"Sketch the J-V curve"* → always label $V_{OC}$, $J_{SC}$, $V_{mp}$, $J_{mp}$, and the $FF$ rectangle.
